Confirmation of this HI detection would be desirable as it is very weak and found at the edge of our survey field. The Galactic extinction in this direction is A_ B 2 mag (Schlegel et al. 1998). Both HIZOA J1940+11 and HIZOA J1914+10 appear isolated, in contrast to HIZOA J0630+08, and lie in the outskirts of the Local Void.

gmw_00010536.name: PGC 38025
gmw_00010536.overview: PGC 38025 is a star-forming lenticular field galaxy that hosts an off-nuclear blue core [arXiv:2104.10909]. This core is an H II region, and the galaxy's ionized and cold molecular gas rotate misaligned with its stellar component [arXiv:2104.10909].

sources.summary: The paper studies PGC 38025 as a star-forming field S0 with an off-nuclear blue core, misaligned gas kinematics, and star formation likely triggered by a gas-rich minor merger.

quotes.quote: In this work, we study one special case, SDSS J120237.07+642235.3 (PGC 38025), a star-forming field S0 galaxy with an off-nuclear blue core. We present optical integral field spectroscopic (IFS) observation with the 3.5 meter telescope at Calar Alto (CAHA) Observatory, and high-resolution millimeter observation with the NOrthern Extended Millimeter Array (NOEMA). We estimated the star formation rate (SFR = 0.446 M_ yr^ 1 ) and gaseous metallicity (12 + log(O/H) = 8.42) for PGC 38025, which follows the star formation main sequence and stellar mass - metallicity relation.

We found that the ionized gas and cold molecular gas in PGC 38025 show the same spatial distribution and kinematics, whilst rotating misaligned with stellar component. The off-nuclear blue core is locating at the same redshift as PGC 38025 and its optical spectrum suggest it is H ii region. We suggest that the star formation in PGC 38025 is triggered by a gas-rich minor merger, and the off-nuclear blue core might be a local star-formation happened during the accretion/merger process.

gmw_00010537.name: ID83492
gmw_00010537.overview: ID83492 is a high-redshift post-starburst galaxy located at a redshift greater than one [arXiv:2306.08120]. It has two close, gas-rich starbursting companions, suggesting that the system may be in the early stages of a major merger [arXiv:2306.08120].

sources.summary: The paper identifies ID83492 as a z > 1 post-starburst galaxy with detected CO, substantial molecular gas, and two close gas-rich starbursting companions suggesting an early major merger.

quotes.quote: In this study we focus on two, post-starburst galaxies at z > 1 (ID83492 and ID97148). We aim, by detecting their CO emission, to estimate their molecular gas content and constrain possible physical mechanisms responsible for galaxy quenching. In addition, we include samples from the literature (see Section subsec:literature ).

In particular, we serendipitously found two CO(3-2) emitters located respectively at 5 .8 and 13 .7 away from ID83492. Their redshift, estimated from the CO emission, is z < 0.0018 ( v_ off < 250 km s ^ 1 ) different from that of our post-SB target. This suggests that ID83492 might be in an early (multiple) merger stage. Given the stellar masses of the companions, this is a major merger (mass ratios 1:1 and 1:1.5).

ID83492 and its two companions might be part of a larger galaxy group or be the parents of a very massive galaxy to be formed by z 0 through the merger of these sources. In the current HST and ALMA data we do not detect tidal features or morphological asymmetries indicating an ongoing merger, although deeper and higher resolution observations might be needed to detect those, especially if the merger is in its early phase.

gmw_00010538.name: ID97148
gmw_00010538.overview: ID97148 is a post-starburst galaxy located at a redshift of z > 1 [arXiv:2306.08120]. Its carbon monoxide emission is spatially offset from the optical peak, and elongation in the near-infrared imaging suggests the galaxy may be undergoing an interaction [arXiv:2306.08120].

sources.summary: The paper identifies ID97148 as a z > 1 post-starburst galaxy with detected CO offset from the optical peak, possible unresolved interaction, and molecular gas retained despite little star formation.

quotes.quote: In this study we focus on two, post-starburst galaxies at z > 1 (ID83492 and ID97148). We aim, by detecting their CO emission, to estimate their molecular gas content and constrain possible physical mechanisms responsible for galaxy quenching. In addition, we include samples from the literature (see Section subsec:literature ).

The spatial position of CO emission of ID97148 instead is offset by 1.6 with respect to the optical peak emission in the astrometrically-calibrated H band from VLT/VIRCAM We calibrated the astrometry of the VIRCAM data against Gaia DR3 by using 30 non-saturated stars in the field of view. Uncertainties on the calibration (estimated as the standard deviation of the (RA) and (DEC) offsets) are _ RA 0.08 and _ DEC 0.06 . . Despite this offset is within the FWHM of the beam ( 1.6 1.3 ) we notice that the H band photometry show elongation in the direction of the CO detection which could be tracing an ongoing interaction (similarly to the EGS-18045 from Belli2021 ). Alternatively the CO emission might originate from a nearby dust-obscured satellite which is not detected in the near-infrared imaging (e.g., Schreiber2018 ). In this case the molecular gas mass that we estimate for ID97148 should be considered as an upper limit.

However, the fact that the redshift of the \ emission matches the one estimated from the optical spectrum (velocity difference v_ off 47 km s^ 1 , consistent with null velocity offset within the uncertainties) rules out the possibility of a spurious detection If we fit the CO map at the optical position of the galaxy, we retrieve a 3 CO flux upper limit f_ CO < 235 m , and a corresponding molecular gas mass M_ H2 < 3.2 10^9 M_ . . We also highlight that next to the CO(3-2) detected line there is a negative noise peak which is evident in the continuum map (Figure fig:maps ) and that could hide part of the emission and artificially offset its peak. To account for the effects of the negative noise peak on the line emission flux, we fitted the 1D spectrum (Figure fig:maps ) with a Gaussian model, allowing for a negative continuum, and estimated the integrated flux of the emission. We compared it with the integrated flux obtained when forcing the Gaussian to have zero continuum. We find that the flux doubles when we account for the negative continuum. Also the velocity width of the line increases by 40 . This translates into a molecular gas mass and molecular gas fraction that are 3 times larger (Section subsec:gas_mass ). In the following we report both molecular gas estimates. We estimated the redshift from the \ lines in two ways, both giving consistent results ( z< 0.0004 ): by computing the signal-weighted average frequency within the line channels and by fitting the one-dimensional spectrum with a Gaussian function. We compared these redshift estimates with those obtained from optical spectra Maltby2016 and found that they agree within 1 , increasing the reliability of the CO detection (Table tab:observations ). We finally estimated their \ flux by fitting their average emission line maps in the uv plane adopting the Fourier Transform of a two-dimensional PSF model with the GILDAS task uv fit . Fluxes and uncertainties determined with the uvmodelfit task provided by CASA give consistent results. The obtained CO(3-2) total fluxes are reported in Table tab:observations . We also serendipitously detected bright emission lines and continuum from two additional galaxies in the surroundings of ID83492 (Figure fig:companions ). The lines are detected at 14 and 16.5 and the continuum at 3.5 and 4.7 respectively (Table tab:observations ). They both have optical counterparts in Hubble Space Telescope Wide Field Camera 3 ( HST /WFC3) imaging from the 3D-HST program ( Skelton2014 , Figure fig:maps ). Their photometric redshift is z 1.1 , implying that the detected line likely is CO(3-2) at z = 1.1375 0.0002 and z = 1.1379 0.0001 , consistent with the spectroscopic redshift of ID83492. Given their velocity difference ( v_ off 250 km s ^ 1 and 195 km s ^ 1 ) and projected distance from ID83492 ( 5.8 48 kpc and 13.7 112 kpc ), they might be companions that are merging with our target galaxy. In the literature, different criteria about the velocity difference and physical proximity are adopted when defining mergers. Galaxies with spectroscopic velocity differences v_ off 500 km s ^ 1 and projected distances d _ proj 50 kpc are typically considered as close pairs (e.g., Lin2008, Patton2008, Lotz2011, Mantha2018 ) and simulations predict that they merge within less than 1 Gyr (e.g., Conselice2006 ). Galaxies with larger separation (up to 150 kpc) are still considered pairs deRavel2009, Lotz2011 and are expected to merge on timescales of 1.5 - 2.5 Gyr, depending on their baryonic mass ratio and orbital parameters Lotz2008, Lotz2010a, Lotz2010b . Based on these results, ID83492 might merge with two companion galaxies within < 2.5 Gyr. We report their observed and measured properties respectively in Table tab:observations and Table tab:measurements . We show their CO(3-2) spectra and spectral energy distributions in Figure fig:companions . Continuum emission We created averaged continuum maps by integrating the spectral range, after excluding the channels where the flux is dominated by the CO emission line. None of our main targets is detected. In Table tab:observations , we report the total flux density upper limits that we estimated as the 5 uncertainty obtained when fitting, in the uv plane, the Fourier Transform of a two-dimensional PSF model with center fixed at the position of the CO detection. We detected the continuum emission of the two serendipitous companion galaxies of ID83492 at 3.5 and 4.7 significance. We also serendipitously detected at 4.8 the continuum of an additional source nearby ID97148 (Figure fig:maps ). However this source is not detected in CO(3-2) and it does not have a counterpart in current catalogs including ground-based optical observations Tanaka2015 . A possible counterpart is found in the catalog by , but it has a distance of 0 .5 from ID97148 and a photometric redshift z 1.1 . The SED fit also returns a very low stellar mass and SFR (log(M _ / M _ ) = 7.7, log(SFR/(M _ yr ^ 1 )) = 0.33). Given the angular distance from ID97148, the redshift difference of z > 0.15 and the highly uncertain values retrieved from the SED fit, we do not consider such detection as a companion galaxy, but rather as a spurious detection or lower-redshift and low-mass interloper. Molecular gas mass We estimated the molecular gas mass of our galaxies from their CO luminosity. We considered a brightness temperature ratio r_ 32 = 0.5 Carilli2013 to account for the excitation of the CO(3-2) transition with respect to the thermalized case. We then converted the estimated CO(1-0) luminosity into molecular gas mass by adopting the standard Milky-Way like conversion factor _ CO = 4.4 M _ K km s ^ 1 pc ^2 Bolatto2013 . This is consistent with most literature studies used to compare with our results (Section subsec:literature ). We corrected the molecular gas mass from literature samples that adopt a different _ CO conversion factor to make them consistent with our assumptions. For our sample galaxies, we obtain molecular gas masses M _ H2 = (4.1 1.1) 10^9 M _ for ID97148 and M _ H2 = (9.9 1.9) 10^9 M _ for ID83492 (Table tab:measurements ). These uncertainties do not account for systematic differences in the (unconstrained) excitation and CO-to-H _2 factors in post-SB galaxies, but allow to directly compare our results with the literature on star-forming and quiescent galaxies. We also estimated an upper limit on the gas mass of our galaxies by using the ALMA continuum which is probing the Rayleigh-Jeans tail of the far-infrared dust emission. The continuum at 850 m in fact probes a wavelength regime where the dust emission is optically thin, providing direct information on the dust (and thus gas) content Magdis2012, Scoville2013, Scoville2015 . By considering the continuum flux upper limits estimated as in Section subsec:continuum (Table tab:observations ), a spectral index = 1.8 Magdis2021, Cochrane2022 , and a specific luminosity at 850 GHz rest-frame (L _ _ 850 ) to gas mass conversion factor _ 850 = L_ _ 850 /M_ gas = 1 10^ 20 erg s^ 1 Hz ^ 1 M _ ^ 1 Scoville2015 , we obtained M _ gas 1.3 10^ 10 M _ for both galaxies at 5 . We also stacked the continuum maps of our target galaxies at the position of the CO detections, reaching a 5 upper limit on the flux F _ cont 9.1 Jy. This implies an upper limit on the average gas mass M _ gas 9.2 10^9 M _ . These upper limits are consistent with the molecular gas estimates obtained from CO within the uncertainties related not only to the flux measurements, but also to the conversion factors (e.g., r_ 32 , _ CO , _ 850 ). Stellar mass and star formation rate We modelled the multi-wavelength photometric catalog in the SXDS field described in . The catalog comprises u band data obtained with CFHT/Megacam, BVRiz with Subaru/Suprime-Cam, JHK from the UKIRT Infrared Deep Sky Survey (UKIDSS, Lawrence2007 ), and Spitzer observations from the UKIDSS Ultra Deep Survey (SpUDS; PI: J. Dunlop). We fit the SEDs with Fast++ https://github.com/cschreib/fastpp Schreiber2018 using Bruzual2003 models, the IMF, delayed SFHs ( SFR (t) te^ t/ with log( /yr^ 1 ) [ 6.5,10 ] in log-steps of 0.1), ages in log-steps of 0.1 from a minimum of 100 Myr and a maximum set by the age of the Universe, the Calzetti2000 dust attenuation curve ( A_ V [ 0,6 ] mag), and free metallicities including sub- and super-solar estimates ( Z=0.008, 0.02, 0.05 ). We fixed the redshifts to the spectroscopic values and modelled the photometry and the spectra simultaneously. In the case of ID83492, the inclusion of the rest-frame optical spectrum does not appreciably affect the best-fit parameters obtained by modelling the photometry only (Figure fig:sed ). On the contrary, the spectrum of ID97148 drops more steeply than the photometry at short wavelengths. We ascribed this difference to an imperfect flux calibration of the spectrum and compensated for it by boosting its uncertainties by a factor of 3 . This more conservative value does not hamper our ability to reproduce the main absorption features in the spectrum and allows us to simultaneously retrieve the overall SED shape at longer wavelengths (Figure fig:sed ). We note that the photometry adopted here is consistent with an independent extraction by , which supports its validity and constraining power. In the case of , the photometry is also consistent with that from 3D-HST Skelton2014 . The best-fit parameters and their uncertainties are reported in Table tab:measurements . The latter were estimated both analytically following and via Monte Carlo bootstrapping (see Schreiber2018 for details about the exact implementation in Fast++ ). We also checked for consistency with SED fitting results reported in the literature Carnall2019, Wild2020 . Only one of our targets (ID83492) is included in those samples and its stellar mass is reported. Both and fit the SED with the code BAGPIPES, with spectral synthesis models, assuming a IMF. While parametrizes the SFH with a double-power-law model, also allows for a secondary burst of star formation (exponentially declining) not to force all the stellar mass production to happen in a single burst. The stellar mass estimated for ID83492 by is log(M_ /M_ ) = 11.19 0.2 when fitting the photometric data only and log(M_ /M_ ) = 11.08 0.05 when fitting photometric and spectroscopic data, whereas reports log(M_ /M_ ) = 11.29 0.2 . Our stellar mass estimate is also consistent with that reported by 3D-HST ( Skelton2014 , log(M_ /M_ ) =10.86 estimated using the code Fast++ ) and that reported by . Despite the different assumptions, SFH used, and codes adopted for the SED fit, all these estimates are consistent within them and with ours, within the uncertainties. Both our target galaxies have detected [ OII ] emission in the optical spectra (Section subsec:sample ). We estimate the dust-corrected [ OII ] luminosity by using the extinction estimated from the best-fit SED and accounting for the fact that emission lines are more attenuated than the stellar continuum ( E(B-V)_ star = 0.58 E(B-V)_ nebular , Calzetti2000 ). We convert the dust-corrected [ OII ] luminosity into SFR by using the calibration by . We find SFR = 0.2 0.1 M _ yr ^ 1 for ID97148 and SFR = 0.3 0.1 M _ yr ^ 1 for ID83492, which are consistent, within the uncertainties, with those estimated from the SED fit. In the following we adopt SFR from the SED fit for consistency with the estimates obtained in the literature. Stellar age Stellar absorption features in the optical spectra, such as the Balmer lines, black together with the 4000 break (D _n 4000) are powerful tools to constrain the mean stellar age and the recent star formation history of galaxies Worthey1994, Kauffmann2003, Gallazzi2005 . From the optical spectra of our galaxies we measure the Lick indices of the high-order Balmer lines H _ F , H F Note that we choose the H _ F and H _ F definitions instead of the wider H _ A , H _ A used in Gallazzi2014 because the latter cannot be measured for ID97148. , and the D _n 4000 index following . For ID97148 we obtain H _ F = 7.1 0.6 , H _ F = 7.0 0.6 , and D _n4000 = 1.166 0.010 , whereas for ID83492 we estimate H _ F = 5.1 0.2 , H _ F = 2.9 4.6 , and D _n4000 = 1.407 0.007 . We add a 10 error to the error budget of D _n4000 to account for uncertainties in spectrophotometric calibration (Figure fig:hdf ). We estimated the mean age of the stellar populations of our sample galaxies by adopting the Bayesian approach described in . Specifically, we compare the observed D _n4000 , H _ F , H _ F absorption index strengths with those predicted by a large Monte Carlo library of model spectra. We adopted the model library described in , which comprises 500000 synthetic spectra assuming randomly generated star formation histories allowing both rising and declining phases, with the addition of random bursts Sandage1986 and metal enrichment histories see for more details Zibetti2017, Zibetti2022 . The base models are Simple Stellar Populations in the 2016 version Chevallard2016 . Considering only the models with a formation epoch compatible with the age of the Universe at the redshift of our sample galaxies, we construct the posterior probability function (PDF) of the R band light weighted mean stellar age and of the mass-weighted mean stellar age. We take the median of the PDF as our fiducial value and the 16th and 84th percentile range as the uncertainties accounting for model degeneracies. We estimate the uncertainties related to using different sets of indices to measure ages of high-redshift post-SBs to be 0.02 dex (see Appendix app:literature_data , point (viii) High-redshift post-SBs ). As a result, for ID97148 we estimate an R band light-weighted mean age of log(age/yr) = 8.6 0.1 and for ID83492 log(age/yr) = 8.9 0.1 . The estimated mass-weighted ages are very similar, only 0.03 dex older, but with larger uncertainties of 0.2 dex. The model library adopted here differs from the one adopted in , which assumed exponential SFHs with random bursts and fixed metallicity along each SFH and which is based on the original BC03 SSP models. The new library results in a better coverage of the index-index diagnostic diagrams. As a reference, we note that adopting the library we would estimate ages older by 0.2 dex and 0.1 dex for ID97148 and ID83492, respectively. These results are consistent with the ages obtained from SED fitting, within the uncertainties: log(age/yr) = 8.4^ +0.5 _ 0.3 for ID97148 and log(age/yr) = 9.4^ +0.3 _ 0.9 for ID83492. In the following we adopt the ages estimated from the stellar population analysis. However, adopting those derived from SED fitting instead, would make our results (e.g., anti-correlation between gas fraction and age, Section subsec:discussion ) even stronger. Columns : (1) Galaxy ID; (2) For the two post-SBs: SFR averaged over 10 Myr from the SED modelling; for the two companions: SFR averaged over 100 Myr from the SED modelling; (3) stellar mass from the SED modelling; (4) molecular gas mass estimated from \ adopting r_ 31 =0.5 and _ CO = 4.4 M _ K km s ^ 1 pc ^2 (Section subsec:gas_mass ); (5) star formation efficiency, SFE = SFR/M _ H2 ; (6) depletion time, t _ d = M _ H2 / SFR (7) gas fraction, f _ H2 = M _ H2 / M _ ; (8) average R band light-weighted age from stellar population modelling. Notes: ^1 The uncertainties on the quantities derived from the SED modelling (Section subsec:sfr ) represent the 95 confidence interval. ^2 See Section subsec:CO for details. Stellar masses and SFRs have all been homogenized to IMF, and molecular gas masses have been homogenized to _ CO = 4.4 K km s ^ 1 pc ^2 . Molecular gas fraction evolution with redshift Based on the CO(3-2) detections, we estimate gas fractions M _ mol /M _ of 8 and 16 for our sample galaxies. These are comparable to gas fractions of other post-SB galaxies at similar redshift from the literature Belli2021, Bezanson2019, Suess2017 which have M _ mol /M _ 5 -- 25 . Such gas fractions are comparable or larger than those measured in quiescent galaxies at high redshift Spilker2018, Hayashi2018, Rudnik2017, Sargent2015 which are often not detected and have M _ mol /M _ 15 . In Figure fig:z_evol we show the molecular gas fraction of galaxies as a function of redshift. We compare different populations: post-SB (our sample, Suess2017 , Bezanson2019 , Belli2021 ), quiescent Sargent2015, Davis2016, Rudnik2017, Hayashi2018, Spilker2018, Williams2020 , and star-forming Saintonge2011, Tacconi2013, Freundlich2019 galaxies with spectroscopic redshifts in the range 0 < z < 2 . The gas fraction of star-forming galaxies increases with redshift, with z 1 - 1.5 sources having one order of magnitude higher M _ mol /M _ than local ones in agreement with the evolution of the main-sequence parametrized by . In the local Universe the molecular gas fraction of quiescent galaxies is 10 lower than that of star-forming systems at the same redshift (Figure fig:z_evol ). At z > 0.5 the CO line of most quiescent galaxies is not detected and therefore f_ H2 is an upper limit. Deeper observations are needed to confirm whether high-redshift quiescent galaxies are indeed more gas-rich than local ones. Post-starburst galaxies at z > 0.5 on the contrary have been mostly detected in current CO observations and have intermediate f_ H2 between star-forming and quiescent systems. In particular, our post-SB galaxies double the sample currently available at z > 1 and have intermediate gas fraction between quiescent and star-forming ones (Figure fig:z_evol ). The other two post-SBs at z > 1 from the literature with CO observations are from (not detected in CO) and (CO detected). The latter study analyzes three massive galaxies at 1 < z < 1.3 , selected to be detected at 24 m. We only consider the one which is classified as post-SB, namely EGS-17533, which has a gas fraction f_ H2 13 . This is consistent with the results obtained for our post-SB targets ( f_ H2 8 16 ), which are not detected at 24 m instead. While the decline of gas fraction with redshift is evident for star-forming galaxies, such an evolution is less clear (and possibly absent) for post-SBs . In particular, post-SBs seem to divide in two populations: those with gas fraction f_ H2 5 at all redshifts (from z 0 up to z 1.5 ), and those which are not CO detected and have molecular gas fraction upper limits f_ H2 1 5 , more similar to high-redshift quiescent galaxies (Figure fig:z_evol ). The latter are, for example, the 8 post-SBs from the sample of which are not CO-detected and have f_ H2 < 1 . They are among the most massive (M _ > 2.5 10^ 11 M _ ) and oldest (time since quenching > 0.1 Gyr, D _n4000 > 1.3 ) post-SBs reported in the literature, suggesting that quenching mechanisms could depend on galaxy mass (see Section subsec:discussion ). Star formation efficiency and Kennicutt-Schmidt plane By comparing the SFR and the molecular gas mass of galaxies in the traditional Schmidt-Kennicutt plane Kennicutt1998 it is possible to assess their star formation efficiency and in turn set constraints on models of galaxy quenching. We show the SFR - M _ H2 relation of our sample and literature galaxies in Figure fig:SFE (right panel). Star-forming galaxies at all redshifts follow a tight relation with a scatter of 0.2 dex, mostly independent of redshift Sargent2014 . Also the local quiescent galaxies from , which sample the low-SFR and low-M _ mol part of the parameter space, appear to follow such relation. Quiescent and post-SB systems at z > 0.5 instead show a systematic offset from this relation toward lower SFR, but with a large scatter ( 1 dex). In particular our two post-SBs have one order of magnitude lower molecular gas mass and almost two orders of magnitude lower SFR than star-forming galaxies at similar redshift (e.g., Tacconi2013 ). Their star formation efficiency, SFE = SFR/M _ mol 0.1 Gyr ^ 1 , is comparable to those of quiescent galaxies at similar redshift. The molecular gas fraction in different samples has been measured by using different CO line transitions, namely CO(1-0), CO(2-1), and CO(3-2), and converting them to M _ H2 by using the excitation factors r_ J1 and _ CO conversion factor, as discussed in Section subsec:gas_mass . We investigated whether the use of different tracers affects the offset and scatter of quiescent and post-SB galaxies in the Schmidt-Kennicutt plane by directly comparing their SFR and CO luminosity ( L'_ CO , Figure fig:SFE , left panel). The offset from the relation of star-forming galaxies remains, as well as the scatter. Our sample galaxies have CO luminosity comparable to z 0 star-forming galaxies from the COLDGASS survey Saintonge2011 and 1 dex lower than star-forming systems at z 1.3 Tacconi2013 . Another source of uncertainty is the estimate of the SFR for post-SB and quiescent galaxies as detecting low levels of star formation at high redshift is observationally challenging. In the literature SFR estimated using different tracers have been compared. In particular, measured the SFR by using three different methods: UV+IR emission, UV-to-IR spectral fit, and dust-corrected [ OII ] emission. They found that the UV+IR-based SFR estimates are systematically 2 - 6 times higher than those obtained with the other tracers. This is in agreement with previous findings by and who pointed out that the relatively old stellar population hosted by post-SBs heats the dust leading to a boost in infrared emission which is unrelated to star formation, hence resulting in higher UV+IR-based SFR estimates than those obtained when properly modelling the UV-to-IR SED. They also pointed out that SFR estimates based on dust-corrected emission lines (e.g., [ OII ] , H , [ NeII ] + [ NeIII ] ) might be underestimated due to inadequate dust correction and are possibly contaminated by AGN emission. On the other hand, the SFR measurements from SED fitting depend on degeneracies of the parameters (e.g., degeneracies between metallicity, age, and extinction of the stellar population) and on the adopted models themselves. In particular SED fitting results for post-SBs are very sensitive to the choice of star formation history, which can lead to systematic differences in the estimate of SFR up to 0.5 dex (e.g., French2018 , Wild2020 , Suess2022a ). In addition, different SFR proxies trace different timescales with the emission lines probing younger stellar populations than the continuum. Due to these reasons, different SFR values are reported in the literature for the same galaxies. One striking example is SDSSJ0912+1523: report a SFR= 2.1 0.8 M yr^ 1 estimated using the dust-corrected [ OII ] luminosity; report a SFR= 0.81^ +1.33 _ 0.76 M yr^ 1 derived from the spectro-photometric fit of UV-to-IR data with a set of non parameteric star formation histories Suess2022b ; finally report three different SFR estimates, SFR < 257 M yr^ 1 derived from the weighted sum of the UV and IR luminosities Bell2005, Wuyts2008 , SFR= 52^ +20 _ 20 M yr^ 1 from spectral fit, and SFR= 4.6 1.4 M yr^ 1 from the dust-corrected [ OII ] luminosity including extra dust attenuation toward the HII regions. In the following, we adopt SFR obtained through SED fit whenever available and in Appendix app:literature_data we report the assumptions underlying the SFR estimates for the literature comparison samples. Beyond the KS relation: exploring the full parameter space Besides that in the Schmidt-Kennicutt plane, other scaling relations linking galaxies molecular gas reservoirs and star formation activity have been proposed Lilly2013, Tacconi2013, Scoville2017, Tacconi2018 . In particular, the molecular gas fraction has been compared with the specific star formation rate, sSFR = SFR/M _ of galaxies, as a function of redshift Tacconi2020 . Most of such relations though have been investigated considering samples of star-forming galaxies. Only recently samples of post-SB and quiescent galaxies have been adopted to investigate the scatter of such relations at the low-end of the star formation activity Williams2020, Bezanson2021 . Across all redshifts, star-forming galaxies follow a similar relation (e.g., Feldmann2020 , Dou2021 ), but spanning different sSFR regimes We note that most of the star-forming galaxies from that seem to deviate from the and relations in Figure fig:sSFR have sSFR upper limits and are therefore formally consistent with those relations. . The post-SB galaxies of this sample instead lie outside such relation, having higher molecular gas fraction than expected given their sSFR. The deviation is still present also when considering the relation between f_ H2 and the sSFR, normalized by the sSFR of main-sequence galaxies from (Figure fig:sSFR , right panel). Our sample post-SBs show similar star formation activity (sSFR/sSFR _ MS ) as local quiescent galaxies Davis2016 , but they have two orders of magnitude larger gas fractions. In general, post-SBs span a large range of sSFR/sSFR _ MS 0.01 - 1 . Their star-formation activity does not seem to correlate with redshift nor with gas fraction (Figure fig:sSFR ). This is at odds with star-forming galaxies which instead show, on average, a decrease of their gas fraction with redshift and with sSFR/sSFR _ MS Saintonge2011, Tacconi2013, Freundlich2019 . Other post-SB galaxies from the literature show similar gas fractions as ours, but their sSFR/sSFR _ MS have a large scatter and can differ up to one order of magnitude (e.g., those by French2015 ). While the gas fraction of star-forming galaxies declines with sSFR/sSFR _ MS and redshift (in Figure fig:sSFR symbols with lighter colors indicate lower-redshift galaxies), such a trend seems to be lacking for post-SBs. However, one additional parameter that plays an important role in galaxy evolution is the stellar mass (e.g., Peng2010 ). In Figure fig:mstar we show the gas fraction of galaxies as a function of their stellar mass, divided in two redshfit bins ( z > 0.5 , left panel, and z < 0.5 right panel). The gas fraction of all galaxy types (star-forming, post-SB, quiescent) declines with increasing stellar mass, at all redshifts, with quiescent galaxies typically sampling the highest mass end (M _ > 10^ 11 M _ ) and having the lowest gas fraction. In particular, current literature studies of post-SB and quiescent galaxies at high redshift mostly consider massive sources with M_ 10^ 11 M_ . Our sample galaxies have 0.3-0.5 dex lower stellar masses, allowing us to start exploring the gas fraction of post-SBs in this relatively low-mass regime (Figure fig:mstar , left panel). More observations of low-mass post-SBs at high redshift are needed to understand whether indeed the gas fraction of post-SBs increases at lower stellar masses (e.g., as for the star-forming population). Different pathways have been proposed to explain the physical properties of post-SB galaxies and their contribution to the population of quiescent galaxies. They might be massive galaxies that have rapidly formed the bulk of their stellar content at z > 2 , consuming most of their molecular gas reservoirs and expelling the remaining gas through outflows, leading them to quiescence; however post-SBs might also be the result of gas-rich major-mergers and/or environment effects that stripped the gas and heated it up preventing new star formation Wild2016, Pawlik2019 . Both processes might be at play and effective, however it is important to understand what mechanisms are dominating at different cosmic times, as well as possible dependences on the galaxies stellar mass and environment. Our post-SB galaxies span the lowest stellar mass range probed by currently available samples (Figure fig:mstar ) and seem to have retained a significant fraction of molecular gas ( f_ H2 8 16 ) detected in CO(3-2), an emission line tracing modestly excited gas. This disfavours scenarios where the gas is consumed and expelled during strong bursts of star formation, which could instead be more common for more massive galaxies (M _ > 10^ 11 M _ , e.g., Williams2000 and part of the sample from Bezanson2021 which is not CO-detected). When we complement our sample with literature data, 6 out of 7 (i.e., 85 ) post-SB or quiescent galaxies with stellar mass M_ 10^ 11 M_ at z > 0.5 are detected in CO and have f_ H2 8 (Figure fig:mstar ). Higher mass galaxies ( M_ > 10^ 11 M_ ) at these redshifts instead have a lower CO detection rate: only 8 out of 27 are detected (i.e., 29 ) and have gas fraction consistent with our sample post-SBs ( f_ H2 5 - 20 ), whereas the non-detections have typical upper limits of f_ H2 5 . The large number of non detections among the high-mass population suggests that multiple mechanisms might be at play for more massive galaxies: in these cases, not only the star formation is suppressed (as for the lower mass galaxies), but also the gas is expelled (e.g., due to AGN or star formation feedback) or stripped during the quenching phase. For the majority of lower mass galaxies instead quenching mechanisms that halt star formation without removing the gas seem to be favoured. Also the fact that post-SBs, especially at low mass and high redshift, have higher gas fraction than expected given their sSFR favours the idea that gas is not expelled before the star formation is quenched or, in other words, that gas depletion is not a direct cause of star formation suppression. This is in agreement with results from probing more massive and lower redshift post-SBs. Mechanisms, such as major mergers, which remove part of the gas and heat up the remaining (e.g., through shocks or by increasing the internal turbulent pressure, Smercina2021 ) are favoured. Indeed we find that ID83492 is possibly undergoing an early merger phase with two gas-rich and starbursting close companions (Section subsec:merger ). We do not find clear signatures of mergers for ID97148 instead, but this might simply be due to the lack of high angular resolution observations that prevent us from detecting close pairs and/or due to the fact that low-surface brightness features indicative of past mergers fade quickly ( < 0.5 Gyr, e.g. Pawlik2019 ). Studying the CO spectral line energy distribution would clarify whether indeed the remaining gas is excited and high- J CO transitions are favoured in post-SB galaxies. Relating the properties of the stellar population (e.g., age) with the gas fraction might further constrain the evolutionary pathways of post-SB galaxies Bezanson2021 . We investigated whether a relation between the gas fraction and the D _n 4000 index, a proxy of the age of the stellar population, is in place by complementing our results with the literature (Figure fig:Dn4000 ). When considering galaxies detected in CO at z > 0.5 - 1.5 (our sample and Spilker2018, Bezanson2021, Belli2021 ) we find that galaxies with higher gas fraction preferentially have lower D _n 4000 (Figure fig:Dn4000 , top panels) and younger ages (Figure fig:Dn4000 , bottom panels). We investigated if, after a first episode that drives the quenching of star formation (e.g., major merger), the little ongoing star formation rate (SFR 0.5 - 1 M _ yr ^ 1 ) can consume the remaining molecular gas reservoir. Even assuming a constant SFR equal to the one we estimated at z 1 , the gas fraction of our sample galaxies would decrease by a factor two (e.g., for ID83492 it would become f_ H2 8 ). This is larger than the f_ H2 1 0.1 measured in z 0 , massive ( M_ 10^ 11.5 M_ ) quiescent galaxies Davis2016 . Similar arguments hold also for ID97148. This indicates that either lower-mass quiescent galaxies have larger molecular gas fractions than massive ( M_ 10^ 11.5 M_ ) ones or that additional mechanisms are needed to remove or heat the gas at low redshift. At lower redshift ( z < 0.5 , French2015, Rowlands2015 ) a broad anti-correlation of f_ H2 with D _n 4000 is maintained (Figure fig:Dn4000 , bottom right panel), although the picture becomes less clear, especially when considering the age estimates, possibly indicating that more quenching processes might be at play for local post-SBs. For example at lower redshift some post-SB galaxies might have re-accreted gas (hence show a relatively large gas fraction), while environmental processes (e.g., ram pressure stripping) and AGN feedback might have played an important role in local environments. Larger samples of post-SB and quiescent galaxies with high signal-to-noise optical spectra will be needed to investigate in the future such relations between f_ H2 and stellar populations. Our sample galaxies are not detected in dust-continuum. However, assuming a standard dust-to-gas conversion factor (Section subsec:gas_mass ) we obtain M _ H2 upper limits consistent with the CO-based moelcular gas mass estimates. This suggests that exotic gas-to-dust ratios (e.g., Whitaker2021 ) are not needed to explain the molecular gas fractions observed in post-SB galaxies. However deeper submillimeter observations are needed to detect the dust-continuum underlying the CO(3-2) emission and properly measure the gas-to-dust ratio in post-SBs. Moreover, our results seem to confirm that the less massive post-SB galaxies retain substantial molecular gas reservoirs, possibly in agreement with studies targeting the dust emission of post-SB and quiescent galaxies and stacking statistical samples (e.g., Gobat2018, Magdis2021 , Bl anquez-Sese et al., submitted). Having deep observations of post-SBs to detect individually their dust continuum will be crucial to confirm these results. The merger scenario It has been suggested several times in previous studies that post-starburst galaxies represent a transition phase between gas-rich mergers and gas-poor quiescent galaxies. A large fraction of local post-SBs in the field show major merger signatures (e.g., strong tidal features, disturbed kinematics, nearby companions) in HST imaging Zabludoff1996, Yang2006, Chandar2021 and in high-resolution ALMA observations Smercina2021 . find that the merger fraction in local post-SBs ranges between 19 and 42 , depending on the merger identification criteria, but merger signatures fade quickly ( < 200 Myr) and therefore such fractions might actually be higher. Indeed, when considering deep HST and SDSS images, find that 88 of their local post-SBs show disturbed morphologies. They also point out that most disturbances are low-surface brightness features which require high sensitivity and fine spatial resolution to be identified, and that fade in short timescales, suggesting that all their post-SBs might have had a merger origin. Similar results are also found by , who also show that post-SBs are 3.6^ +2.9 _ 1.3 ( 2.1^ +1.9 _ 0.73 ) times more likely to show disturbances than quiescent (star-forming) galaxies. In the cosmological hydrodynamical EAGLE simulations, between 30 and 50 of post-SBs have undergone a merger just before the onset of their post-starburst episode Davis2018, Pawlik2019 . Similarly, idealised simulations of galaxy mergers have found that a brief ( < 600 Myr) post-starburst phase can follow a gas-rich merger which triggers a central starburst phase, succeeded by the rapid quenching of star formation Wild2009, Bekki2005, Snyder2011 . Studying the morphology of high-redshift post-SBs is still challenging, due to the need of high-resolution and deep observations. have studied the morphology of 145 post-SBs at z 0.7 and found no correlation between their size and time since quenching. This finding disfavours the formation of post-SBs purely through mergers which would instead ignite a central burst of star formation, that in turn would make the galaxy more compact and shut off star formation. On the contrary, other works found that post-SBs are in general smaller than the average population of quiescent galaxies (e.g., Whitaker2012, Yano2016, Almaini2017, Wu2018 ). Therefore, evidence for mergers being at the origin of post-SBs, especially at high redshift, is still inconclusive. To assess whether our sample galaxies are undergoing a merger we investigated their environment. In particular, we serendipitously found two CO(3-2) emitters located respectively at 5 .8 and 13 .7 away from ID83492. Their redshift, estimated from the CO emission, is z < 0.0018 ( v_ off < 250 km s ^ 1 ) different from that of our post-SB target. This suggests that ID83492 might be in an early (multiple) merger stage. Given the stellar masses of the companions, this is a major merger (mass ratios 1:1 and 1:1.5). ID83492 and its two companions might be part of a larger galaxy group or be the parents of a very massive galaxy to be formed by z 0 through the merger of these sources. In the current HST and ALMA data we do not detect tidal features or morphological asymmetries indicating an ongoing merger, although deeper and higher resolution observations might be needed to detect those, especially if the merger is in its early phase. The two companion galaxies are gas-rich, star-forming sources in a starburst phase (sSFR > 10 , Figure fig:sSFR ), lying above main-sequence galaxies in the KS plane (Figure fig:SFE ). On the contrary ID97148 does not show any clear companion, a part from a continuum-detected source that might rather be a lower redshift interloper (Section subsec:continuum ). Observations with better angular resolution are needed to understand whether ID97148 is undergoing a late merger phase with two nuclei which are not resolved with the current angular resolution (pair closer than 1 .5 12.5 kpc at z 1.2 ).

gmw_00010539.name: 2MASS J11052188+3814018
gmw_00010539.overview: 2MASS J11052188+3814018 is a nearby radio-quiet, type II active galactic nucleus located in the core of a spiral galaxy [arXiv:2110.07373]. While clearly visible in optical wavelengths, it is also detected as an X-ray source and classified as a star-forming galaxy by the SDSS spectrograph [arXiv:2110.07373].

sources.summary: The paper describes 2MASS J11052188+3814018 as a nearby radio-quiet type II AGN in a spiral-galaxy core, visible optically and detected as an X-ray spot.

quotes.quote: 2MASS J11052188+3814018 (LEDA 33514) is located at the edge of the field of view near the bright blazar Markarian 421. Quasar LEDA 33514 itself is clearly visible in optics (R=11.3, M=-24.3). It is the core of a spiral galaxy and in X-rays looks like a fairly clear spot. This quasar is close, z= 0.02847, radio-quiet, type-II AGN (as classified in NED) . 2MASS J11052188+3814018 with number SDSS J110521.89+381401.7 has the interactive spectrum obtained by the SDSS spectrograph and classified by it as starforming galaxy.

gmw_00010540.name: 2MASS J12555303+2724053
gmw_00010540.overview: 2MASS J12555303+2724053 is a member of the Coma Cluster [arXiv:2110.07373] classified as a low-luminosity, radio-quiet quasar or Seyfert 1 galaxy [arXiv:2110.07373]. Its low-frequency radio continuum emission is consistent with being dominated by star formation [arXiv:2110.07373].

sources.summary: The paper presents 2MASS J12555303+2724053 as a Coma-cluster QSO/Seyfert 1 and low-luminosity radio-quiet quasar whose radio continuum is consistent with star formation.

quotes.quote: 2MASS J12555303+2724053 (PB 3126) is a member of the Coma Cluster (Abell 1656), QSO at z = 0.31605 ( M_z=-23.95 0.50 ), alternative classification is Seyfert 1 . It is included in the sample LoTSS/HETDEX (Hobby-Eberly Telescope Dark Energy Experiment) of quasars selected by their optical spectra in conjunction with sensitive and high-resolution low-frequency radio data provided by the LOw Frequency ARray (LOFAR) as part of the LOFAR Two-Metre Sky Survey (LoTSS) to investigate their radio properties . It is a low-luminosity radio-quiet QSO. Radio continuum emission at low frequencies in low-luminosity quasars is consistent with being dominated by star formation.

gmw_00010541.name: SDSS J013433.43+001206.6
gmw_00010541.overview: SDSS J013433.43+001206.6 is a radio-quiet type I quasar [arXiv:2110.07373]. Its spectrum is dominated by typical broad and narrow emission lines, but the object is difficult to observe in the optical range due to blending from a nearby bright star [arXiv:2110.07373].

sources.summary: The paper describes SDSS J013433.43+001206.6 as a difficult-to-observe distant quasar affected by nearby starlight, with SDSS/BOSS emission-line spectrum and radio-quiet type I QSO classification.

quotes.quote: SDSS J013433.43+001206.6 is very problematic to observe. At a distance of 113 angular seconds from the quasar there is a star HD9649 with a magnitude of V = 7.87. It blends the quasar in the optical range.

In the SDSS catalog it is object number SDSS J120858.00+454035.4 with the spectrum obtained by the BOSS spectrograph, its class is Broadline QSO. The spectrum is dominated by the typical QSO emission lines: broad CIV, CIII ] , CII, MgII, narrow Balmer series. Besides the major emission lines several weaker features are recognized as well, e.g. HeII, [ OII ] , [ NeIII ] . The line content of the quasar spectrum is similar to QSO J1208+4540. SDSS J013433.43+001206.6 unambiguously classified as radio-quiet type I QSO.

gmw_00010542.name: host of GRB 050223
gmw_00010542.overview: The host of GRB 050223 is a dusty starburst galaxy [arXiv:1407.8180]. It contains a radio point source coincident with the host galaxy, which has been observed with the Herschel Space Telescope [arXiv:1407.8180].

sources.summary: The paper reports a radio point source at the GRB 050223 host, notes weak Herschel constraints, and argues its star-formation estimate may exceed prior SED-based values.

quotes.quote: A third (GRB 050223) shows a significant (3 ) point source coincident with the host galaxy identified by 2006A&A...459L...5P .

An additional constraint exists for one of our detected sources - the host of GRB 050223 - in the form of mid-infrared Herschel Space Telescope data. 2014arXiv1402.4006H observed a small sample of `dark' GRBs with the PACS and SPIRE instruments, obtaining constraints on their mid-infrared spectral energy distribution (SED). In the case of GRB 050223, the host galaxy remained undetected, and the SED fit is driven by the optical to near-infrared data observed through to the WISE W1 and W2 bands. As figure 2 of 2014arXiv1402.4006H makes clear, the Herschel data do not tightly constrain the SED of this target, and derived parameters such as dust temperature and emission spectrum, star burst fraction, star formation rate and obscuration are highly degenerate. In the case of GRB 050223 the fit to the stellar spectrum dominated optical does little to break these degeneracies or constrain the longer wavelength emission.

Given that this host galaxy had already been identified as a dusty starburst, with an A _V>2 i.e. extinguished by a factor >5 in the ultraviolet, 2006A&A...459L...5P , one might expect an estimate of the infrared emission to exceed the ultraviolet estimate by this factor, even without accounting for any more obscured emission.

gmw_00010543.name: J1333+6246
gmw_00010543.overview: J1333+6246 is an emission-line galaxy characterized by a low escape fraction of only a few percent [arXiv:2009.09882]. It stands apart due to a nondetection of its Lyman-alpha line alongside a large Lyman-alpha deficit [arXiv:2009.09882].

sources.summary: The paper describes J1333+6246 as a low-escape-fraction leaker with a large line deficit, a nondetection in one diagnostic line, and the largest excess in the sample.

quotes.quote: However, we note that three galaxies in our sample with a large deficit in \ ( 0.3) have intermediate (J1011+1947, f_ esc =11,4 ) to low escape fractions (J1248+4259, J1333+6246, labeled on the plot). One of them (J1333+6246) strikingly stands apart with a nondetection of the \ line but the largest \ excess in the sample.
However, the most deficient galaxy in our sample (J1248+4259) and J1333+6246, which is undetected in , have escape fractions of only a few percent.

gmw_00010544.name: NGC 357
gmw_00010544.overview: NGC 357 is a double-barred galaxy [arXiv:1111.1718] that features an outer ring [arXiv:0707.4599]. Its bulge, inner bar, and outer bar are nearly coeval and old, suggesting that the redistribution of existing stars shaped its inner structures [arXiv:1111.1718].

sources.summary: The paper reports deep images of NGC 357, finds bar-end drops in its profiles, shows deprojection lengthens and increases the bar ellipticity, and examines the ring position-angle change.

quotes.quote: Comments on Individual Galaxies Our images of NGC 357, NGC 1211 and NGC 7280 reach the 25 B-band mag arcsec ^ 2 isophote.
NGC 357 is a nice example of these changes: the bar becomes longer and more eccentric when deprojected, whereas the outer disc becomes more circular, as expected (see Fig. res357 ).

In NGC 357 one sees that the outer change in position angle due to the ring practically disappears. The line of nodes lies roughly along the position angle of the ring, which is then stretched along the perpendicular direction. The result is that from being almost perpendicular to the bar, the ring becomes almost parallel. This is interesting since both simulations sch79,sch81 and analysis of observations but86,but95 show that inner rings are preferentially aligned with bars.

sources.summary: The paper studies double-barred NGC 357, finding old, nearly coeval bulge and bar structures whose stellar redistribution, rather than recent gas-driven star formation, shaped the inner components.

quotes.quote: To test this idea, we perform for the first time a detailed analysis of the photometry, kinematics and stellar populations of a double-barred galaxy: NGC 357. We find that this galaxy is either hosting a pseudobulge or a classical bulge together with an inner disc. We compare the relative mean luminosity-weighted age, metallicity and enhancement between the (pseudo)bulge, inner bar and outer bar, finding that the three structures are nearly coeval and old. Moreover, the bulge and inner bar present the same metallicity and overabundance, whereas the outer bar tends to be less metal-rich and more enhanced. These results point out that, rather than the classical secular scenario in which gas and star formation play a major role, the redistribution of the existing stars is driving the formation of the inner structures.

gmw_00010545.name: MM14
gmw_00010545.overview: MM14 is a tentative submillimeter galaxy candidate detected with the Submillimeter Array [arXiv:1007.0200]. The system features a faint southern source and a northern optical source that may represent an interacting merger or an extended galaxy with an obscured spiral arm [arXiv:1007.0200].

sources.summary: The paper treats MM14 as a tentative high-redshift submillimeter galaxy candidate, with an SMA detection, uncertain counterpart identification, a likely faint K-band source, and possible interaction with MM14N.

quotes.quote: SMA detections Fitting two-dimensional Gaussians to the SMA images, leads to flux densities of 16.8 1.5 mJy and 8.5 2.0 mJy for MM1 and MM14, respectively. The MM14 detection is tentative since, as we shall see below, it could not be reliably identified with any significant counterpart at other wavelengths.

Due to its proximity to the SMA position, it appears to be the most likely counterpart to the submm emission despite its faintness. Hereafter, we refer to this source as MM14S (south), while for the northern optical source we refer as MM14N. However, since we could not reliably identify any significant multi-wavelength counterpart for this source and given the relatively low significance of the SMA detection, we label this source (MM14S) as a tentative detection. The spatial configuration between MM14S and MM14N could resemble a merger/interaction system, where the submm emission comes from a highly obscured source (MM14S), but could also correspond to an extended galaxy with the submm emission located in an obscured spiral arm. The offset between the MM14N and the submm position (MM14S) is 9 kpc, assuming z 3.5 , similar to the case of the high-redshift SMG GN20 Iono2006 , where the submm and the optical peaks are separated by 0.8 , or 6 kpc.

gmw_00010546.name: AGC 123216
gmw_00010546.overview: AGC 123216 is an exceptionally blue, gas-rich dwarf galaxy candidate with an ultra-diffuse size [arXiv:2304.08552]. It features an optical component that is elongated in the east-west direction, surrounded by a relatively rounded concentration of neutral hydrogen gas [arXiv:2304.08552].

sources.summary: AGC 123216 is described as an extremely blue, gas-rich almost-dark TDG candidate with elongated optical light, rounded HI, UDG-like size, and unusually high dark matter content for a TDG.

quotes.quote: AGC 123216 itself has an interesting appearance, with an optical component that is elongated in the east-west direction but surrounded by a relatively rounded concentration of HI gas, although this appearance is somewhat dependent on the elongated beam (Fig. fig:AGC123216closeup ). The measured properties of AGC 123216 can be found in Table tab:AGC123216results , and the surface brightness profiles are shown in Fig. fig:AGC123216_sbprof . It has a central surface brightness in g of 24.86 0.03 mag arcsec ^ 2 and an absolute total magnitude in g of -13.44 0.08 mag. The g- color of -0.11 0.12 makes it exceptionally blue. The effective radius from the Sersic profile fit is 1.83 0.04 kpc, which combined with the central surface brightness, places it in the regime of UDGs as defined by .

The stellar mass of AGC 123216 is estimated at (3.86 1.24) 10^6 M_ , and its HI-to-stellar mass ratio is 115.28 42.20. While it is extremely gas-rich, it has apparently formed relatively few stars for its gas mass. It has a dynamical mass of (2.33 0.82) 10^9 M_ , which yields a dynamical-to-gas mass ratio of 4.40 1.70. lr@ lr ht 1 Property [ Units ] & 1 @ Value & 1 Property [ Units ] & 1 Value RA [ h m s, J2000 ] & 02 04 44.6 & Dec [ ^ ' ", J2000 ] & +28 48 11.28 V_h' [ km s ^ 1 ] & 5111 14 & N_ HI,peak [ 10^ 20 cm ^ 2 ] & 5.02 S_ 21 ' [ Jy km s ^ 1 ] & 0.38 0.05 & S_ 21 [ Jy km s ^ 1 ] & 0.34 0.03 M_ HI ' [ 10^8 M_ ] & 4.45 0.79 & M_ HI [ 10^8 M_ ] & 3.98 0.62 M_ gas ' [ 10^8 M_ ] & 5.91 1.05 & M_ gas [ 10^8 M_ ] & 5.30 0.83 R_ HI [ kpc ] & 7.69 1.06 & Inclination [ ^ ] & 45 5 R_e [ kpc ] & 1.83 0.04 & R_d [ kpc ] & 2.32 0.05 _ g,0 [ mag arcsec ^ 2 ] & 24.86 0.03 & _ ,0 [ mag arcsec ^ 2 ] & 25.05 0.08 m_g [ mag ] & 20.80 0.04 & m_r [ mag ] & 20.91 0.11 M_g [ mag ] & -13.44 0.08 & M_r [ mag ] & -13.33 0.13 (g-) [ mag ] & -0.11 0.12 & M_ * [ 10^6 M_ ] & 3.86 1.24 M_ bary ' [ 10^8 M_ ] & 5.96 1.05 & M_ dyn [ 10^9 M_ ] & 2.33 0.82 M_ HI '/L_g & 17.04 3.39 & M_ HI '/L_r & 28.80 6.32 M_ HI '/M_ * & 115.28 42.20 & M_ dyn /M_ gas & 4.40 1.70 RA and Dec are listed for the optical component of AGC 123216.

gmw_00010547.name: MRK 365
gmw_00010547.overview: MRK 365 is a spiral galaxy located southwest of AGC 123216 [arXiv:2304.08552]. It features HI gas offset from its optical center, a low-surface-brightness stellar wrapping, and a single exponential disk that may indicate a late-stage merger [arXiv:2304.08552].

sources.summary: MRK 365 is treated as the closest possible parent of AGC 123216, a spiral with offset HI, low-surface-brightness stellar wrapping, and possible late-stage merger evidence.

quotes.quote: MRK 365 is a spiral galaxy to the southwest of AGC 123216. MRK 365 is the potential parent that is closest to AGC 123216, with a minimum projected separation of 214 7 kpc and a heliocentric velocity difference of 40 15 km s ^ 1 . The HI gas in MRK 365 appears to be offset from the optical center (Fig. fig:MRK365 ), and high-sensitivity images reveal a low surface brightness string of stellar emission wrapping around the outside of the galaxy. It has been listed in the literature as having a double nucleus, which would suggest a previous or ongoing merger, though there is discussion about whether this is true Gimeno2004 . constructed a luminosity profile and found that it was consistent with the presence of a single exponential disk; this is a sign that if there is a merger, it is in a late stage. NGC 807 is the next closest potential parent to AGC 123216, with a minimum projected separation of 231 8 kpc and a heliocentric velocity difference of 361 218 km s ^ 1 .

gmw_00010548.name: MCG 05-06-003
gmw_00010548.overview: MCG 05-06-003 is an edge-on spiral galaxy that features a bar [arXiv:2304.08552]. It exhibits a slight extension of neutral hydrogen to the northeast and is considered the least likely of three potential parent galaxies to be the progenitor of AGC 123216 [arXiv:2304.08552].

sources.summary: MCG 05-06-003 is described as the farthest candidate parent of AGC 123216, an edge-on barred spiral with slight HI extension and weaker progenitor evidence.

quotes.quote: At a projected separation of 331 11 kpc, MCG 05-06-003 is the farthest potential parent from AGC 123216. Not much is known about MCG 05-06-003, which appears to be an edge-on spiral galaxy with a significant bar. The HI map shows a slight extension of HI to the northeast, perpendicular to the direction of AGC 123216. Given the closer proximity (see Table tab:ALFALFAdata ) along with extensive HI distortion or apparent recent interaction history of the other two potential parent galaxies, we consider MCG 05-06-003 to be the least likely of the three galaxies to be the progenitor of AGC 123216.

gmw_00010549.name: AGC 219369
gmw_00010549.overview: AGC 219369 is a blue, round, low-surface-brightness dwarf galaxy that appears to be dynamically stable [arXiv:2304.08552]. Although its heliocentric velocity suggests a distance of 9.2 Mpc, it may be associated with the spiral galaxy NGC 3510 at a distance of approximately 16.8 Mpc [arXiv:2304.08552].

sources.summary: AGC 219369 has an ambiguous distance, may be associated with NGC 3510, and is described as round, blue, low-surface-brightness, dynamically stable, and likely dark matter dominated.

quotes.quote: AGC 219369 has a heliocentric velocity of 667 11 km s ^ 1 , which implies a distance of 9.2 2.2 Mpc according to the flow model that was used to construct the ALFALFA catalog. However, AGC 219369 is located in the “triple-value region" of the sky, where proximity to the Virgo Cluster affects the radial velocities of nearby objects such that objects at three different distances can have the same radial velocity Marinoni1998,Shaya2017 . This makes measuring the distance to AGC 219369 with only the heliocentric velocity difficult. Redshift-independent Tully-Fisher distance measurements for the nearby spiral galaxy NGC 3510 (which has a similar heliocentric velocity to AGC 219369) place it at 16.8 2.5 Mpc Cosmicflows3 , and this is in agreement with the distance of 16.7 3.3 Mpc from the flow model.

In Fig. fig:AGC219369closeup , we can see that the HI distribution of AGC 219369 appears to be round, as is the optical component, which would suggest dynamical stability. The surface brightness profiles of AGC 219369 are plotted in Fig. fig:AGC219369_sbprof , with scale bars for physical units corresponding to the reported ALFALFA distance of 9.2 Mpc Haynes2018 . The surface brightness profile in g follows an exponential law very closely, and results in a central surface brightness in g of 25.00 0.03 mag arcsec ^ 2 . The (g-) color of 0.08 0.03 indicates it is quite blue.

gmw_00010550.name: NGC 3510
gmw_00010550.overview: NGC 3510 is a nearby, highly edge-on spiral galaxy [arXiv:2304.08552] that exhibits a large, highly symmetric warp [arXiv:astro-ph/0207112]. It has undergone a tidal interaction that stretched its stellar arms, distorted its neutral hydrogen gas, and produced asymmetric tails [arXiv:2304.08552].

sources.summary: NGC 3510 is discussed as a nearby spiral possibly connected to AGC 219369, with interaction-stretched stellar arms, distorted HI, asymmetric tails, and difficult edge-on interpretation.

quotes.quote: Redshift-independent Tully-Fisher distance measurements for the nearby spiral galaxy NGC 3510 (which has a similar heliocentric velocity to AGC 219369) place it at 16.8 2.5 Mpc Cosmicflows3 , and this is in agreement with the distance of 16.7 3.3 Mpc from the flow model. NGC 3510 has clearly undergone an interaction that stretched its stellar arms and distorted its HI (Fig. fig:AGC219369full ), and AGC 219369 is the nearest object to it (angular separation of 12.2 arcminutes, which corresponds to only 59 12 kpc at a distance of 16.7 Mpc), so given the ambiguity on the distance it is reasonable to consider that AGC 219369 may have a distance of 16.7 Mpc as well. The HI tail is significantly more substantial on the north side of NGC 3510, but tidal interactions usually produce two tidal tails which may or may not be similar sizes based on the parameters of the interaction Duc2008a . We can consider whether the HI that formed the southern tail may have concentrated into a more massive object at its end and separated from it, producing AGC 219369, or whether AGC 219369 originally came from the larger tail and the southern tail was always short. In spite of the motion that the curvature of the HI tails suggests, NGC 3510 is very edge on, and Fig. fig:NGC3510_velmap confirms that the rotation axis is roughly perpendicular to the line of sight.

sources.summary: The paper says UGC 6126's warp-radius measurement was affected by spiral arms and identifies it as one of only two systems with large, symmetric warps.

quotes.quote: The warp radius for one side of a galaxy (UGC 6126) was strongly affected by the presence of spiral arms. We manually set the warp radius of that side to that of the other side of the galaxy.
There are only 2 systems with large warps and high symmetry: UGC 6964 and UGC 6126.

gmw_00010551.name: AGC 229398
gmw_00010551.overview: AGC 229398 is an almost-dark galaxy candidate with a low surface brightness and a large effective radius [arXiv:2304.08552]. While its neutral hydrogen distribution is round, its optical component is elongated and features a small stellar emission knot to the northeast [arXiv:2304.08552].

sources.summary: AGC 229398 is the farthest and most massive almost-dark candidate, with round HI, elongated optical light, large effective radius, low surface brightness, and low dynamical-to-gas mass ratio.

quotes.quote: AGC 229398 is the farthest and most massive AD of the set, with a distance of 104.0 2.3 Mpc and an HI mass of (1.91 0.22) 10^9 M_ Haynes2018 . It has two potential parent galaxies, of which UGC 6989 is the most distant potential parent in the sample (Fig. fig:AGC229398full ). While the HI distribution is round, the optical component of AGC 229398 itself is elongated in the east-west direction (Fig. fig:AGC229398close ). There also appears to be a small knot of stellar emission to the north-east.

The measured properties of AGC 229398 can be found in Table tab:AGC229398results , and Fig. fig:AGC229398_sbprof shows the surface brightness profiles. It has the largest effective radius, with r_ e = 9.05 1.10 kpc, and a central surface brightness in g of 23.84 0.13 mag arcsec ^ 2 . The (g-) color of 0.35 0.15 mag is the reddest of the AD-TDG candidates. The HI mass measured by WSRT is significantly smaller than the one measured by ALFALFA, which suggests that ALFALFA may be detecting diffuse gas that is distributed over a larger area.

gmw_00010552.name: KUG 1158+216
gmw_00010552.overview: KUG 1158+216 is a spiral galaxy located at a distance of approximately 110.1 Mpc [arXiv:2304.08552]. It features a faint eastern stellar tail, which suggests the galaxy may have experienced a past tidal interaction [arXiv:2304.08552].

sources.summary: KUG 1158+216 is the closest potential parent of AGC 229398, with comparable distance and velocity and a faint eastern stellar tail possibly left by tidal interaction.

quotes.quote: The spiral galaxy KUG 1158+216, which is located to the south of AGC 229398, is the closest potential parent galaxy in distance, heliocentric velocity, and angular separation. The distance given by the ALFALFA survey is 110.1 2.3 Mpc, the heliocentric velocity difference is 423 126 km s ^ 1 , and the projected separation on the sky is 102 2 kpc Haynes2018 . There is a faint stellar tail extending from the eastern side that may be the remains of a tidal interaction (left panel of Fig. fig:AGC229398parents ). We do not have maps of the HI emission for KUG 1158+216, so we are unable to examine the neutral gas distribution for signs of tidal distortion in the direction of AGC 229398.

gmw_00010553.name: UGC 6989
gmw_00010553.overview: UGC 6989 is a spiral galaxy with distance estimates ranging from 79.8 to 96.0 Mpc [arXiv:2304.08552]. Despite being a possible parent galaxy to AGC 229398, its spiral disk and neutral hydrogen distribution show little sign of disturbance [arXiv:2304.08552].

sources.summary: UGC 6989 is a more distant possible parent of AGC 229398, but discrepant distance estimates and undisturbed HI and spiral structure weaken that interpretation.

quotes.quote: UGC 6989 is a spiral galaxy some distance away from AGC 229398, with a projected separation of 482 11 kpc. The ALFALFA survey lists the distance of UGC 6989 as 96.0 2.4 Mpc, but there are some discrepancies between other measurement methods. The redshift-independent Tully-Fisher measurement from the Cosmicflows-4 survey, which uses the relationship between HI linewidths (i.e. rotation rates) and galaxy luminosities to measure the distances to almost 10,000 spiral galaxies, is 79.8 8.9 Mpc Kourkchi2020 , which would remove it from consideration as a possible parent. The HI distribution and spiral disk of UGC 6989 do not show much sign of disturbance (right panel of Fig. fig:AGC229398parents ), though AGC 229398 is far enough away that if it had come from UGC 6989, enough time may have passed for it to restabilize. lr@ lr ht 1 Property [ Units ] & 1 @ Value & 1 Property [ Units ] & 1 Value RA [ h m s, J2000 ] & 12 00 39.8 & Dec [ ^ ' ", J2000 ] & +21 24 47.6 v_ h ' [ km s ^ 1 ] & 6965 14 & N_ HI,peak [ 10^ 20 cm ^ 2 ] & 3.95 S_ 21 ' [ Jy km s ^ 1 ] & 0.75 0.04 & S_ 21 [ Jy km s ^ 1 ] & 0.32 0.03 M_ HI ' [ 10^9 M_ ] & 1.91 0.23 & M_ HI [ 10^8 M_ ] & 8.15 1.11 M_ gas ' [ 10^9 M_ ] & 2.54 0.31 & M_ gas [ 10^9 M_ ] & 1.09 0.15 R_ HI [ kpc ] & 9.83 1.53 & Inclination [ ^ ] & 66 8 R_e [ kpc ] & 9.05 1.10 & R_d [ kpc ] & 0.95 0.12 _ g,0 [ mag arcsec ^ 2 ] & 23.84 0.13 & _ ,0 [ mag arcsec ^ 2 ] & 24.48 0.10 m_g [ mag ] & 19.74 0.11 & m_r [ mag ] & 19.39 0.10 M_g [ mag ] & -15.35 0.12 & M_r [ mag ] & -15.69 0.11 (g-) [ mag ] & 0.35 0.15 & M_ * [ 10^7 M_ ] & 9.78 4.82 M_ bary ' [ 10^9 M_ ] & 2.64 0.31 & M_ dyn [ 10^9 M_ ] & 1.64 0.39 M_ HI '/L_g & 12.51 1.99 & M_ HI '/L_r & 13.88 2.10 M_ HI '/M_ * & 19.54 9.92 & M_ dyn /M_ gas & 1.52 0.41 RA and Dec are listed for the optical component of AGC 229398.

gmw_00010554.name: AGC 333576
gmw_00010554.overview: AGC 333576 is a low surface brightness dwarf galaxy candidate with a rounded optical component and an elongated neutral hydrogen structure [arXiv:2304.08552]. It has a relatively large stellar mass of approximately 1.64 billion solar masses and a very low dynamical-to-gas mass ratio [arXiv:2304.08552].

sources.summary: AGC 333576 is a likely evolved TDG candidate near NGC 7775, with rounded optical light, elongated HI, low central surface brightness, large stellar mass, and very low dynamical-to-gas ratio.

quotes.quote: The optical component of AGC 333576 is relatively rounded, though there appear to be two distinct branches on the left side and there are clear knots of brighter stars (right panel of Fig. fig:AGC333576_closeup ). The surrounding HI is slightly elongated along the northwest/southeast axis.

The measured properties of AGC 333576 are listed in Table tab:AGC333576results , and Fig. fig:AGC333576_sbprof displays the surface brightness profiles. It has a central surface brightness in g of 24.57 0.02 mag arcsec ^ 2 , an effective radius of 4.40 0.05 kpc, and a g- color of 0.21 0.03 mag. AGC 333576 is estimated as having a stellar mass of (1.64 0.12) 10^8 M_ , the largest in the set, which gives it a HI-to-stellar mass ratio of 7.60 1.28. The dynamical mass of (1.90 0.37) 10^9 M_ gives a very low dynamical-to-gas mass ratio of 1.07 0.27.

gmw_00010555.name: NGC 7775
gmw_00010555.overview: NGC 7775 is a spiral galaxy characterized by chaotic stellar arms and a potentially massive star-forming region [arXiv:2304.08552]. It exhibits an optical tidal tail and a distorted neutral hydrogen distribution extending toward its companion, AGC 333576 [arXiv:2304.08552].

sources.summary: NGC 7775 is the obvious parent candidate for AGC 333576, showing chaotic stellar arms, distorted HI toward AGC 333576, and an optical tidal tail curving toward it.

quotes.quote: NGC 7775 is a spiral galaxy with chaotic stellar arms and a bright area in the lower right corner of the galaxy which may be a massive star formation region (Fig. fig:AGC333576_full ). NGC 7775's HI distribution reveals a clear distortion in the direction of AGC 333576 (Fig. fig:AGC333576_full ) and the optical image shows a tidal tail extending from the north side and curving toward AGC 333576 (Fig. fig:AGC333576_closeup , left). According to the HI heliocentric velocities from , both objects are 93.9 4.3 Mpc away, and this is in agreement with other redshift-independent measurements for NGC 7775 (96.50 19 Mpc from Theureau2007 ).

gmw_00010556.name: WISEA J235250.49+284555.2
gmw_00010556.overview: WISEA J235250.49+284555.2 is an elliptical galaxy located near AGC 333576 [arXiv:2304.08552]. Because of its similar velocity and close physical proximity, it may participate in the interactions of this neighboring system [arXiv:2304.08552].

sources.summary: WISEA J235250.49+284555.2 is noted as an elliptical near AGC 333576 at similar velocity that may participate in the system interactions despite not being an ALFALFA parent.

quotes.quote: In the course of investigating this system, we have also found the elliptical galaxy to the upper left of AGC 333576, WISEA J235250.49+284555.2. Not much is known about this galaxy, and it should be noted that this galaxy has been mistakenly cross-identified with AGC 333576 in the NASA/IPAC Extragalactic Database, so some of the information listed there is actually data associated with the AD. It has a measured heliocentric velocity of 7157 24 km s ^ 1 Huchra2012 , which places it at a similar distance to the other two galaxies in this system, and it is 105 0.125 away from AGC 333576 on the sky, or roughly 48 2 kpc. Because WISEA J235250.49+284555.2 is an elliptical, it likely did not have enough HI gas to be included in the ALFALFA catalog and thus be identified as a potential parent galaxy; however, it may still be involved in the interactions of this system. lr@ lr ht 1 Property [ Units ] & 1 @ Value & 1 Property [ Units ] & 1 Value RA [ h m s, J2000 ] & 23 52 43.6 & Dec [ ^ ' ", J2000 ] & +28 44 42.5 V_ h ' [ km s ^ 1 ] & 7031 13 & N_ HI,peak [ 10^ 20 cm ^ 2 ] & 5.89 S_ 21 ' [ Jy km s ^ 1 ] & 0.60 0.04 & S_ 21 [ Jy km s ^ 1 ] & 0.64 0.05 M_ HI ' [ 10^9 M_ ] & 1.25 0.19 & M_ HI [ 10^9 M_ ] & 1.33 0.21 M_ gas ' [ 10^9 M_ ] & 1.66 0.25 & M_ gas [ 10^9 M_ ] & 1.77 0.28 R_ HI [ kpc ] & 16.73 1.57 & Inclination [ ^ ] & 57 2 R_e [ kpc ] & 4.40 0.05 & R_d [ kpc ] & 3.94 0.04 _ g,0 [ mag arcsec ^ 2 ] & 24.57 0.02 & _ ,0 [ mag arcsec ^ 2 ] & 24.45 0.02 m_g [ mag ] & 18.48 0.02 & m_r [ mag ] & 18.27 0.02 M_g [ mag ] & -16.38 0.10 & M_r [ mag ] & -16.60 0.10 (g-) [ mag ] & 0.21 0.03 & M_ * [ 10^8 M_ ] & 1.64 0.12 M_ bary ' [ 10^9 M_ ] & 1.82 0.25 & M_ dyn [ 10^9 M_ ] & 1.90 0.37 M_ HI '/L_g & 3.18 0.59 & M_ HI '/L_r & 4.00 0.75 M_ HI '/M_ * & 7.60 1.28 & M_ dyn /M_ gas & 1.07 0.27 RA and Dec are listed for the optical component of AGC 333576. Properties marked with a ' are derived from ALFALFA measurements.

gmw_00010557.name: XMMSL1 J002202.9+254004
gmw_00010557.overview: XMMSL1 J002202.9+254004 is a variable source that exhibits variability in both the X-ray and optical bands [arXiv:1011.3515]. Its high X-ray-to-optical flux ratio suggests it may be a blazar, although the lack of a radio association casts doubt on this classification [arXiv:1011.3515].

sources.summary: The paper describes XMMSL1 J002202.9+254004 as a variable source whose X-ray and optical behavior and high X-ray-to-optical flux ratio suggest a possible blazar, though lacking radio support.

quotes.quote: Four of the XRT-detected sources show significant X-ray variability on the basis of multiple XRT observations, on time scales of days to months and by factors of 1.4--2. These are XMMSL1 J002202.9 + 254004, XMMSL1 J094551.3 194352, XMMSL1 J095336.4 + 161231 and XMMSL1 J175542.2 + 624903.

XMMSL1 J002202.9+254004 Two Swift pointed observations were taken 10 months apart, during which we see significant variability in both the X-ray (count rate increasing by a factor of 5) and optical (an increase of 1 magnitude) bands. This, together with a high X-ray to optical flux ratio, suggest this is a blazar whose X-ray emission is boosted by contributions from a jet, while the lack of a radio association casts doubt on this classification.

gmw_00010558.name: XMMSL1 J064109.2-565542
gmw_00010558.overview: XMMSL1 J064109.2-565542 is an active galactic nucleus located at a redshift of 0.368 [arXiv:1011.3515]. It is classified as a broad-line active galactic nucleus based on the detection of a broad H-beta emission line [arXiv:1011.3515].

sources.summary: The paper presents optical spectroscopy of XMMSL1 J064109.2-565542 and classifies it as a broad-line AGN at redshift 0.368 from broad H and oxygen emission lines.

quotes.quote: XMMSL1 J064109.2-565542 An optical spectrum for this source was obtained at the NTT, La Silla (Chile) on 2010 March 8. Observing conditions were good with sky transparency clear to photometric and a seeing of 1 '' . Two exposures of 600 s each at an airmass of 1.1 were made with the ESO Faint Object Spectrograph and Camera (EFOSC2) with a 1 '' slit width oriented at the parallactic angle and grating 4, covering the wavelength range from 4500--7500 \ at 12 \ resolution (from unblended arc lines taken through the slit at 6000 ). A standard reduction process was applied using IRAF routines. Wavelength calibration was carried out by comparison with exposures of an Helium-Argon arc lamp, with an accuracy <1 .

Relative flux calibration was carried out by observations of the spectrophotometric standard star LTT 3218 Hamuy . We estimate an error on the flux calibration < 10 from the standard adjustment during the calibration procedure. The spectrum has been corrected for Galactic reddening. The object is classified as a broad-line AGN, based on the detection of a broad (FWHM > 1000 km s ^-1 ) H 4861 \ line, at a redshift of 0.368 0.001 (from the detection of the [ OII ] 3727, [ OIII ] 4959 and [ OIII ] 5007 emission lines) as shown in Figure spectrum .

gmw_00010559.name: XMMSL1 J095336.4+161231
gmw_00010559.overview: XMMSL1 J095336.4+161231 is a Type I active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1702.06807] and a known quasar at a redshift of z = 0.87 [arXiv:1011.3515]. The source exhibits variable X-ray flux [arXiv:1011.3515, arXiv:1702.06807] and may be radiating close to its Eddington limit [arXiv:1702.06807].

sources.summary: The paper identifies XMMSL1 J095336.4+161231 with a known z=0.87 quasar, noting XRT and UVOT detections, AGN-like fluxes, and year-scale X-ray fading.

quotes.quote: XMMSL1 J095336.4+161231 This source is detected with XRT and UVOT. The X-ray spectral shape is poorly constrained, but the X-ray and optical fluxes are of the same order suggesting this is an AGN. At the XRT position there is a known quasar: SDSS J095336.86 + 161228.8 which lies at a redshift of z = 0.87 Adelman . The XRT observations span 1 year, during which time the source halved in X-ray flux.

sources.summary: The paper identifies XMMSL1 J095336.4+161231 as a Type I AGN whose black hole mass and bolometric luminosity suggest near-Eddington emission, with variable X-ray flux and a relatively narrow broad H line.

quotes.quote: The AGN from the unidentified Slew sample which were confirmed elsewhere, XMMSL1 J095336.4+161231 and XMMSL1 J175542.2+624903, are also of Type I.

The implied black hole mass and bolometric luminosity of one of our sources, XMMSL1 J095336.4+161231, suggest it could be radiating close to the Eddington limit. The X-ray flux of this AGN is well constrained in the combined XRT spectrum, but is variable over the two observations by a factor of 1.1--3.0 in 16 months (with no change in hardness ratio) and is not contemporaneous with the pre-2003 USNO-B1.0 Monet I-band magnitude.

In contrast to most other estimates reported here, the H broad line FWHM for XMMSL1 J095336.4+161231 has been taken from a published fit using only a single Gaussian Alam and at FWHM = 3452 km s ^ 1 is relatively narrow among our sources.

gmw_00010560.name: XMMSL1 J175542.2+624903
gmw_00010560.overview: XMMSL1 J175542.2+624903 is a type 1 active galactic nucleus located at a redshift of z = 0.236 [arXiv:1011.3515]. The source is coincident with a ROSAT-discovered source and exhibits X-ray variability [arXiv:1011.3515].

sources.summary: The paper describes XMMSL1 J175542.2+624903 as a variable X-ray source coincident with a ROSAT source and catalogued as a z=0.236 type 1 AGN.

quotes.quote: XMMSL1 J175542.2+624903 This source was observed twice by both with Swift and XMM-Newton and shows X-ray variability. This source is coincident with 1RXS J175546.2 + 624927 discovered by ROSAT . It is listed in the Large Quasar Astrometric Catalogue Souchay as being at redshift z = 0.236 , and the same source is recorded in the optical identification of ROSAT FSC sources Mickaelian , ROSAT NEP X-ray source catalog Henry and ROSAT North Ecliptic Pole Survey Gioia classed as type 1 AGN. The RASS flux at the XRT position is a factor of 2 lower than during the XRT observation. The source type indicators presented in this paper are consistent with this classification.

gmw_00010561.name: WISEA J034638.04+431348.3
gmw_00010561.overview: WISEA J034638.04+431348.3 is a faint, low-mass dwarf galaxy located at a distance of approximately 140.4 Mpc [arXiv:2206.01662]. It is characterized by a very low star formation rate and a low stellar mass [arXiv:2206.01662].
