We detect the \ emission which we associated with S1 and S2 galaxies. The non-detected galaxies are small compact dwarfs and we show that one galaxy, S4, is an \ deficient galaxy. The likely reason for deficiency in S4 is the tidal interaction with S2, see Figure fig:j1250_multiw . The two most luminous galaxies in J1250-20, S1 and S2 are interacting, see Figure fig:j1250_multiw . These galaxies have a comparable stellar masses however, they have vastly different \ mass.

The \ contours that we associate with S2 galaxies overlap also on top of its smaller companion with which its interacting, S4. In this work we associate \ emission to S2, as S4 is physically smaller, has stellar mass 0.9 dex smaller, and has a lower specific star-formation. The \ mass extracted from the region between the S1 and S2 galaxies is 10 ^ 8.9 M _ . This is a lower limit of the \ mass between galaxies as there is uncertainty where to place galaxy `borders'. The \ velocity of the extracted region corresponds to the recessional group velocity (in velocity space between S1 and S2), and in this region we do not detect faint stellar streams.

gmw_00010648.name: NGC 3643
gmw_00010648.overview: NGC 3643 is a galaxy located at a distance of approximately 29.4 to 29.96 megaparsecs [arXiv:2306.11788]. It has a redshift of z = 0.005811 and served as the host galaxy for a young Type Ia supernova [arXiv:2306.11788].

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 3643 as the host galaxy and adopts a distance near 30 Mpc after comparing redshift-derived, literature, and velocity-field estimates.

quotes.quote: Its host galaxy, NGC 3643, has a redshift of z = 0.005811 vanDriel16 and a distance of 29.4 2.1 Mpc (measured using the NASA/IPAC Extragalactic Database, NED http://ned.ipac.caltech.edu/ ). It was promptly classified as a young SN Ia with strong C features Burket05:05P . High-cadence photometry starting immediately after discovery revealed a luminous, day-long flash, which was interpreted as interaction with circumstellar material CSM; Jiang+21_SN20hvf . The early light-curve evolution and photospheric spectra of are similar to 03fg-like SNe Ia Jiang+21_SN20hvf, Srivastav23 . The data presented here extend the spectroscopic coverage of to 332 days after peak brightness. We present several late-time, nebular spectra, which are qualitatively similar to that of 03fg-like SNe Ia. Examining the nebular line profiles in detail, we find three distinct kinematic components, indicating an asymmetric explosion and a surviving WD. We present our observations in Section s:obs . In Section s:anal , we examine our full data set. We discuss our results in Section s:disc and conclude in Section s:conc . We obtained a total of 4 optical spectra of that range from 240 to 332 days after maximum light. Two spectra were acquired with the Kast spectrograph Miller93 on the Lick Shane telescope and two spectra were acquired with the LRIS with the Low-Resolution Imaging Spectrometer LRIS; Oke95 , mounted on the 10-meter Keck I telescope at the W. \ M. \ Keck Observatory. The Kast spectra were obtained on 2021 Jan 6 and 2021 Feb 6 UT and were both observed with the 452/3306 grism and 300/7500 grating. These observations used the 2 wide slit and d57 dichroic and cover a wavelength range of 3,255 -- 10,893 . The LRIS observations were obtained with two different setups. On 2021 Feb 12 UT, we observed a low-resolution setting (1800 and 1430 s blue channel exposures with the B600/4000 grism) and a high-resolution red channel setting (two 825 s exposures with the R1200/7500 grating, with a pixel scale of 0.4 /pixel). The blue channel and red channel cover spectra cover 3,141 -- 5,640 and 6,210 -- 7,800 , respectively. On 2021 Apr 8 UT we observed an identical blue channel setup, but obtained two 525 s red channel exposures with the R400/8500 grating, with pixel scales of 0.63 and 1.16 /pixel, respectively). This low-resolution spectrum covers 3,162 -- 10,147 . We used the 1.0 wide slit and the D560 dichroic for all observations and oriented the slit to include the host-galaxy nucleus. The atmospheric dispersion corrector unit was deployed. Each of these spectra includes H , He 1 6678, [ O 1 ] 6300, 6364 and the 7300 \ line complex, the primary focus of our current analysis. All data were reduced using the UCSC Spectral Pipeline https://github.com/msiebert1/UCSC_spectral_pipeline which makes use of standard iraf IRAF is distributed by the National Optical Astronomy Observatory, which is operated by the Association of Universities for Research in Astronomy (AURA) under a cooperative agreement with the National Science Foundation. and python routines for bias/overscan corrections, flat fielding, flux calibration, and telluric lines removal, using spectro-photometric standard star spectra, obtained the same night Silverman12:bsnip . We present these spectra in f:neb_spec . Distance and Luminosity NGC 3643 is close enough that a redshift-derived distance could be significantly biased. However, there are no redshift-independent distance measurements for NGC 3643 in the literature.

Literature values for NGC 3643 under different peculiar velocities and Hubble constant assumptions result in similar results of = 32.1 mag Kourkchi17, Leroy19 . Using the Numerical Acion Methods velocity field reconstruction Shaya17 for the Cosmicflows-3 catalog Kourkchi20 through the Extragalactic Distance Database Tully09 and assuming H_ 0 = 73.04 km s ^ 1 Mpc ^ 1 Riess16, Riess18:gaia , we find = 32.38 0.13 mag ( D = 29.96 Mpc), which we consider the best value for the distance to .

gmw_00010649.name: Phoenix
gmw_00010649.overview: Phoenix is the brightest cluster galaxy of a massive, X-ray luminous galaxy cluster [arXiv:1611.02562]. While previous studies estimated its star formation rate to be up to 1000 solar masses per year, accounting for internal extinction suggests a lower rate of 454 to 494 solar masses per year [arXiv:1611.02562].

sources.summary: The paper treats Phoenix as the BCG of the Phoenix cluster, fitting its SED to constrain extreme star formation and favoring lower SFRs when extinction priors are applied.

quotes.quote: We use our Bayesian-motivated spectral energy distribution (SED)-fitting code, , to estimate the plausible SFR values in the brightest cluster galaxy of a massive, X-ray luminous galaxy cluster, Phoenix. Previous studies of Phoenix have resulted in the highest measurement of SFR for any galaxy, with the estimates reaching up to 1000 . However, a very small number of models have been considered in those studies.

We consider two models for star formation history, instantaneous bursts and continuous star formation, a wide range of ages for the old and the young stellar population, along with other discrete parameters, such as the initial mass function, metallicities, internal extinction and extinction law. We find that in the absence of any prior except that the maximum cooling rate < 3000 , the SFR lies in the range ( 2230-2890 ) . If we impose an observational prior on the internal extinction, E(B-V) 0.6 , the best-fit SFR lies in ( 454-494 ) , and we consider this as the most probable range of SFR values for Phoenix. The SFR dependance on the extinction is a reflection of the standard age-extinction degeneracy, which can be overcome by using a prior on one of the two quantities in question.

gmw_00010650.name: J0921+0007
gmw_00010650.overview: J0921+0007 is an optically faint, X-ray-bright quasar at a redshift of z=6.56 [arXiv:2211.13820, arXiv:2111.03073]. It exhibits properties similar to a narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy, hosting a low-mass supermassive black hole that accretes at a super-Eddington rate [arXiv:2211.13820].

sources.summary: The paper confirms J0921+0007 as a z=6.56 X-ray-bright, optically faint quasar with NLS1-like properties, low black-hole mass, super-Eddington accretion, and steep X-ray spectrum.

quotes.quote: Here we present the eROSITA X-ray detection of a second high-redshift quasar in the eFEDS field: the z=6.56 quasar J0921+0007, initially discovered in a dedicated survey based on the Hyper Suprime Cam (HSC) Subaru Strategic Program SSP; aihara22 : the Subaru High-z Exploration of Low-luminosity Quasars SHELLQs; matsuoka18 . The source is optically faint but X-ray bright. Its optical and near-infrared (NIR) spectral properties potentially make it a high-redshift NLS1. We present the eROSITA detection of this optically faint source with a 21 ks Chandra ACIS-S follow-up observation and derive its X-ray properties in Sect. sec:det .

At z=6.56 , J0921+0007 is the most distant X-ray-selected AGN to date and can therefore be used to impose constraints on the high-z XLF. We derived a comparatively low black hole mass for a sample of high-redshift quasars with comparable optical/UV luminosity, see e.g. onoue19 , which implies that the source is accreting at a super-Eddington rate. The values reported in this work ( M_ BH = (2.5 0.3) 10^8 M_ and = 2.3^ +0.4 _ 0.3 ) are consistent with the typical properties of local NLS1s e.g. sulentic00,collin04,rakshit17 . We obtained a relatively steep power-law fit to the X-ray spectrum of the source: = 3.2 .

sources.summary: The paper lists HSC J0921+0007 as a known z=6.56 QSO whose low classifier probability is tied to brown-dwarf-like optical colors and WISE-band flux discrepancies.

quotes.quote: Among the classified sources there are eight known high- z QSOs that were found in the VIKING survey area from past works: DELS J1217+0131 ( z=6.17 ; Banados16 ; Wang17 ), ATLAS J025.6821-33.4627 ( z=6.31 , hereafter J0142-3327; Carnall15 ), HSC J1137+0045 ( z=6.4 ; Matsuoka19a ), J0148-2826 ( z=6.54 ; Yang20a ), HSC J0921+0007 ( z=6.56 ; Matsuoka18b ), VIK J0305-3400 ( z=6.604 ; Venemans13 ), DELS J1048-0109 ( z=6.63 ; Wang17 ), HSC J1205-0000 ( z=6.74 ; Matsuoka16 ). These sources and their main properties are listed in Table tab:known_qsos , while their probabilities of being high- z QSOs are pinpointed with red arrows in Fig. fig:probabilities .

In the range 6.5 z 7 , as reported in Table tab:known_qsos and displayed in Fig. fig:probabilities (middle panel), our method is able to recover one QSO (based on our P_ th =7 ), VIK J0305-3400 ( P_ th 24.5 ), while the other four are consistent with being contaminants ( P_ QSO 10^ 2 ). Among them, J0921+0007 ( P_ QSO 10^ 4 ) is also a HSC selected QSO ( J=20.9 ) that has similar optical colors to Galactic brown dwarfs ( Matsuoka18b ). Adopting the same procedure as described above to generate 10,000 contaminants and 6.5 z 7 QSOs for each known QSO, we show in Fig. fig:known_65_7 the deconvolved relative-flux relative-flux contours for the simulated contaminants (black) and high- z QSOs (blue), compared to the properties of the known 6.5 z 7 QSOs from the VIKING survey area.

Also in this case, it is apparent that the relative fluxes of the four QSOs with P_ QSO 10^ 2 are inconsistent with the deconvolved QSO model properties (blue contours in Fig. fig:known_65_7 ) in some sub-plots: 1) J0148-2826 is inconsistent with panels showing f_ H , f_ W1 , and f_ W2 , 2) HSC J0921+0007 is inconsistent with panels showing f_ W1 , and f_ W2 , 3) DELS J1048-0109 is not consistent with panels showing f_ H , and f_ W2 , and 4) HSC J1205-0000 is not consistent with the QSO distribution in any panel. We provide a more detailed discussion of these discrepancies between real and simulated QSO properties in deviations .

gmw_00010651.name: J123726+621128
gmw_00010651.overview: J123726+621128 is an extended radio galaxy that exhibits a wide-angle-tail morphology [arXiv:1705.03766]. The galaxy features polarized twin jets and lobes, which produce strong polarized emission at 5.5 GHz [arXiv:1705.03766].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J123726+621128 as one of two extended polarized GOODS-N radio sources, with wide-angle-tail morphology, polarized twin jets and lobes, and strong 5.5 GHz polarized emission consistent with 1.5 GHz measurements.

quotes.quote: In particular J123726+621128 has a wide-angle tail (WAT) morphology with polarisation detected in the twin jets and in both lobes as shown in Fig. fig-pol .

They detected 14 radio sources; two are in the field of view covered by our observations: J123644.3+621132 and J123725.9+621128 (wrongly named as J1237744.1+621128 in Table 1 in 2014ApJ...785...45R ) and both show polarised emission at 5.5 GHz. These are the two extended sources discussed above, and the sources with the strongest polarised emission at 5.5 GHz. Taking into account that the synthesized beam of the 1.5 GHz observations is 1.6 1.6 arcsec ^2 (compared to 0.56 0.47 arcsec ^2 of our 5.5 GHz observations), the amount of polarised flux detected at the two frequencies is consistent.

gmw_00010652.name: J123644+621133
gmw_00010652.overview: J123644+621133 is a Fanaroff-Riley Class I radio galaxy [arXiv:0706.3777, arXiv:1705.03766] that hosts an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:0706.3777]. It exhibits a classical core-plus-jet structure [arXiv:1705.03766] with jet-dominated radio emission [arXiv:0706.3777] and strong polarized emission at 5.5 GHz [arXiv:1705.03766].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J123644+621133 as an FR I radio galaxy with extended jets, compact radio-core evidence for AGN activity, and jet-dominated radio emission among radio-X-ray crossmatched sources.

quotes.quote: J123644+621133 is an FR 1 Fanaroff74 radio galaxy with jets extending over 12 .

Extremely compact radio cores (brightness temperature ( >10^5 - 10^6 ) K) were detected in J123642+621331, J123644+621133, J123646+621404 and J123652+121444 using the European VLBI Network (EVN) and global VLBI Garrett01, Chi06 , confirming the presence of an AGN. J123644+621133 is unmistakably an FR 1.

Figure LRLXBAUER.PS also shows that 2 out of 4 radio AGN with L_ X < 10^ 35 W lie close to the starburst-based relationships and the other 2 are under-luminous in X-rays (although one of these, the source with the lowest value of L_ X /L_ R , is the FR 1 J123644+621133, the only radio-X-ray crossmatched source to have jet-dominated radio emission).

sources.summary: The paper describes J123644+621133 as the other extended polarized GOODS-N galaxy, showing classical FRI core-plus-jet structure and among the strongest 5.5 GHz polarized emission in the field.

quotes.quote: The second extended, polarised source, J123644+621133, represents the other galaxy in this field showing the classical FRI structure (core+jets).

They detected 14 radio sources; two are in the field of view covered by our observations: J123644.3+621132 and J123725.9+621128 (wrongly named as J1237744.1+621128 in Table 1 in 2014ApJ...785...45R ) and both show polarised emission at 5.5 GHz. These are the two extended sources discussed above, and the sources with the strongest polarised emission at 5.5 GHz. Taking into account that the synthesized beam of the 1.5 GHz observations is 1.6 1.6 arcsec ^2 (compared to 0.56 0.47 arcsec ^2 of our 5.5 GHz observations), the amount of polarised flux detected at the two frequencies is consistent.

gmw_00010653.name: PSO J347.2702+48.70
gmw_00010653.overview: PSO J347.2702+48.70 is a massive host galaxy of a fast radio burst located at a luminosity distance of 362.4 Mpc [arXiv:2308.12118]. It has a redshift of z=0.0771, moderate star formation, and a composite BPT classification [arXiv:2308.12118].

sources.summary: The paper identifies PSO J347.2702+48.70 as the FRB host: a z=0.0771 massive galaxy with moderate star formation, 362.4 Mpc luminosity distance, and composite BPT classification.

quotes.quote: \ was localised to a host galaxy with redshift z=0.0771(1) by the Deep Synoptic Array (DSA-110) during its commissioning phase ravi_2023_apjl . The host, PSO J347.2702+48.70, is a massive galaxy (log M _*=10.0(1) ) with moderate star formation (SFR 0.1 M _ yr ^ 1 ), not unlike other FRB host galaxies gordon_2023_arxiv . The host galaxy is located at a luminosity distance of 362.4(1) Mpc and classified as composite in the Baldwin, Phillips Terlevich (BPT) scheme.

gmw_00010654.name: IRAS F12514+1027
gmw_00010654.overview: IRAS F12514+1027 is a hyperluminous infrared galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.30 [arXiv:astro-ph/0211166]. It hosts both a starburst and a highly obscured type II quasar, with its soft X-ray spectrum exhibiting thermal emission from the starburst [arXiv:astro-ph/0211166].

sources.summary: The paper describes IRAS F12514+1027 as a detected hyperluminous infrared galaxy whose X-ray spectrum indicates starburst emission plus a highly obscured type II quasar with a Compton-thick line of sight.

quotes.quote: IRAS F12514+1027 ( z=0.30 ) is detected in 18.6 ks with 130 EPIC-pn counts over 0.2-12 . The soft X-ray spectrum exhibits thermal emission from the starburst, with T 0.3 and L(0.5-2.0 )=2.1 10^ 42 . With its Fe K-edge, the flat continuum above 2 is interpreted as cold reflection from a hidden AGN of intrinsic L(2-10 ) 1.8 10^ 44 . Comparison with the infrared power requires that the X-ray reflector subtend 2 /5 sr at the central engine.

IRAS F12514+1027 therefore comprises a highly obscured (i.e. type II) quasar, together with a starburst of comparable luminosity. The starburst itself may constitute the obscuring medium, as proposed by Fabian et al. (1998) to account for the high solid angle obscuration required by the hard X-ray background population.

gmw_00010655.name: IRAS F00235+1024
gmw_00010655.overview: IRAS F00235+1024 is a hyperluminous infrared galaxy at a redshift of z=0.575 [arXiv:astro-ph/0211166]. It is an interacting system containing both a starburst and an obscured active galactic nucleus [arXiv:astro-ph/0211166].

sources.summary: The paper describes IRAS F00235+1024 as an undetected hyperluminous infrared galaxy whose SED implies a starburst and obscured quasar, with X-ray limits requiring Compton-thick direct obscuration.

quotes.quote: In this paper we present XMM-Newton EPIC imaging spectroscopy of two HyLIRGs with ROSAT non-detections in Wilman et al. (1998): IRAS F00235+1024 ( z=0.575 ; L_ Bol =1.85 10^ 13 h_ 50 ^ 2 ) and F12514+1027 ( z=0.30 ; L_ Bol =1.51 10^ 13 h_ 50 ^ 2 ). The former has a starburst optical spectrum and HST imaging shows an interacting system (Farrah et al. 2002a). The latest SED models (Farrah et al. 2002b) imply that the starburst (with star formation rate 1000 ) and AGN contribute equally to the total infrared emission, and that the AGN dust-torus is seen almost edge on.

No source is detected at the position of IRAS F00235+1024 (RA 00 26 06.7, Dec + 10 41 27.6; J2000.0) in either the EPIC pn or MOS cameras, but we confine our discussion to the more sensitive pn observations.

Since the SED modelling suggests the existence of an obscured quasar of L_ Bol =9.1 10^ 12 , corresponding to L(2-10 )=10^ 45 (assuming as above the Elvis et al. 1994 hard X-ray--bolometric conversion), Table 2 implies that this quasar is completely obscured in direct light by a Compton-thick (N_ H > 1.5 10^ 24 ) absorber.

gmw_00010656.name: J033226.23-274222.8
gmw_00010656.overview: J033226.23-274222.8 is a late-type spiral galaxy at redshift z=0.6679 with an extended disk and a small central bulge [arXiv:0711.0611]. The system is undergoing a minor merger with an infalling satellite, which has produced surrounding debris and shock-heated gas [arXiv:0711.0611].

sources.summary: The paper presents J033226.23-274222.8 as a late-type spiral at z=0.6679 undergoing a minor merger, with debris, an infalling satellite, and an off-center shock signature.

quotes.quote: In Fig. fig1 , we show a B-V-z composite HST/ACS image, which presents an extended view of J033226.23-274222.8. J033226.23-274222.8 is a late-type spiral galaxy with a small red central bulge (B/T=0.05) and an extended disk (half light radius R_ half =9.47kpc, see Neichel07 ). One can note another galaxy located 5 arcsec SSE: unfortunately, we were not able to retrieve any valuable redshift information for this galaxy, but the presence of a lot of debris surrounding J033226.23-274222.8 suggests that these two galaxies might be in the first stage of an interaction.

The gas map shows a peak off-centered from the dynamical center, and close to the infalling satellite. Such a peak cannot be attributed to rotation and is interpreted as the signature of shock-heated gas during the collision. We derive a total SFR 21 M_ /yr and a rough stellar mass ratio estimate 1:18, in agreement with this kind of events. Therefore, J033226.23-274222.8 seems to be the first detection of a minor merger at z 0.6 , and more generally at high redshift.

gmw_00010657.name: NGC 5073
gmw_00010657.overview: NGC 5073 is a distant galaxy characterized by a warped, lopsided disk [arXiv:2210.12255] and a strong nuclear source [arXiv:2403.05709]. Its emission is dominated by this nucleus, which has been classified as a starburst [arXiv:2403.05709] and may host an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2403.05709].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 5073 as distant, nuclear-source dominated, possibly AGN/starburst, and essentially unresolved; a faint disk remains and both contours and profiles show no radio halo.

quotes.quote: NGC 5073: This is one of the more distant galaxies in our sample, and its emission is primarily concentrated in a strong nuclear source, removed before analysis. It was studied in the past by , who could not make any conclusions because the galaxy was practically unresolved in the images. They also suggest that it could host an AGN, but a recent study made by in which they study the relation between starbursts and AGNs, this galaxy is classified as a starburst.

The remaining emission consists of a faint disk. Both the radio contour map and the z profile indicate the absence of a radio halo. To accurately describe the profile, a single exponential function was utilized. The resulting disk scale height was found to be particularly small, indicating that this galaxy is essentially unresolved, as well as in previous images.

sources.summary: The paper reports that NGC 5073 is warped and lopsided, causing strong quadrupolar signal in both the central and outer disc regions of its mid-plane asymmetry map.

quotes.quote: For NGC 3628, NGC 4710, NGC 5073, and NGC 5529 the mid-plane asymmetry maps are strongly affected by a quadrupolar signal (which affects at least half the extent of the galaxy itself). This signal implies that we were unable to correctly identify the disc PA, despite our careful analysis. Visual inspection of the S ^4 G images of these galaxies and corresponding unsharp masked images (Fig.  fig:sample , middle panels) identifies strong and intrinsically asymmetric features in the discs (such as warps and/or lopsided discs) which may have affected the measurement of the disc PA.

NGC 5073 is warped at |x| 75 arcsec, and lopsided, being more extended on the left side. A resulting strong quadrupolar signal appears both in the central and in the outer regions of the disc, with an opposite sign, in the mid-plane asymmetry map.

gmw_00010658.name: ESO 443-042
gmw_00010658.overview: ESO 443-042 is a highly inclined S0/a galaxy that hosts an X-shaped boxy or peanut-shaped bulge [arXiv:2210.12255]. The galaxy's outer disc shows evidence of warping, but there are no evident asymmetries in its surface brightness [arXiv:2210.12255].

sources.summary: The paper finds ESO 443-042 is a highly inclined S0/a galaxy with an X-shaped B/P bulge and outer warping, but no evident surface-brightness asymmetry.

quotes.quote: ESO 443-042 is a highly-inclined S0/a galaxy, hosting an X-shaped structure (visible in the unsharp mask in Fig.  fig:sample ) associated with a B/P bulge Buta2015 . The disc shows evidence of warping. The corresponding distortion is located in the very outer region and is responsible for the weak quadrupolar signal visible at |x| 17.3 kpc in the mid-plane asymmetry map. The mid-plane asymmetry diagnostics are shown in Fig.  fig:sample and Fig.  fig:AMP_real_galaxies , bottom-left panel.

The red contours in the mid-plane asymmetry map highlight the X-shaped structure, extending to x 7 kpc. No asymmetries in the surface brightness are evident. The corresponding mid-plane asymmetry profile is calculated adopting z_ min =0.9 kpc and z_ max =3.3 kpc; it remains flat over the entire range (i.e., x < 13.8 kpc).

gmw_00010659.name: NGC52
gmw_00010659.overview: NGC52 is an edge-on galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.01817 [arXiv:2306.08646]. It hosts an active nucleus observed through a thick layer of interstellar matter, with optical diagnostics suggesting a Seyfert 2 or LINER classification [arXiv:2306.08646].

sources.summary: The paper identifies edge-on galaxy NGC52 as an X-ray counterpart with redshift, infrared color, radio association, weak optical emission lines, and Seyfert 2/LINER-like active-nucleus diagnostics.

quotes.quote: There is the edge-on galaxy NGC52 in the \ and \ position error circles (Fig. fig:guid_images ). It is located at redshift z=0.01817 (according to the SIMBAD database) and has an infrared color W1-W2=0.26 . The radio source NVSS J001440+183455 can also be associated with this object. Weak narrow H and [ NII ] 6583 emission lines are seen in the optical spectrum (Fig. fig:spec0014_0107 , Table tab:spec_lines ).

The narrow-line flux ratio 2ha= 0.43 0.10 points to the presence of an active nucleus in the galaxy, according to the BPT diagram (see Fig. chart:bpt ), while the absence of a broad component in the H line suggests that this is a Seyfert 2 (Sy2) galaxy. In principle, the absence of the [ OIII ] 5007 and H emission lines supposes that this is a LINER object, but this is highly unlikely, taking into account the object's high X-ray luminosity ( 3 10^ 42 erg s ^ 1 in the 4--12 keV energy band). The weakness of the emission lines in the optical spectrum probably stems from the fact that the active nucleus is observed through a thick layer of interstellar matter in the galaxy.

gmw_00010660.name: WISEA J002243.69+804346.1
gmw_00010660.overview: WISEA J002243.69+804346.1 is an extended optical and infrared active galactic nucleus located at a redshift of z=0.1147 [arXiv:2306.08646]. It is classified as a Seyfert 1 galaxy due to the presence of broad Balmer emission lines, and its X-ray spectrum shows only slight intrinsic absorption [arXiv:2306.08646].

sources.summary: The paper identifies WISEA J002243.69+804346.1 as an extended AGN-like optical/infrared object with broad Balmer lines, redshift z=0.1147, Seyfert 1 classification, and slight X-ray absorption.

quotes.quote: There is the extended optical and infrared object WISEA J002243.69+804346.1 (Fig. fig:guid_images ) with a color W1-W2=0.61 typical for AGNs in the \ and \ position error circles. Balmer emission lines, broad H and H , are observed in the galaxy's spectrum (Fig. fig:spec0014_0107 , Table tab:spec_lines ). The forbidden [ OIII ] 4959, [ OIII ] 5007 lines are also present. The redshift of the source is z=0.1147 0.0013 .

Against the background of the broad H line it is impossible to distinguish its narrow component and the narrow [ NII ] 6583 line. In the case of H we can set only an upper limit on the flux in the narrow component and, accordingly, a 2 lower limit on the flux ratio, 3hb > 0.6 . However, the presence of broad H and H components allows us to say with confidence that this is a Seyfert 1 (Sy1) galaxy. In the X-ray spectrum there is evidence only for slight intrinsic absorption ( 10^ 21 cm ^ 2 ).

gmw_00010661.name: WISEA J010743.11+574417.7
gmw_00010661.overview: WISEA J010743.11+574417.7 is an extended optical and infrared active galactic nucleus located at a redshift of z = 0.06992 [arXiv:2306.08646]. Classified as a Seyfert 1.9 galaxy with a composite optical spectrum, the object shows no detected absorption in its X-ray spectrum [arXiv:2306.08646].

sources.summary: The paper identifies WISEA J010743.11+574417.7 as an extended AGN-colored source with optical emission lines, redshift z=0.06992, Sy1.9 classification, composite-line placement, and no significant X-ray absorption.

quotes.quote: There is the extended optical and infrared object WISEA J010743.11+574417.7 (Fig. fig:guid_images ) with a color W1-W2=0.78 typical for AGNs in the \ and \ position error circles. A broad H line and narrow H , [ OIII ] 4959, [ OIII ] 5007, H , and [ NII ] 6583 lines are observed in the optical spectrum (Fig. fig:spec0014_0107 , Table tab:spec_lines ). The redshift of the source is z = 0.06992 0.00030 .

The line flux ratios 3hb = 0.50 0.12 and 2ha = 0.70 0.08 , according to the BPT diagram (Fig. chart:bpt ), and the presence of a broad H component with FWHM > 2000 km s ^ 1 with the absence of a broad H component allow the object to be classified as Sy1.9. On the BPT diagram the source falls into the region of galaxies with a composite spectrum most likely because we cannot reliably distinguish the narrow H and [ NII ] 6583 lines for it. No significant absorption was revealed in the X-ray spectrum.

gmw_00010662.name: 2MASS J02122646+5209533
gmw_00010662.overview: 2MASS J02122646+5209533 is an extended optical and infrared active galactic nucleus located at a redshift of z = 0.23810 [arXiv:2306.08646]. It is classified as a Seyfert 1 galaxy with broad Balmer emission lines and no detected absorption in its X-ray spectrum [arXiv:2306.08646].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 2MASS J02122646+5209533 as an extended AGN-colored counterpart with broad Balmer and forbidden lines, redshift z=0.23810, Seyfert 1 classification, and no significant X-ray absorption.

quotes.quote: There is the extended optical and infrared object 2MASS J02122646+5209533 (Fig. fig:guid_images ) with a color W1-W2=0.89 typical for AGNs in the \ and \ position error circles. Balmer emission lines with broad H and H components and narrow forbidden [ OIII ] 4959, [ OIII ] 5007, [ NII ] 6548, and [ NII ] 6583 lines are seen in the optical spectrum (Fig. fig:spec0212_0252 , Table tab:spec_lines ). The measured redshift is z = 0.23810 0.00011 . The narrow-line flux ratios 2ha= 0.39 0.05 and 3hb = 0.87 0.11 (Fig. chart:bpt ) and the presence of broad H and H components allow the object to be classified as Sy1. No significant absorption was revealed in the X-ray spectrum.

gmw_00010663.name: WISEA J025209.64+482959.4
gmw_00010663.overview: WISEA J025209.64+482959.4 is an extended optical and infrared active galactic nucleus located at a redshift of z = 0.03366 [arXiv:2306.08646]. It is classified as a Seyfert 1.9 galaxy and exhibits moderate absorption in its X-ray spectrum [arXiv:2306.08646].

sources.summary: The paper identifies WISEA J025209.64+482959.4 as an extended AGN-colored counterpart with multiple optical emission lines, redshift z=0.03366, Sy1.9 classification, and moderate X-ray absorption.

quotes.quote: There is the extended optical and infrared object WISEA J025209.64+482959.4 (Fig. fig:guid_images ) with a color W1-W2=0.71 typical for AGNs in the \ and \ position error circles. Emission lines are seen in the optical spectrum (Fig. fig:spec0212_0252 , Table tab:spec_lines ): broad H , narrow H and H , and forbidden [ OIII ] 4959, [ OIII ] 5007, [ OI ] 6300, [ NII ] 6548, [ NII ] 6583, [ SII ] 6716, and [ SII ] 6730. The measured redshift is z = 0.03366 0.00008 . The narrow-line flux ratios 2ha= 0.06 0.03 , 3hb = 1.25 0.12 (Fig. chart:bpt ) and the presence of a broad H component allow the object to be classified as Sy1.9. Moderate absorption ( 3 10^ 21 cm ^ 2 ) was revealed in the X-ray spectrum.

gmw_00010664.name: LEDA 16297
gmw_00010664.overview: LEDA 16297 is an active galaxy located at a redshift of approximately 0.031 [arXiv:2306.08646]. It is classified as a Seyfert 1.9 galaxy based on its optical emission lines, and its X-ray spectrum reveals absorption of 10^22 cm^-2 [arXiv:2306.08646].

sources.summary: The paper identifies LEDA 16297 as the X-ray counterpart galaxy with SIMBAD redshift z=0.03123, AGN-like color, optical emission lines, Sy1.9 classification, and significant X-ray absorption.

quotes.quote: In the \ and \ position error circles there is the galaxy LEDA 16297 (Fig. fig:guid_images ) with redshift z=0.03123 (SIMBAD) and color W1-W2=0.39 with which the radio source NVSS J045432+524009 can also be associated. Emission lines are seen in the optical spectrum (Fig. fig:spec0454_1109 , Table tab:spec_lines ): broad and narrow H , narrow H , and forbidden [ OIII ] 4959, [ OIII ] 5007, [ OI ] 6300, [ NII ] 6548, [ NII ] 6583, [ SII ] 6716 and [ SII ] 6730. The measured redshift is z = 0.03117 0.00012 . Based on the narrow-line flux ratios 2ha= 0.255 0.020 , 3hb = 1.19 0.13 (Fig. chart:bpt ) and the presence of a broad H component, the object can be classified as Sy1.9. Significant absorption ( 10^ 22 cm ^ 2 ) was revealed in the X-ray spectrum.

gmw_00010665.name: 2MASX J05131637+6627498
gmw_00010665.overview: 2MASX J05131637+6627498 is a Seyfert 2 galaxy located at a redshift of approximately z = 0.01479 [arXiv:2306.08646]. Its optical spectrum displays narrow emission lines, and its X-ray spectrum exhibits absorption of at least 10^22 cm^-2 [arXiv:2306.08646].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 2MASX J05131637+6627498 as a galaxy counterpart with redshift z=0.01491, AGN-like infrared color, narrow optical emission lines, Sy2 classification, and significant X-ray absorption.

quotes.quote: In the \ and \ position error circles there is the galaxy 2MASX J05131637+6627498 (Fig. fig:guid_images ) at redshift z=0.01491 (SIBMAD) with a color W1-W2=0.50 with which the radio source NVSS J051316+662801 can also be associated. Narrow Balmer H and H emission lines and narrow forbidden [ OIII ] 4959, [ OIII ] 5007, [ OI ] 6300, [ NII ] 6548, [ NII ] 6583, [ SII ] 6716, and [ SII ] 6730 lines are seen in the optical spectrum (Fig. fig:spec0454_1109 , Table tab:spec_lines ). The measured redshift is z = 0.01479 0.00008 . The narrow-line flux ratios 2ha= 0.053 0.011 , 3hb = 0.90 0.04 (Fig. chart:bpt ) and the absence of broad H and H components allow the object to be classified as Sy2. This is consistent with significant absorption ( 10^ 22 cm ^ 2 ) in the X-ray spectrum.

gmw_00010666.name: WISEA J110943.77+800805.6
gmw_00010666.overview: WISEA J110943.77+800805.6 is an active galactic nucleus located at a redshift of z=0.18879 [arXiv:2306.08646]. Optical emission line ratios classify the object as a Seyfert 2 galaxy, which exhibits an unusually hard X-ray continuum but no detected intrinsic absorption [arXiv:2306.08646].

sources.summary: The paper identifies WISEA J110943.77+800805.6 as an AGN-colored infrared/radio counterpart with redshift z=0.18879, Seyfert 2 optical ratios, no strong absorption, and an unusually hard X-ray continuum.

quotes.quote: There is the infrared and radio source WISEA J110943.77+800805.6 = NVSS J110944+800807 (Fig. fig:guid_images ) with a color W1-W2=0.76 typical for AGNs in the \ and \ position error circles.

Narrow H and H emission lines and narrow forbidden [ OII ] 3727, [ OIII ] 4959, [ OIII ] 5007, [ OI ] 6300, [ NII ] 6548, [ NII ] 6583, [ SII ] 6716, and [ SII ] 6730 lines are seen in the optical spectrum (Fig. fig:spec0454_1109 , Table tab:spec_lines ). The measured redshift is z=0.18879 0.00031 . The narrow-line flux ratios 2ha= 0.00 0.05 , 3hb = 1.02 0.14 (Fig. chart:bpt ) and the absence of broad H and H components allow the object to be classified as Sy2.

In spite of this, no statistically significant intrinsic absorption is revealed in the X-ray spectrum, while the upper limit on the absorption column density is <4 10^ 21 cm ^ 2 at 90 confidence. At the same time, the power-law continuum is unusually hard for AGNs, with a slope =0.7^ +0.5 _ 0.4 . This may suggest that the spectrum of this source actually has a more complex shape, which is impossible to ascertain due to the insufficient number of photons in the spectrum being analyzed.

gmw_00010667.name: LEDA 3097794
gmw_00010667.overview: LEDA 3097794 is a Seyfert 2 galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.03054 [arXiv:2306.08646]. It hosts an active galactic nucleus characterized by an infrared color typical of AGNs, narrow optical emission lines, and X-ray absorption [arXiv:2306.08646].

sources.summary: The paper identifies LEDA 3097794 as a galaxy counterpart with redshift z=0.03054, AGN-like infrared color, narrow Balmer and forbidden lines, Sy2 classification, and significant X-ray absorption.

quotes.quote: In the \ and \ position error circles there is the galaxy LEDA 3097794 (Fig. fig:guid_images ) with a redshift z=0.03054 (SIMBAD, based on the 6dF survey) and an infrared color W1-W2=0.78 typical for AGNs. Narrow Balmer H and H emission lines and narrow forbidden [ OIII ] 4959, [ OIII ] 5007, [ NII ] 6548, and [ NII ] 6583 lines are seen in the optical spectrum (Fig. fig:spec1612_1821 , Table tab:spec_lines ). The narrow-line flux ratios 2ha= 0.26 0.14 , 3hb >0.8 and the absence of broad H and H components allow the object to be classified as Sy2 (Fig. chart:bpt ). This is consistent with significant absorption ( 10^ 22 cm ^ 2 ) in the X-ray spectrum.

gmw_00010668.name: 2MASX J16194407 1326166
gmw_00010668.overview: 2MASX J16194407 1326166 is a Seyfert 1.9 galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.07891 [arXiv:2306.08646]. The galaxy features an active galactic nucleus with typical infrared colors, a broad H-alpha emission line, and an X-ray spectrum showing no absorption [arXiv:2306.08646].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 2MASX J16194407 1326166 as a galaxy counterpart with redshift z=0.07891, AGN-like infrared color, broad H emission, Sy1.9 classification, and no significant X-ray absorption.

quotes.quote: In the \ and \ position error circles there is the galaxy 2MASX J16194407 1326166 (Fig. fig:guid_images ) with a redshift z=0.07891 (SIMBAD, based on the 6dF survey) and an infrared color W1-W2=0.81 typical for AGNs. A broad H emission line and narrow forbidden [ OIII ] 4959, [ OIII ] 5007, [ NII ] 6548, and [ NII ] 6583 lines are seen in the optical spectrum (Fig. fig:spec1612_1821 , Table tab:spec_lines ). The narrow-line flux ratios 2ha >-0.7 , 3hb >0.7 (Fig. chart:bpt ) and the presence of a broad H component allow the object to be classified as Sy1.9. No significant absorption was revealed in the X-ray spectrum.

gmw_00010669.name: LEDA 2772547
gmw_00010669.overview: LEDA 2772547 is a Seyfert 2 active galactic nucleus located at a redshift of z = 0.0631 [arXiv:2306.08646]. The galaxy is associated with a radio source and exhibits X-ray absorption of 3 x 10^22 cm^-2 [arXiv:2306.08646].

sources.summary: The paper identifies LEDA 2772547 as an AGN-colored galaxy counterpart with optical and radio associations, redshift z=0.0631, Sy2 classification, and significant X-ray absorption.

quotes.quote: In the \ and \ position error circles there is the galaxy LEDA 2772547 (Fig. fig:guid_images ) with a color W1-W2=0.91 typical for AGNs. The radio source VLASS1QLCIR J182111.52+765816.6. can also be associated with it.

A narrow H emission line and narrow forbidden [ OIII ] 4959, [ OIII ] 5007, [ OI ] 6300, [ NII ] 6548, [ NII ] 6583, [ SII ] 6716, and [ SII ] 6730 lines are seen in the optical spectrum (Fig. fig:spec1612_1821 , Table tab:spec_lines ). The Fraunhofer MgI and NaD, F absorption lines are also seen. The redshift was measured from the emission lines, z = 0.0631 0.0004 . The narrow-line flux ratios 2ha= 0.14 0.04 , 3hb >0.9 (Fig. chart:bpt ) and the absence of broad H and H components allow the object to be classified as Sy2. Significant X-ray absorption ( 3 10^ 22 cm ^ 2 ) is observed.

gmw_00010670.name: 2MASS J19370820+6608213
gmw_00010670.overview: 2MASS J19370820+6608213 is a narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.07136 [arXiv:2306.08646]. The active galactic nucleus exhibits slight X-ray absorption and displays broad Balmer emission lines in its optical spectrum [arXiv:2306.08646].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 2MASS J19370820+6608213 as an optical/radio AGN-colored counterpart with broad Balmer lines, redshift z=0.07136, narrow-line Seyfert 1 classification, and slight X-ray absorption.

quotes.quote: In the \ and \ position error circles there is the optical and radio source 2MASS J19370820+6608213 = NVSS J193710+660830 (Fig. fig:guid_images ) with an infrared color W1-W2=0.64 typical for AGNs. Balmer emission lines are seen in the optical spectrum (Fig. fig:spec1937_2111 , Table tab:spec_lines ): broadH , broad H , broad and narrow H , broad and narrow H . Narrow forbidden [ OIII ] 4959, [ OIII ] 5007, [ NII ] 6548, [ NII ] 6583, [ SII ] 6716, and [ SII ] 6730 lines are also observed. The measured redshift is z = 0.07136 0.00012 . The narrow-line flux ratios 2ha= 0.48 0.04 , 3hb = 1.01 0.09 (Fig. chart:bpt ) and the presence of broad H , H , H , and H components with a typical line FWHM 2000 km s ^ 1 allow the object to be classified as a narrow-line Seyfert 1 (NLSy1) galaxy. There may be slight absorption ( 3 10^ 20 cm ^ 2 ) in the X-ray spectrum.

gmw_00010671.name: 2MASS J20033397+7013369
gmw_00010671.overview: 2MASS J20033397+7013369 is a Seyfert 1 active galactic nucleus located at a redshift of z = 0.09759 [arXiv:2306.08646]. Its optical spectrum displays broad Balmer lines and narrow forbidden lines, while its X-ray spectrum reveals slight absorption [arXiv:2306.08646].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 2MASS J20033397+7013369 as an AGN-colored optical counterpart with broad Balmer and forbidden lines, redshift z=0.09759, Seyfert 1 classification, and slight X-ray absorption.

quotes.quote: In \ and \ position error circles there is the optical object 2MASS J20033397+7013369 (Fig. fig:guid_images ) with a color W1-W2=0.89 typical for AGNs. Broad H and H emission lines and narrow forbidden [ OIII ] 4959, [ OIII ] 5007, [ NII ] 6548 and [ NII ] 6583 lines are observed in the optical spectrum (Fig. fig:spec1937_2111 , Table tab:spec_lines ). The measured redshift is z = 0.09759 0.00002 . The narrow-line flux ratios 2ha >0.23 , 3hb >0.8 (Fig. chart:bpt ) and the presence of broad H and H components allow the object to be classified as Sy1. Slight absorption ( 2 10^ 21 cm ^ 2 ) was revealed in the X-ray spectrum.

gmw_00010672.name: WISEA J211151.78+722816.4
gmw_00010672.overview: WISEA J211151.78+722816.4 is an active galactic nucleus located at a redshift of 0.10611 [arXiv:2306.08646]. It is classified as a Seyfert 2 galaxy based on its optical spectrum, and its X-ray spectrum exhibits absorption [arXiv:2306.08646].

sources.summary: The paper identifies WISEA J211151.78+722816.4 as an optical/radio AGN-colored counterpart with redshift 0.10611, Sy2 optical classification, and significant X-ray absorption.

quotes.quote: In the \ and \ position error circles there is the optical and radio source WISEA J211151.78+722816.4 = NVSS J211152+722819 (Fig. fig:guid_images ) with an infrared color W1-W2=1.08 typical for AGNs. A narrow H emission line and narrow forbidden [ OIII ] 4959, [ OIII ] 5007, [ NII ] 6548, and [ NII ] 6583 lines are observed in the optical spectrum (Fig. fig:spec1937_2111 , Table tab:spec_lines ). The measured redshift is 0.10611 0.00011 . The narrow-line flux ratios 2ha= 0.20 0.07 , 3hb >0.9 (Fig. chart:bpt ) and the absence of broad H and H components allow the object to be classified as Sy2. Significant absorption ( 10^ 22 cm ^ 2 ) is present in the X-ray spectrum.

gmw_00010673.name: PKS J0636 2036
gmw_00010673.overview: PKS J0636 2036 is an isolated elliptical galaxy [arXiv:1809.09327] classified as a Fanaroff-Riley II radio galaxy [arXiv:1801.02452]. It features two polarized radio hotspots [arXiv:1809.09327, arXiv:1801.02452], with the southern hotspot representing the brightest extragalactic polarized source detected at low frequencies [arXiv:1809.09327].

sources.summary: PKS J0636 2036 is described as an isolated-elliptical-hosted large radio galaxy with two polarised hotspots, including the brightest low-frequency extragalactic polarised source in the catalogue.

quotes.quote: We present postage stamp images of PKS J0636 2036 (hereafter PKSJ0636) in the upper panels of Figure fig:doubles_postage . We detect two polarised sources associated with the Northern hotspot (NH) and Southern hotspot (SH) of this LAS radio galaxy. PKSJ0636 is hosted by an isolated elliptical galaxy at redshift z=0.055 McAdam1975 .

At this redshift, the angular diameter between hotspots (approx. 14 arcmin) corresponds to a physical separation of 0.92 Mpc. The SH is the brightest extragalactic polarised source yet detected at low frequencies, with a polarised flux density in our catalogue of 1.23 0.02 Jy PSF ^ 1 , and FD =+50.2 0.1 rad m ^ 2 . We measure broadly consistent flux densities and FD to previous work with the MWA Lenc2017,OSullivan2018 .

sources.summary: The paper studies low-frequency polarization of FRII radio galaxy PKS J0636 2036, resolving both hotspots and modeling Faraday depolarization in the southern hotspot.

quotes.quote: We present a low-frequency, broadband polarization study of the FRII radio galaxy PKS J0636 2036 ( z=0.0551 ), using the Murchison Widefield Array (MWA) from 70 to 230 MHz. The northern and southern hotspots (separated by 14.5 on the sky) are resolved by the MWA (3 .3 resolution) and both are detected in linear polarization across the full frequency range. A combination of Faraday rotation measure (RM) synthesis and broadband polarization model-fitting are used to constrain the Faraday depolarization properties of the source. For the integrated southern hotspot emission, two RM component models are strongly favoured over a single RM component, and the best-fitting model requires Faraday dispersions of approximately 0.7 and 1.2 (with a mean RM of 50 ). High resolution imaging at 5 with the ATCA shows significant sub-structure in the southern hotspot and highlights some of the limitations in the polarization modelling of the MWA data.

gmw_00010674.name: UGC 11369
gmw_00010674.overview: UGC 11369 is a barred spiral galaxy of the SBa type [arXiv:1506.02845]. Located at a distance of approximately 66.2 megaparsecs [arXiv:1506.02845], the galaxy has a redshift of 0.015207 and hosted the supernova SN 2013en [arXiv:1506.02845].

sources.summary: The paper identifies UGC 11369 as the SBa host of SN 2013en, using its recession velocity, distance, redshift, and host-galaxy Na D absorption for the supernova analysis.

quotes.quote: SN 2013en (= PSN J18513735+2338206) in UGC 11369 was discovered Ciab13 in an unfiltered CCD image (limiting magnitude of 19.5 mag ) taken by a 0.5 meter Newtonian telescope in the course of the Italian Supernovae Search Project (ISSP) http://italiansupernovae.org on July 30.98 UT. Its J2000.0 coordinates are R.A. = 18^ h 51^ m 37^ s .35 , Decl. = +23^ d 38 ' 20 ''.6 , located at 6 '' west and 17 '' north of the center of the galaxy UGC 11369 (see Figure  Fig:1 ).

The host galaxy of SN 2013en, UGC 11369, is an SBa galaxy with a recession velocity of 4559 35 km s^ 1 Falc99 . Adopting a cold-dark-matter cosmological model with parameters _ =0.73 , _ m =0.27 , H_ 0 =73 5 \ km s^ 1 Mpc^ 1 , the virgo infall distance of UGC 11369 is D=66.2 4.7 Mpc ( =34.11 0.15 mag ) , which will be adopted in this work in the calculations of the luminosity of SN 2013en. The redshift of the UGC 11369 of z =0.015207 is used as the redshift of SN 2013en.
The \ D absorption due to the host galaxy of SN 2013en is measured to be about 1.62 at t +4 days, which correspond to a possible host galaxy reddening E(B-V)_ host =0.26 0.03 mag Tura03, Pozn12 .

gmw_00010675.name: galaxy associated with the DLA at z_ abs =2.2618
aliases_local: galaxy associated with the DLA at z_ abs =2.2618
gmw_00010675.overview: The galaxy associated with the DLA at z_abs=2.2618 is a high-redshift system [arXiv:2011.04557]. It exhibits Lyman-alpha emission at a redshift of z=2.2616, which is spatially offset from the background quasar by 1.3 arcseconds [arXiv:2011.04557].

sources.original_name: galaxy associated with the DLA at z_ abs =2.2618
sources.summary: The paper reports the Q 0124+0044 DLA counterpart as Ly emission at z=2.2616 offset from the quasar, with minimal slit-loss and a lower-limit star formation rate.

quotes.quote: The X-shooter slit was placed at a single position angle of +130 east of north that serendipitously contained emission from the galaxy associated with the DLA at z_ abs =2.2618 . Remarkably, the slit position was only 1 1 degree off from the true position angle as measured on the HST images presented in this work (see Table tab:galfit ). This implies that slit-loss was minimal and caused by slit-width, not by false angle.The emission counterpart was discovered during the XQ-100 campaign by one of the authors of this work (LC), but never reported in previous studies, as XQ-100 papers focused on the absorption properties of the DLA in the 1-dimensional (1D) quasar spectrum. Here we present an analysis of the 2-dimensional (2D) X-shooter spectrum, and report on the emission associated with the DLA counterpart. We detect Ly emission at z_ em =2.2616 , spatially offset from the quasar by 1.3 arcsec (see Fig.  fig:0124 ).

gmw_00010676.name: absorber towards Q 0139--0824
gmw_00010676.overview: The absorber towards Q 0139--0824 is a high-redshift galaxy with a confirmed Lyman-alpha emission line [arXiv:2011.04557]. This emission is spatially offset by 1.6 arcseconds south-west of the background quasar [arXiv:2011.04557].

sources.summary: The paper confirms Ly emission for the Q 0139--0824 absorber, measures its offset and Ly flux, and reanalyzes the absorption metallicity from X-shooter data.

quotes.quote: The absorber towards Q 0139--0824 was presented in . A tentative detection of Ly emission associated with the absorber at z_ abs = 2.6773 was originally found with a low signal in IFU data from VLT/VIMOS (Programme ID 077.A-0450, PI: Christensen) but never published. To confirm the detection we conducted deeper follow-up spectroscopic observations using the VLT FORS spectrograph (Programme ID 081.A-0506, PI: Christensen). The deeper data confirmed the detection of Ly , spatially offset by 1.6 arcsec south-west of the quasar (see Fig.  fig:0139 ),and is published here for the first time. During subsequent X-shooter observations aimed at identifying additional emission lines (Programme ID 088.A-0378, PI: Christensen) a position angle of 98 east of north was used.

gmw_00010677.name: emission counterpart of the absorber towards Q 0310+0055
aliases_local: emission counterpart of the absorber towards Q 0310+0055
gmw_00010677.overview: The emission counterpart of the absorber towards Q 0310+0055 is a high-redshift galaxy detected via Lyman-alpha line emission [arXiv:2011.04557]. Photometric observations of the galaxy include a narrow-band continuum magnitude of 26.8, alongside B and V band non-detections [arXiv:2011.04557].

sources.original_name: emission counterpart of the absorber towards Q 0310+0055
sources.summary: The paper describes the Q 0310+0055 counterpart as a Subaru/FOCAS Ly-detected galaxy, corrects its narrow-band continuum, includes photometric limits, and derives an SFR lower limit.

quotes.quote: The emission counterpart of the absorber towards Q 0310+0055 was detected using long-slit spectra from Subaru/FOCAS Kashikawa2014 . To accommodate the Subaru/FOCAS narrow-band detection ( mag _ NB502 = 25.46 0.13 ; Kashikawa2014 ) in our stellar mass analysis (see Sect.  subsec:SED ), we first remove the contribution from Ly line emission. Based on the reported Ly luminosity of L_ Ly = (1.07 0.14) 10^ 42   erg s ^ 1 , and assuming a flat continuum shape over the NB502 bandpass, with a filter width of 60  https://www.naoj.org/Observing/Instruments/FOCAS/camera/filters.html , we obtain a NB continuum magnitude of NB502 _ cont. = 26.8 0.6 . For the SED fits using LePhare (see Sect.  subsec:SED ), we construct a simple tophat narrow-band filter transmission curve with a central wavelength _c = 5025   and a width of 60  . We also include their B and V band non-detections (25.32 and 25.50 magnitudes, respectively) reported as 3 limits in 2 arcsec apertures at the position of the narrow-band detection.

gmw_00010678.name: emission counterpart of the DLA towards Q 0338--0005
aliases_local: emission counterpart of the DLA towards Q 0338--0005
gmw_00010678.overview: The emission counterpart of the DLA towards Q 0338--0005 is a high-redshift Lyman-alpha emitter [arXiv:2011.04557]. It has a star formation rate lower limit of 0.3 solar masses per year and exhibits Lyman-alpha emission at an impact parameter of 0.5 arcseconds [arXiv:2011.04557].

sources.original_name: emission counterpart of the DLA towards Q 0338--0005
sources.summary: The paper treats the Q 0338--0005 DLA counterpart as an X-shooter and UVES-confirmed Ly emitter, with measured impact parameter, position angle, and SFR lower limit.

quotes.quote: The emission counterpart of the DLA towards Q 0338--0005 was originally detected by Krogager2012 as part of the VLT/X-shooter campaign, and was confirmed in an archival VLT/UVES spectrum by Bashir2019 . Krogager2012 report the detection of Ly emission at an impact parameter of 0.5 arcsec with a position angle of 58 east of north. Krogager2017 report a lower limit to the SFR of >0.3   \ based on the line flux of Ly as measured in the long-slit spectra taken during the X-shooter campaign. The Galactic extinction is rather high for this sightline, with an extinction correction in the F606W filter of 0.207 mag.

gmw_00010679.name: emission counterpart of the absorber towards Q 1313+1441
aliases_local: emission counterpart of the absorber towards Q 1313+1441
gmw_00010679.overview: The emission counterpart of the absorber towards Q 1313+1441 is a high-redshift galaxy [arXiv:2011.04557]. It was detected via Lyman-alpha emission in the trough of a damped Lyman-alpha absorption profile [arXiv:2011.04557].

sources.original_name: emission counterpart of the absorber towards Q 1313+1441
sources.summary: The paper revises the Q 1313+1441 absorber counterpart from two possible slit detections to a single extended, disturbed near-infrared counterpart in HST data.

quotes.quote: The emission counterpart of the absorber towards Q 1313+1441 was detected by Krogager2017 as part of the X-shooter campaign Fynbo2010, Krogager2017 . The detection is based on Ly emission in the trough of the damped Ly absorption profile at an impact parameter of 1.3 arcsec. The authors report detections in two slits with different orientations yielding seemingly inconsistent relative positions of the emission. The quoted impact parameter refers to the brighter of the two detections. Krogager2017 suggest that the two detections may arise from two different neighbouring galaxies.

gmw_00010680.name: emission counterpart of the DLA towards Q 2059--0528
aliases_local: emission counterpart of the DLA towards Q 2059--0528
gmw_00010680.overview: The emission counterpart of the DLA towards Q 2059--0528 is a high-redshift galaxy detected via Lyman-alpha emission [arXiv:2011.04557]. This emission suggests a low impact parameter of less than 0.75 arcseconds and provides a lower limit to the star formation rate of greater than 0.2 solar masses per year [arXiv:2011.04557].

sources.original_name: emission counterpart of the DLA towards Q 2059--0528
sources.summary: The paper reports the Q 2059--0528 DLA counterpart as Ly emission seen in multiple slits, giving a robust stacked flux, low impact parameter, and SFR constraints.

quotes.quote: The emission counterpart of the DLA towards Q 2059--0528 was detected by Hartoog2015 , as part of the X-Shooter campaign Fynbo2010, Krogager2017 . The authors tentatively detect Ly emission in all three slit orientations (at 3 in individual slits). By stacking all three observations, Hartoog2015 obtain a robust detection in Ly of f_ Ly = (10.19 1.67) 10^ 18  erg s ^ 1  cm ^ 2 . The Ly line flux provides a lower limit to the SFR of >0.2   . The fact that emission is seen in all three slits suggests a low impact parameter for the counterpart ( < 0.75 arcsec).

gmw_00010681.name: emission counterpart of the absorber towards Q 2239--2949
aliases_local: emission counterpart of the absorber towards Q 2239--2949
gmw_00010681.overview: The emission counterpart of the absorber towards Q 2239--2949 is a high-redshift galaxy located at a redshift of z=1.825 [arXiv:2011.04557]. It is spatially offset from the quasar by 2.4 arcseconds and has a Lyman-alpha emission line flux that indicates a star formation rate lower limit of greater than 0.07 solar masses per year [arXiv:2011.04557].

sources.original_name: emission counterpart of the absorber towards Q 2239--2949
sources.summary: The paper identifies the Q 2239--2949 absorber counterpart as a Ly emitter at z=1.825, reports its quasar offset, SFR lower limit, and notes sub-DLA metallicity caveats.

quotes.quote: The emission counterpart of the absorber towards Q 2239--2949 was identified by Zafar2017 . The authors report a detection of Ly emission at z_ abs = 1.825 spatially offset by 2.4 arcsec from the quasar. The Ly emission line flux of the counterpart translates into a lower limit on the SFR of >0.07 0.01   . The absorber is a sub-DLA and no ionisation correction was applied to the reported metallicity, which should therefore be interpreted with care. We adopt the following extinction corrections for the F606W, F105W and F160W filters: 0.043, 0.013, and 0.009 mag, respectively.

gmw_00010682.name: absorber towards Q 2247--6015
gmw_00010682.overview: The absorber towards Q 2247--6015 is a high-redshift galaxy counterpart detected in the H-alpha emission line [arXiv:2011.04557]. It is located at an impact parameter of 3.1 arcseconds from the background quasar and exhibits a dust-corrected star formation rate of 33 solar masses per year [arXiv:2011.04557].

sources.summary: The paper discusses the Q 2247--6015 absorber counterpart as an H-detected galaxy with an alternate quasar-field name, 3.1 arcsec impact parameter, and dust-corrected SFR.

quotes.quote: Q 2247--6015 (alternative name: HE 2243--60) The absorber towards Q 2247--6015 was first analysed by Lopez2002 using data from VLT/UVES. Bouche2012, Bouche2013 conducted comprehensive followup observations of the quasar field using VLT/SINFONI IFU data and detect the associated counterpart in H at an impact parameter of 3.1 arcsec. The authors report a dust-corrected H star formation rate of 33  , which owing to the aperture-free IFU data is not affected by slit-losses. Extinction corrections for the F606W, F105W and F160W filters are applied as follows: 0.047, 0.015 and 0.009 mag, respectively.

gmw_00010683.name: IC 4538
gmw_00010683.overview: IC 4538 is a spiral galaxy characterized by a high-surface-brightness disk [arXiv:1501.07178] and a complex, asymmetric spiral arm structure [arXiv:1806.06591]. The galaxy features a multi-armed pattern that affects its ellipticity profile [arXiv:1501.07178], and it does not host a bar [arXiv:1501.07178].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes ESO-LV 5140100 as a high-surface-brightness spiral whose bulge spectra were measured, no bar was needed, and multi-armed structure affects its ellipticity profile.

quotes.quote: ESO-LV 1890070, ESO-LV 4460170, ESO-LV 4500200, and ESO-LV 5140100 turned out to host a high surface-brightness disk and therefore were not considered in the subsequent analysis morelli2012 . Since the long-slit spectra of the four neglected galaxies were available to us, we decided to derive the stellar population properties of their bulges.

A bar was detected and modelled in ESO-LV 4460170 and ESO-LV 4500200, whereas no bar was needed to improve the surface-brightness models of ESO-LV 1890070 and ESO-LV 5140100. The bump in the surface-brightness profile associated to both a significant peak in the ellipticity and a roughly constant position angle is the photometric signature of the bar in ESO-LV 4460170 and ESO-LV 4500200 (see aguetal09 for a detailed discussion). The flat surface-brightness profile of ESO-LV 1890070 and the increase of ellipticity measured between 30 and 60 arcsec are due to the two prominent and symmetrical arms characterizing the galaxy spiral pattern. The multi-armed structure of ESO-LV 5140100 results in the increase and variation of the ellipticity between 20 and 40 arcsec.

The properties of the stellar populations of ESO-LV 1890070, ESO-LV 4460170, and ESO-LV 5140100 are consistent with the previous results obtained for the bulges of spiral galaxies jabletal07, gandetal07, moreetal08 . The gradients of the stellar population properties along the major axis of all the galaxies were measured in their bulge-dominated regions. The results are consistent with previous findings for the bulges of spiral galaxies jabletal07, moreetal08, morelli2012 .

sources.summary: The paper presents IC 4538 as a complex spiral case where the secondary Fourier maximum, rather than the strongest peak, yields a pitch angle matching the deprojected image.

quotes.quote: The highest amplitude of IC 4538 may correspond to some other bi-symmetric component, although it is inconspicuous in the deprojected image, or it may be just caused by the complicated spiral structure. If the most prominent peak were chosen to do the calculation, it would lead to a severely incorrect (usually overestimated) pitch angle. Instead, we identify the secondary maxima, the highest amplitude of the m = 4 Fourier mode (purple arrow in Figure 7), to calculate a pitch angle of 19 98, which produces synthetic arms that match well the deprojected image and is consistent with the value of 20 0 derived from the 1D method.
Even though the spiral arms are not perfectly symmetric, as in the case of IC 4538 shown in Figure 7 (bottom-left panel), the 2D method can still correctly measure the pitch angle.

gmw_00010684.name: RG3
gmw_00010684.overview: RG3 is a bent, double-lobed radio galaxy projected against the middle of a southeastern radio relic [arXiv:2111.06940]. The galaxy exhibits the radio properties of a Fanaroff-Riley II source, and its radio emission may be interacting with the relic [arXiv:2111.06940].

sources.summary: RG3 is a bent double-lobed radio galaxy projected on the SE relic, with FR-II properties, possible host candidates, weak RM change, and a possible interaction with the relic.

quotes.quote: The second, bent, radio galaxy is RG3, seen projected against the middle of the SE relic (RA = 20 14 25.847 , Dec = 57 09 46.86 ).

The third radio galaxy (RG3) is a double lobed radio galaxy located at the edge of the SE radio relic, close to the expected location of the shock front. RG3 shows radio properties of FR-II galaxies and it is not associated with any of the spectroscopically identified cluster members of . The spectral index of the hotspots appear inverted, with = 0.45 in the eastern lobe and = 0.17 in the western lobe. The source lack an obvious counterpart in the optical, infrared, X-ray and gamma ray bands.

With no z information, RG3 may or may not be a member of the cluster; however, the radio emission of the radio galaxy bridged into the relic and it is spatially coincident with a break in the filamentary structure of the relic. This could be an indication of an interaction between RG3 and the eastern relic.

gmw_00010685.name: NGC4061
gmw_00010685.overview: NGC 4061 is a bent-double radio galaxy [arXiv:1007.3286] that features symmetric, decelerated radio jets [arXiv:1007.3286]. The galaxy is located within X-ray emitting intergalactic gas [arXiv:1007.3286], which interacts with and bends its jet structures [arXiv:1007.3286, arXiv:astro-ph/0610400].

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 4061 as a bent-double radio-galaxy probe of intragroup gas, deriving its velocity, modeling bent jets, and finding the gas density consistent with X-ray emission.

quotes.quote: The velocity of bent-double radio galaxy, NGC 4061, as measured by 2004ApJ...607..202M is 7393 32 . Previous radial velocity measurements have given this source a velocity of 7203 27 1991trcb.book.....D and 7369 52 1999PASP..111..438F . We follow the method for using bent-double radio sources as probes of intergalactic gas outlined in 2008ApJ...685..858F with a few additional caveats. We are assuming that the jet bulk flows have decelerated to non-relativistic speeds on kilo-parsec scales, and certainly by the time they are bent by the motion of the host galaxy ( 15 kpc from the core of the radio galaxy). This assumption is borne out by the symmetric fluxes in the two jets (see Figure fig:lgt6800 ), were they still relativistic then beaming effects would produce asymmetric fluxes which would be easily observable in these data.

With the standard equipartition minimum synchrotron pressure from relativistic electrons only, the density of the intergalactic gas that NGC 4061 is traveling through is 4 1 10^ 28 or 4 1 10^ 4 . This intergalactic gas density, for the case including entrained protons, is very similar to the density in the model fit to the X-ray emitting intergalactic gas at the projected position of NGC 4061 ( 30 kpc from the X-ray centre). We have good reason to believe that NGC 4061 is located within the X-ray emitting gas because the X-ray contours trace the flaring ends of the jets very closely.

sources.summary: The paper models NGC4061 as a 21 cm radio source, comparing simulated jet structure with a radio map and deriving a velocity geometry for the galaxy and jet.

quotes.quote: We have chosen the radio source NGC4061 at 21 cm, see Figure 5b in venkatesan for a radio map; the basic data as extracted from the radio observations are reported in Table data_ngc4061 . The result of the simulation is visualised in Figure NGC4061 and in Figure NGC4061_real with the radio map superimposed on the simulation ; the caption of the Figure shows the adopted input parameters while the output data are reported in Table fine_ngc4061 . } } In this case we know that the dispersion velocity in the cluster is 485 km/sec , see Table 1 in venkatesan . The velocity v_z in our model is 119.08 km/sec implying an angle of 14.2^ between the direction of the galaxy motion and the initial direction of the jet. A typical plot of the jet velocity as a function of the time is reported in Figure NGC4061_velo .

gmw_00010686.name: J0316+0904
gmw_00010686.overview: J0316+0904 is a blazar classified as a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:1402.5464]. Its host galaxy has been detected with imaging redshift estimates that are consistent with its spectra-derived bounds [arXiv:1402.5464].

sources.summary: The paper reports a likely BL Lac host detection for J0316+0904, with 2.2 systematic significance, visual support from radial profiles, and an imaging redshift consistent with spectral bounds.

quotes.quote: Two are seen with somewhat lower confidence, with J0316+0904 having 2.2 _ sys significance and J1023 4336 appearing at 1.1 _ sys (although both are of high statistical significance). Visual inspection of the images and the azimuthally average radial profile plots (below) confirm that the former is a very likely detection, but the latter, while a plausible detection, may be more safely treated as an upper limit.

For the five sources with host detection, two (J0316+0904 and J0543 5532) have imaging redshift estimates consistent with the spectra-derived bounds in that paper; the low significance detection of J1023 4336 is also consistent.

gmw_00010687.name: J1023 4336
gmw_00010687.overview: J1023 4336 is a galaxy associated with a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:1402.5464]. It has a marginal host galaxy detection that may be interpreted as an upper limit, implying a redshift lower bound of z > 0.42 [arXiv:1402.5464].

sources.summary: The paper treats J1023 4336 as a marginal BL Lac host detection, probably safer as an upper limit, but still consistent with spectral bounds and implying z greater than 0.42.

quotes.quote: Two are seen with somewhat lower confidence, with J0316+0904 having 2.2 _ sys significance and J1023 4336 appearing at 1.1 _ sys (although both are of high statistical significance). Visual inspection of the images and the azimuthally average radial profile plots (below) confirm that the former is a very likely detection, but the latter, while a plausible detection, may be more safely treated as an upper limit.

For the five sources with host detection, two (J0316+0904 and J0543 5532) have imaging redshift estimates consistent with the spectra-derived bounds in that paper; the low significance detection of J1023 4336 is also consistent.
In six cases we derive lower bounds on the redshift; J1023 4336 may be interpreted as a lower bound of z> 0.42 .

gmw_00010688.name: J0114+1325
gmw_00010688.overview: J0114+1325 is a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:1402.5464] that lies at a redshift slightly below its spectral lower-bound estimate [arXiv:1402.5464]. The host galaxy has two close companion galaxies within a 20 kiloparsec radius that overlap with its wings and complicate flux measurements [arXiv:1402.5464].

sources.summary: The paper finds J0114+1325 slightly below spectral lower-bound redshift estimates and reports two close companion galaxies whose overlap with the BL Lac host complicates flux measurement.

quotes.quote: Three (J0114+1325, J0115+2519, and J0721+7120) lie at slightly lower z than the spectral lower bound, but are within 3 , and J0115+2519 is fully consistent with the strict lower limit provided by an intervening absorber.

J0114+1325 had two companions within a 3 ^ ( 20 kpc) radius. These companions were close enough that they overlap significantly with the BL Lac host wings, making accurate flux measurement difficult; we found their i ^ magnitudes to be roughly 20.5 and 20.8.

gmw_00010689.overview: Object 4950 is an early-type galaxy located at a redshift of z=1.55 [arXiv:astro-ph/0503102]. Its spectrum shows a rise toward the ultraviolet below 6000 Å, and it exhibits a weak [OII] 3727 emission line [arXiv:astro-ph/0503102].

sources.summary: The paper assigns object 4950 a secure z=1.55 early-type-galaxy redshift, with SED and spectral agreement, UV rise, K20 membership, and weak [OII] emission.

quotes.quote: 4950: z=1.55 , Class = A. The SED and spectral fitting agree very closely with z=0.02 , the spectrum clearly shows the feature. The rise toward the UV below 6000 \ is consistently present also in the photometry. This object is also part of the K20 survey (Cimatti et al. 2002b) with a measured redshift of z=1.553 . A weak [ OII ] 3727 emission line is detected in the K20 survey spectrum.

gmw_00010690.overview: The galaxy 1025 is a high-redshift object with a secure spectroscopic redshift of 1.73 [arXiv:astro-ph/0503102]. It exhibits a fairly blue spectrum with an upturn consistent with A-type stars, and it has been detected in the X-rays [arXiv:astro-ph/0503102].

sources.summary: The paper assigns object 1025 a secure z=1.73 redshift, notes its blue spectrum and characteristic feature, and reports X-ray detection plus compatible photometric redshift.

quotes.quote: 1025: z=1.73 , Class = A. The SED and spectral fitting agree extremely closely with z=0.01 . The spectrum, that is fairly blue especially at <2500 , shows the characteristic feature. The upturn in the blue spectra is consistent with what expected for A-type stars (Fig. fig:MgUV1 ). This source is detected in the X-rays (Giacconi et al. 2002; Alexander et al. 2003; see Sect. sec:Xrays ). Zheng et al. (2004) propose 1.47<z_ phot <1.58 , reasonably consistent with our spectroscopic redshift.

gmw_00010691.overview: The galaxy 3523 is a high-redshift, early-type galaxy with a spectroscopic redshift of z=1.76 [arXiv:astro-ph/0503102]. Its spectrum is consistent with the presence of a 2900 Å break, and its stellar content is characteristic of an old galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0503102].

sources.summary: The paper favors z=1.76 for object 3523 despite an alternate spectral fit, relying on SED consistency, a 2900 break, and agreement with prior old-galaxy photometry.

quotes.quote: 3523: z=1.76 , Class = B. The spectrum would suggest z 1.1 as the best fitting old galaxy solution by placing the 4000 \ break around 9000 \ and the CaII H K lines in the dip at 8700 . The above solution is not consistent with the photometric SED, as at z 1.1 no reasonably good fit could be obtained. A secondary solution from spectral fitting, with nearly the same ^2 of the best one, is found at z=1.76 , in very good agreement with the photometric redshift within z=0.04 . We propose the latter solution as the spectroscopic redshift for this galaxy. The feature is not evident, although the spectrum is consistent at least with the presence of a 2900 \ break. The proposed redshift thus relies strongly on the overall SED and spectral fitting.

The absorption like spectral features at 8900 \ and 9200 \ are to be considered spurious noise features for our proposed redshift. Yan et al. (2004) suggest this is an old galaxy at z_ phot =1.6 , in good agreement with the redshift proposed here.

gmw_00010692.overview: The high-redshift galaxy 3650 has a spectroscopic redshift of 1.91 [arXiv:astro-ph/0503102] and is classified as a compact galaxy [arXiv:1511.06415]. It resides in a dense environment [arXiv:1101.0843] with 34 identified satellite galaxies [arXiv:1511.06415] and exhibits a red-centered color gradient [arXiv:1101.0843].

sources.summary: Galaxy 23555 has a spectroscopic redshift and a red-centered color gradient with red upturns; the paper attributes outer-annulus systematics to contamination from nearby luminous sources.

quotes.quote: Four galaxies have spectroscopic redshift, 22704 and 23555 from cimatti08 , 24279 from daddi05 and 24626 from vanzella08 .
Galaxy 23555 and 24279 also have well--defined red centers, but their outskirts are observed at relatively lower S/Ns and the resulting color gradient is not as clear as that of Galaxy 24626.

Galaxy 23555 exhibits the red upturn in both the (B-V) and (U-V) color gradients. The photometric redshift probability distribution of the outermost annulus of this galaxy (see sub:photozdist ) shows a relatively large deviation from its spectroscopic redshift, suggesting that the photometry of this area of the galaxy is subject to some systematics. A visual inspection of the the H--band image reveals that this galaxy resides in a relatively dense environment, with a luminous, large companion and a bright star located nearby. Low--surface brightness H--band light from these sources is very likely contaminating the outermost annulus of the galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper identifies XDF463 as the spectroscopic compact galaxy in XDF, estimates its virial radius, and finds 34 satellites whose existing mass alone cannot explain expected descendant growth.

quotes.quote: To minimize the uncertainty, we select those with spectroscopic redshift ( z_ spec. ), finding only one compact galaxy, XDF463. The location of XDF463 and its virial radius are shown in a pseudo color image in Fig. fig1 . Fortunately, XDF463 resides in the central part of the XDF, where we can search its satellite galaxies impartially.
From the stellar mass, M_* 3.9 10^ 10 M_ , we estimate its virial radius at the redshift to be r_ vir 300 kpc, based on the numerical simulation by Oser et al.

The total mass of 34 satellites is 4.3 10^ 10 M_ , which is comparable to that of XDF463. The mass growth of XDF463 by the accretion of the satellites is, however, in deficit to become a massive elliptical galaxy in the local universe ( 10^ 11 M_ ) expected from the numerical simulations (e.g., Oser et al. oser10 ) and the observations (e.g., van Dokkum et al. vandokkum10 ), even if all satellites fall to the central galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper gives object 3650 a secure z=1.91 classification, with closely matching SED and spectral fits and a very evident diagnostic feature.

quotes.quote: 3650: z=1.91 , Class = A. The SED and spectral fitting agree extremely closely with z=0.01 . The feature is very evident.

gmw_00010693.overview: The object 3574 is a high-redshift galaxy with a spectroscopically confirmed redshift of 1.98 [arXiv:astro-ph/0503102]. Photometric and spectral fitting indicate that it is an old, early-type galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0503102].

sources.summary: The paper assigns object 3574 a secure z=1.98 redshift, with close SED-spectral agreement, a detected feature, and excellent agreement with Yan et al. photometry.

quotes.quote: 3574: z=1.98 , Class = A. The SED and spectral fitting agree closely with z=0.04 . The spectrum shows the feature. Yan et al. (2004) propose this is an old galaxy at z_ phot =1.9 , in excellent agreement with our spectroscopic redshift.

gmw_00010694.overview: The galaxy 1446 is a high-redshift source at z=2.47 [arXiv:astro-ph/0503102]. Its photometric spectral energy distribution is best fit by old, passive stellar population models, although it is also detected as a hard X-ray source [arXiv:astro-ph/0503102].

sources.summary: The paper treats object 1446 as a less-secure z=2.47 source, considering AGN-line alternatives but finding old/passive SED fits significantly preferred over reddened QSO templates.

quotes.quote: 1446: z=2.47 , Class = B. The SED and spectral fitting are in fair agreement at best with z 0.4 . The spectrum has a strong feature at 9200 \ that in the spectral fitting with old models is identified with the feature. Emission line identification algorithms described in Xu et al. (2005), however, pick up the feature as a possible emission line. If the feature is an emission line we find best fitting 9230 , FWHM 160 \ and EW 150 . The large FWHM and EW would suggest an AGN emission line.

This would be possible as the object is an hard X-ray source (Giacconi et al. 2002; Alexander et al. 2004), although no strong emission line is detected in the FORS spectra described by Szokoly et al. (2004). We tried to fit its photometric SED with reddened QSO/AGN templates but the fit still significantly prefers old/passive models.

gmw_00010695.name: NVSS J093241+530633
gmw_00010695.overview: NVSS J093241+530633 is an active galactic nucleus with a broadband spectral energy distribution similar to a blazar [arXiv:1801.01905]. Its high-energy emission is well explained by the external Compton process, indicating a strong similarity to flat-spectrum radio quasars [arXiv:1801.01905].

sources.summary: The paper reports SED modeling for NVSS J093241+530633, giving leptonic model parameters and finding blazar-like, FSRQ-like external-Compton emission behavior.

quotes.quote: We generate the broadband spectral energy distributions (SEDs) of two sources, NVSS J093241+530633 and GB6 J0937+5008, for which we could find the archival multi-wavelength data. The SEDs are modeled using a simple one-zone leptonic emission model see, e.g., for details 2009MNRAS.397..985G and the results are shown in the top panel of Figure fig:3lac . The associated parameters for NVSS J093241+530633 are as follows: broken power law spectral indices before ( p ) and after ( q ) the break= 1.8 and 3.7, respectively; magnetic field ( B )= 1.3 Gauss; bulk Lorentz factor ( )= 10; break ( _ b ) and maximum ( _ max ) energies of the electron energy distribution= 53 and 3000, respectively; distance of the emission region ( R_ diss )= 0.07 pc; size of the broad line region ( R_ BLR )= 0.06 pc, disk luminosity ( L_ disk )= 3.5 10 ^ 45 .

As can be seen, the sources exhibit a SED similar to blazars. Interestingly, the LAT spectrum is well explained by the external Compton process with the BLR and the dusty torus being the primary sources of the seed photons for the inverse Compton scattering.
