This indicates a stronger similarity with FSRQs than BL Lac type objects see, e.g., for similar results 2012A&A...548A.106F,2013ApJ...768...52P .

gmw_00010696.name: 3FGL J0937.7+5008
gmw_00010696.overview: 3FGL J0937.7+5008 is a narrow-line Seyfert 1 candidate galaxy [arXiv:1801.01905] located at a redshift of z=0.28 [arXiv:1801.01905]. The active nucleus exhibits a point-like structure [arXiv:1107.5443], a blazar-like spectral energy distribution [arXiv:1801.01905], and high long-term radio variability at 5 GHz [arXiv:1107.5443].

sources.summary: The paper classifies 3FGL J0937.7+5008 as an NLSy1 candidate, notes weak Fe II but otherwise similar multi-wavelength properties, and models blazar-like external-Compton emission.

quotes.quote: 3FGL J0937.7+5008 ( z=0.28 ), 3FGL J1520.3+4209 ( z=0.49 ), and 3FGL J2118.4+0013 ( z=0.46 ). All of them were classified as FSRQs in 3LAC and therefore, following 2017ApJS..229...39R , they can now be considered as NLSy1 galaxies. Note that 3FGL J0937.7+5008 exhibits a relatively weak Fe ii emission Fe ii /H _ = 0.05 ; 2017ApJS..229...39R contrary to that typically seen in NLSy1 sources.

Since all of the other multi-wavelength properties of 3FGL J0937.7+5008 are similar to the known NLSy1 objects, we include it in our sample, though it would be appropriate to call this object as a NLSy1 candidate to remain aligned with the classification definition of a NLSy1 source 1985ApJ...297..166O,1989ApJ...342..224G .

For 3FGL J0937.7+5008, we adopt the following SED parameters: p =1.9, q =3.6, B = 1.7 Gauss; = 17; _ b = 46; _ max = 3000; R_ diss = 0.01 pc; R_ BLR = 0.007 pc, L_ disk = 5.1 10 ^ 43 . As can be seen, the sources exhibit a SED similar to blazars. Interestingly, the LAT spectrum is well explained by the external Compton process with the BLR and the dusty torus being the primary sources of the seed photons for the inverse Compton scattering. This indicates a stronger similarity with FSRQs than BL Lac type objects see, e.g., for similar results 2012A&A...548A.106F,2013ApJ...768...52P .

sources.summary: The paper reports that J0937.1 + 5008 has strong long-term 5 GHz variability, a later 2.3-fold flux increase, no 1986-1987 short-term variability, point-like VLBI structure, and Fermi detection.

quotes.quote: J0937.1 + 5008 -- The source J0937.1 + 5008 exhibits a high degree of variability at 5 GHz, of the order of 60 to 70 on a time scale of about 22 years comparing the Effelsberg flux density and the GB6 flux density. Moreover, it shows an increase in flux density by a factor of 2.3 comparing the Effelsberg flux density with the flux density measured few months later (22 Oct 2009) with the European VLBI Network observations at the same frequency (Mantovani et al. in prep.). Short-term variability was not found comparing the two measurements made in 1986 and 1987 by the GB6. A preliminary milli-arcsecond resolution image of J0937.1 + 5008 (resolution better than 5 mas; rms noise 1.5 mJy/beam) shows that the source is point-like mantovani2010 . J0937.1 + 5008 was detected by the Fermi Gamma-Ray Space Telescope abdo .

gmw_00010697.name: source C
gmw_00010697.overview: Source C is a massive galaxy with a stellar mass exceeding 10^11 solar masses [arXiv:2301.01375]. It is a likely active galactic nucleus host with narrow emission lines, and it is suggested to be the seed of a growing brightest cluster galaxy within an ongoing mega-merger [arXiv:2301.01375].

sources.summary: The paper identifies source C as the most likely AGN host among the central galaxies, emphasizing its large stellar mass, narrow lines, high OH equivalent width, and inferred SMBH mass.

quotes.quote: Source C does distinguish itself with an anomalously narrow \ and CO(4--3) line width for its luminosity Hill20 . Rotermund21 identified C as a significant outlier from the \ galaxy sample in its M_ dyn /M_ gas ratio inferred from the narrow CO(4--3) line width and large luminosity, similar to many high- z QSOs (e.g., Narayanan2008,walter2009,hill2019 ), where selection effects favoring face-on orientation offer viable explanations. It is also noteworthy that source C has by far the largest stellar mass of any cluster member ( > 10^ 11 M _ Rotermund21,hill2022 ). It is associated with a bright and very compact HST F160W source hill2022 , as shown in Figs. fig:field and fig:moments , and it has been suggested to be the seed of a growing BCG galaxy in this ongoing mega-merger Rennehan . \ kinematics We consider here a more detailed analysis of the kinematic properties of the B, C, and G galaxies.

Concluding remarks Thus while the radio observations do not have sufficient spatial resolution to uniquely identify one of the three galaxies as the AGN, the source properties themselves suggest source C could be a likely host, considering mainly its large stellar mass, along with narrow emission lines, and high EW(OH).

Finally, taking source C as the most likely host, we can infer the SMBH mass from the stellar mass that has been well characterized for C Rotermund21,hill2022 . For M ^* = 4 10^ 11 M _ , the SMBH mass is 7 10^ 8 M _ (e.g. ding2020 ).

sources.summary: The paper describes source G as one of the central ALMA galaxies, with a large inferred disk mass, a double-horned profile indicating a rotating disk or bar, and high inclination.

quotes.quote: Sources B and G have similarly large inferred disk masses (18 10 ^ 11 M _ ). However, the distinct double-horned profile of source G (Appendix sec:appendixB ) is direct evidence for a rotating disk or bar-like structure at high inclination (explaining the broad velocity profile), while the profile for B is possibly due to a tidal torque in response to the interaction with C. Source G also has a higher aspect ratio (2.3, major/minor axes) in moment-1 than B and C, suggesting the disk is seen closer to edge-on. Explicitly using this higher implied inclination in Eq. eq:dyn_mass_disk brings down the disk mass estimate by 25 , more consistent with the much lower gas mass of G compared with B and C.

gmw_00010698.name: source B
gmw_00010698.overview: Source B is a submillimeter-luminous galaxy located in the core region of a cluster [arXiv:2301.01375]. It has a large inferred disk mass and is likely interacting with its close neighbors, which may cause a tidal torque in its profile [arXiv:2301.01375].

sources.summary: The paper describes source B as a central submillimeter galaxy in the interacting B-C-G complex, with large inferred disk mass and the highest confirmed excitation SLED of the trio.

quotes.quote: As noted, these are three very submm-luminous sources in the core region ( S_ 850 = 6.7 mJy for B, 4.7 mJy for C, and 1.3 mJy for G), with only source A being brighter, although two even more luminous sources are present in the northern extension (sources N1 and N2; Hill20 ). The most distinguishing features of this trio (beyond their flux-ordered source names serendipitously spelling out `BCG') are their locations near the center-of-mass of the cluster core, and their immediate environment. They are very close neighbours (they lie within an arcsecond of each other), and are likely to be interacting.

Sources B and G have similarly large inferred disk masses (18 10 ^ 11 M _ ). However, the distinct double-horned profile of source G (Appendix sec:appendixB ) is direct evidence for a rotating disk or bar-like structure at high inclination (explaining the broad velocity profile), while the profile for B is possibly due to a tidal torque in response to the interaction with C.

Source B has the highest excitation SLED confirmed of the three, lying near the M82 SLED. Source B also has a stronger cool/warm gas component similar to Mrk231. However, all three are reasonably characterized with a combination of cool and warm star-forming Photo Dissociation Regions (PDR) components, and without significant XDR contributions.

gmw_00010699.name: SQB
gmw_00010699.overview: SQB is a molecular-gas peak located in the eastern-most tidal tail of the Stephan's Quintet system [arXiv:astro-ph/0508117]. It is associated with dust lanes, active star formation, and a substantial mass of molecular gas [arXiv:astro-ph/0508117].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SQB as the main molecular-gas peak in Stephan's Quintet's new tidal tail, associated with dust lanes, star formation, substantial molecular mass, and a low CO-to-H2 conversion factor.

quotes.quote: With our lower sensitivity, but larger field of view observations, we detect three clumps of CO emission in the eastern-most tidal tail of HCG 92 (Region B). Two of the clumps (SQB and SQB NW) lie directly on the tidal tail and correspond well with dust lanes observed in the HST images from . Figures overview and moment0 show these clumps overlaid on HST and DSS images, and the correspondence with the dust lanes is obvious for the clumps that lie on the optical tail.

Assuming a distance of 88 Mpc (for H _0 = 75 /Mpc) we obtain a molecular mass for the central CO peak of 4.7 10^8 \ assuming a Galactic CO-to-H _2 conversion factor. If the molecular gas in SQB is similar in its properties to the giant molecular clouds in the Milky Way then we can assume that most of the mass is in clouds 10 ^6 . In this case, SQB likely consists of 10 ^2 clouds of that mass, which are the precursors to the formation of OB associations.
This yields values of / _ Gal of 0.4, < 0.4 and < 1 for the clouds SQB, SQB SE and SQB NW, respectively.

gmw_00010700.name: SQB NW
gmw_00010700.overview: SQB NW is a newly detected clump of carbon monoxide emission located on the eastern-most tidal tail of the Stephan's Quintet galaxy group, HCG 92 [arXiv:astro-ph/0508117]. The clump aligns directly with observed dust lanes but lacks any corresponding neutral hydrogen emission [arXiv:astro-ph/0508117].

sources.summary: The paper reports SQB NW as a new CO clump on Stephan's Quintet's tidal tail, aligned with dust lanes, absent from prior PdB and IRAM maps, lacking corresponding H I emission.

quotes.quote: With our lower sensitivity, but larger field of view observations, we detect three clumps of CO emission in the eastern-most tidal tail of HCG 92 (Region B). Two of the clumps (SQB and SQB NW) lie directly on the tidal tail and correspond well with dust lanes observed in the HST images from .

The detection of SQB NW is new with our observations -- the Plateau de Bure interferometer maps of lis04 do not show it. Our detection of SQB NW falls outside the primary beam of the PdB interferometer, thus the observations of lis04 were not sensitive to this emission region. The same authors in lis02 did not observe the location of SQB NW in their IRAM 30m mapping of HCG 92. SQB NW does not have any corresponding H 1 emission in the VLA map by wil02 .
This yields values of / _ Gal of 0.4, < 0.4 and < 1 for the clouds SQB, SQB SE and SQB NW, respectively.

gmw_00010701.name: SQB SE
gmw_00010701.overview: SQB SE is a newly detected molecular gas clump located to the southeast of a tidal tail [arXiv:astro-ph/0508117]. It lies within the Arc-N neutral hydrogen cloud, offset from known star-forming regions and visible dust obscuration [arXiv:astro-ph/0508117].

sources.summary: The paper describes SQB SE as a newly detected molecular-gas clump southeast of the tidal tail, offset from known star-forming or dust features but lying within Arc-N H I gas.

quotes.quote: In addition to the two clumps in the tail, we detect a clump of molecular gas to the south-east of the tail (SQB SE). This emission region does not correspond with any previous observed star forming regions or evident dust obscuration. Also, it seem to be at a slightly different velocity than the other two regions in the tail (see Table obstable ).

While the CO emission at SQB SE does not correspond with any features visible, it does lie within the H 1 cloud designated as ``Arc-N'' by wil02 . None of the peaks in the CO emission we detect correspond with the H 1 peak of the Arc-N region.
We further point out that both the SQ tip detection by lis04 and our new detection of SQB SE similarly lie on the edges of the peak in H 1 column density.

gmw_00010702.name: HST14176
gmw_00010702.overview: HST14176 is an early-type galaxy located within a galaxy cluster [arXiv:0908.0748]. It is identified as a gravitational lens and represents a mass-to-light ratio outlier, which may be influenced by its cluster environment or uncertainties in its effective radius [arXiv:0908.0748].

sources.summary: The paper discusses HST14176 as a cluster-associated lens and mass-to-light outlier whose effective-radius uncertainty changes inferred lensing mass without materially changing the fitted relation.

quotes.quote: The lens Q2237+030 is actually the bulge of a barred galaxy. In the present study the bulge is treated as an early type galaxy. HST14176 is a part of a cluster, which is not included in the models. Another possible problem is a large uncertainty in the effective radius.

A closer look at the V-band luminosities for selected galaxies reveals that HST14176, B1608 and MG2016 emerge as outliers with mass-to-light ratios 1 . This can be explained by nearby groups and clusters (e.g. in the case of HST14176) or mass-contamination influencing the path of light. Another reason can be uncertainties in the effective radii, as already mentioned in Section sec:svs .

gmw_00010703.name: LBG 22914
gmw_00010703.overview: LBG 22914 is a high-redshift Lyman-break galaxy candidate [arXiv:2009.00024]. Its redshift of 6.319 was initially identified from a Lyman break and later confirmed by the detection of a [CII] emission line [arXiv:2009.00024].

sources.summary: The paper identifies LBG 22914 as a high-redshift candidate with a z=6.319 Lyman-break redshift, later confirmed by [CII] emission, and reports Ly-alpha limits.

quotes.quote: We also included in the masks an LBG candidate, 22914, that was not part of the original sample of 2017A&A...606A..23B , but was subsequently included after revision of the photometric errors. We measured for LBG 22914 a redshift of =6.319 based on a clear Lyman break in the spectrum. The redshift was later confirmed by the detection of a [ CII ] 158 m line in the NOEMA mm-band spectrum the DEIMOS spectrum is shown in their Fig. 1 2019A&A...631L..10D .
The upper limits of flux and equivalent width of the \ line in 22914 were estimated from the continuum S/N and the spectral resolution following the recipe of 2009A&A...493...39M .

gmw_00010704.name: LBG 20654
gmw_00010704.overview: LBG 20654 is a high-redshift Lyman-break galaxy located at a redshift of z = 6.226 [arXiv:2009.00024]. Its authenticity was confirmed by the detection of an unresolved emission line in both FORS2 and DEIMOS spectra [arXiv:2009.00024].

sources.summary: The paper describes LBG 20654 as a z=6.226 high-redshift LBG whose unresolved emission line was detected with FORS2 and also appears in a DEIMOS spectrum.

quotes.quote: Nine candidate high-z galaxies from 2017A&A...606A..23B were included in the observed slitmask: three of them were confirmed to be genuine high-redshift ( > 5.7) LBGs, other two showed very faint (S/N 2) features compatible with a redshift larger than six (but one of the two unsecure identifications was LBG 20654, subsequently confirmed by deeper FORS2 observations), while the remaining four were too noisy or even undetected.

In the LBG 20654 spectrum an unresolved emission line is detected with a formal S/N=9 at the observed wavelength of 8787 , placing this objects at = 6.226. The line is also detected, albeit with much lower significance, in a DEIMOS spectrum, further confirming its authenticity.

gmw_00010705.name: LBG 18262
gmw_00010705.overview: LBG 18262 is a high-redshift Lyman-break galaxy located at a redshift of z=6.238 [arXiv:2009.00024]. Observations of the galaxy reveal a slightly resolved Lyman-alpha emission line that exhibits a characteristically asymmetric profile with a red wing [arXiv:2009.00024].

sources.summary: The paper reports LBG 18262 as a high-redshift LBG with a slightly resolved, asymmetric Ly-alpha emission line at z=6.238, using the emission peak for redshift.

quotes.quote: The FORS2 spectrum of LBG 18262 shows a slightly resolved emission line, detected with S/N=8 at the observed wavelength of 8801 . The line is undoubtedly asymmetric with a red wing, a typical shape of high-redshift \ emission lines, and yields a redshift of = 6.238 for this source.

The redshift value is consistent, within the errors, with that obtained from the Gaussian fit of the line, apart from the two objects (LBG 18262 and LAE2) in which \ has an asymmetrical profile and the peak position is known to provide a more correct redshift estimation.

gmw_00010706.name: HDF147
gmw_00010706.overview: HDF147 is a massive, high-redshift radio galaxy located at a redshift of z=1.918 [arXiv:0902.1528, arXiv:1301.1253]. Its radio emission is dominated by a compact active galactic nucleus core [arXiv:0902.1528, arXiv:1301.1253] with a faint, one-sided southern jet [arXiv:0902.1528].

sources.summary: The paper presents HDF147 as a massive z~2 radio galaxy whose compact MERLIN morphology and weak dust/X-ray signatures favor a low-luminosity AGN over star formation.

quotes.quote: We present high spatial resolution MERLIN 1.4GHz radio observations of two high redshift (z 2) sources, \ (HDF147) and \ (HDF130), selected as the brightest radio sources from a sample of submillimetre-faint radio galaxies. They have starburst classifications from their rest-frame UV spectra. However, their radio morphologies are remarkably compact ( < 80mas and < 65mas respectively), demanding that the radio luminosity be dominated by Active Galactic Nuclei (AGN) rather than starbursts. Near-IR imaging ( HST NICMOS F160W) shows large scale sizes (R _ 1/2 0.75 , diameters 12kpc) and SED fitting to photometric points (optical through the mid-IR) reveals massive ( 5 10 ^ 11 M _ ), old (a few Gyr) stellar populations. Both sources have low flux densities at observed 24 and are undetected in observed 70 \ and 850 , suggesting a low mass of interstellar dust.

Muxlow et al. (2005) classify HDF147 (J123623+621642) as an AGN candidate and note that it has a faint one-sided radio emission extending 0.6 \ to the south of the compact core. This core and jet-like emission can clearly be seen in Figure 1.

sources.summary: The source is identified with an I=23.9 galaxy at z=1.918; its steep-spectrum compact emission, radio excess, and milliarcsecond core indicate concentrated radio luminosity.

quotes.quote: VLA J123623+621642 has a steep radio spectrum ( =0.63 ) and its MERLIN-VLA image shows a compact component with weak extended (sub-arcsec) emission R00,muxlow05 . The radio source is identified with an I=23.9 galaxy at z=1.918 ; its radio and infrared data indicate a radio-excess. As inferred from the VLBI observations, a substantial fraction of the radio luminosity is contained within its milliarcsec-scale radio core.

gmw_00010707.name: SDSS J170007.17+375022.2
gmw_00010707.overview: SDSS J170007.17+375022.2 is a spiral galaxy located at a redshift of 0.063 [arXiv:1005.3223]. Its bulge contains a relatively old stellar population, while its radio emission is likely produced by star formation within the spiral arms [arXiv:1005.3223].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSS J170007.17+375022.2 as the lone spiral in a low-concentration subsample, with old bulge light and radio emission likely tied to spiral-arm star formation.

quotes.quote: Only one galaxy is indeed a spiral (SDSS J170007.17+375022.2) at a redshift of 0.063. However, the 3 SDSS fiber covers only its bulge, returning a D_ n (4000) = 1.75, typical of a rather old stellar population. This is the value used to locate this source in the D_ n (4000) vs. L_ 1.4GHz /M_ * plane. We argue that a substantial fraction of its radio emission can originate from younger star within the spiral arms, an idea supported by the lack of a central source in the FIRST image.

gmw_00010708.name: SDSS J133453.37-013238.5
gmw_00010708.overview: SDSS J133453.37-013238.5 is a radio-loud active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1005.3223]. It is associated with a radio source that exhibits a clear Fanaroff-Riley II morphology extending over 90 kiloparsecs [arXiv:1005.3223].

sources.summary: The paper describes SDSS J133453.37-013238.5 as a radio-loud AGN with clear 90 kpc FR II structure, while questioning its low black-hole-mass estimate.

quotes.quote: One of them (SDSS J133453.37-013238.5) is associated with a radio source of clear FR II morphology extended over 90 kpc, an unmistakable sign of the presence of a RLAGN. Nonetheless, the quoted SMBH masses are not actual measurements, but estimates derived from the stellar velocity dispersion, _* . Since this relation has an intrinsic scatter, in a population with a low-mass cut-off in black hole masses, a tail of low _* is indeed expected. Without a prior knowledge of SMBH masses distribution it is difficult to estimate whether our fraction of low _* objects ( 1 of the sample) is consistent with this interpretation.

We then used the relation between the host and SMBH masses derived by marconi03 to obtain a independent M_ BH measurement. Their host masses are in the range log M_*/M_ =10.54 - 10.99 corresponding to log (M_ BH /M_ ) 7.93 - 8.37 .

gmw_00010709.name: SMM J1237+6203
gmw_00010709.overview: SMM J1237+6203 is a high-redshift ultraluminous infrared galaxy at a redshift of z=2.07 [arXiv:0911.0014]. The galaxy hosts an active galactic nucleus and exhibits a large-scale energetic outflow traced by broad, extended oxygen emission [arXiv:0911.0014].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SMM J1237+6203 as a z~2 ULIRG/quasar with strong submillimetre/radio properties and broad, extended [O III] emission tracing a large-scale energetic outflow.

quotes.quote: In this paper we present Gemini-North NIFS IFU observations of the [ O iii ] emission from a z 2 quasar (SMM J123716.01+620323.3, hereafter referred to as SMM J1237+6203) that is hosted in an Ultraluminous Infrared (IR) Galaxy (ULIRG; L_ IR >10^ 12 L_ ; e.g., \ Sanders Mirabel 1996).

The optical position of SMM J1237+6203 is _ 2000 = 12 ^ h 37 ^ m 16 0, _ 2000 = +62^ 03 ^ 23 2 (Barger et al. 2003). SMM J1237+6203 has a submm flux of S _ 850 =5.3 1.7 mJy (Chapman et al. 2005), is identified with a moderately bright VLA radio counterpart (S _ 1.4 =164.0 11 Jy; Biggs Ivison 2006), and has an optical spectroscopic redshift of z=2.07 (Barger et al. 2003). The rest-frame 1.4 GHz luminosity ( 3 10^ 24 W Hz ^ 1 ) suggests a rest-frame far-IR luminosity of 6 10^ 12 L_ , under the assumption of the local radio-far-IR luminosity correlation (Alexander et al. 2005a; e.g., \ Helou et al. 1985; Condon 1992), identifying SMM J1237+6203 as a ULIRG.

The broad [ O iii ] luminosity of SMM J1237+6203 is comparable to the HzRGs studied by Nesvadba et al. (2006, 2007a, 2008; [ 1--8 ] 10^ 43 erg s ^ 1 ). In Fig. 2 we show the [ O iii ] intensity, narrow [ O iii ] velocity (measured with respect to the systemic redshift), and line-free optical continuum of SMM J1237+6203, measured from the NIFS data cube. The [ O iii ] emission is strongly peaked and co-spatial with the optical continuum peak, as would be expected for an optically luminous AGN.

gmw_00010710.name: F 1
gmw_00010710.overview: F 1, also known as UCD-FORS 81, is an ultracompact dwarf galaxy [arXiv:1510.05010, arXiv:1011.1852]. Structural analysis reveals a faint envelope [arXiv:1510.05010], while stellar population studies indicate an old age [arXiv:1011.1852].

sources.summary: The paper measures UCD-FORS 81/Fornax UCD2 structural parameters, contrasts them with prior HST fits, and reports improved double-profile residuals with a visible envelope.

quotes.quote: For UCD-FORS 81 ( = Fornax UCD2) we find an effective radius of r_ eff =37.82 2.58 pc and a Sérsic index of n=2.03 . For this object a much higher Sérsic index of n=6.8 and an effective radius of 26.6 pc was derived in , based on higher resolution HST photometry. High Sérsic indices are very sensitive to the determination of the sky level, and already a slight underestimation will lead to a higher Sérsic index, which might cause this difference. Another explanation for this discrepancy is that an intrinsic double profile was fitted with a single component in their work.

The double profile fit is especially good for UCD-FORS 32, 45 and 81, for which a faint envelope is even visible from the images in figure fig:extended by eye. For illustrating the improvement in residuals for the double component fits, those three objects have been included in the last three panels of figure fig:surfprofiles and all of them show significant improvement of the residuals when using a K+S profile instead of a single Sérsic.

sources.summary: The paper compares F 1 (UCD 2) with previous FLAMES measurements, finding consistent velocity dispersion but discrepant metallicity and age estimates likely explained by age-metallicity degeneracy and data quality.

quotes.quote: Three objects in our sample, F 1 (UCD 2), F 19 (UCD 3), and F 24 (UCD 4), have published measurements of internal kinematics and stellar populations CCB08 obtained using exactly the same data analysis technique, i.e. the NBursts full spectral fitting FLAMES-LR04 spectra having similar although slightly wider wavelength range but twice lower spectral resolution. The velocity dispersion measurements agree remarkably well for all three objects. The metallicity measurements agree within a few hundredths dex for UCD 3 and UCD 4, however being discrepant by 0.4 dex for UCD 2. For this object, the age estimations also differ: intermediate in CCB08 and old in our present study. We notice, however, that the discrepancy of the UCD 2 age and metallicity measurements in CCB08 and our present study follow the age--metallicity degeneracy.

gmw_00010711.name: F 24
gmw_00010711.overview: F 24, also known as UCD 4, is classified as an ultracompact dwarf galaxy [arXiv:1011.1852]. Spectroscopic observations of the galaxy have provided measurements of its internal velocity dispersion, metallicity, and stellar age [arXiv:1011.1852].

sources.summary: The paper compares F 24 (UCD 4) to prior work, reporting good agreement in velocity dispersion and close agreement in metallicity and age between studies.

quotes.quote: Three objects in our sample, F 1 (UCD 2), F 19 (UCD 3), and F 24 (UCD 4), have published measurements of internal kinematics and stellar populations CCB08 obtained using exactly the same data analysis technique, i.e. the NBursts full spectral fitting FLAMES-LR04 spectra having similar although slightly wider wavelength range but twice lower spectral resolution. The velocity dispersion measurements agree remarkably well for all three objects.

The metallicity measurements agree within a few hundredths dex for UCD 3 and UCD 4, however being discrepant by 0.4 dex for UCD 2. For this object, the age estimations also differ: intermediate in CCB08 and old in our present study. We notice, however, that the discrepancy of the UCD 2 age and metallicity measurements in CCB08 and our present study follow the age--metallicity degeneracy. Given quite poor data quality and large size of stellar population confidence levels in CCB08 we conclude that the discrepancy between the measurements can be explained by statistical effects. Age determinations for UCD 3 and UCD 4 agree between the two studies within 2 of their statistical uncertainties.

gmw_00010712.name: J1211+743
gmw_00010712.overview: J1211+743 is a giant radio galaxy characterized by a one-sided jet and two asymmetric radio lobes [arXiv:1206.4843]. One lobe is a Fanaroff-Riley type II lobe with a hotspot, while the other is a diffuse relic lobe that correlates with the direction of the jet [arXiv:1206.4843].

sources.summary: The paper explains J1211+743 as a giant radio galaxy whose one-sided jet and asymmetric lobes are best understood through restarted nuclear activity plus orientation-dependent light-travel effects.

quotes.quote: J1211+743 is a giant radio galaxy with a one-sided jet and two asymmetric lobes, one of which is of Fanaroff-Riley (FR) type II with a hotspot and the other is a diffuse relic devoid of a hotspot. The jet points towards the latter lobe, which is difficult to explain in a standard way within the double-lobed radio source paradigm. Here, I propose to assume that the nucleus of J1211+743 has undergone a re-ignition of activity and its lobes, presumably both originally of FR II type, represent an earlier active phase, while the jet represents the current one. The asymmetry of the lobes is a consequence of the orientation of the source combined with an activity switch-off that occurred between two active periods.

The relic lobe is on the near side with regard to the observer, whereas the radiation from the far-side lobe arrives significantly later owing to its longer distance to the observer. The far-side lobe is thus perceived to have not yet decayed. On the other hand, the jet behaves in a standard way, i.e. its projected orientation reflects the near side of the source. Hence, we are able to explain why the location of the relic lobe correlates with the direction of the jet.

gmw_00010713.name: ID=7
gmw_00010713.overview: ID=7 is a high-quality strong gravitational lens candidate with a faint, very red central deflector galaxy [arXiv:2004.02715]. This deflector has a high photometric redshift of 0.86 and a large Einstein radius of approximately 5 arcseconds, which may indicate a dark-matter-rich system or a small galaxy group [arXiv:2004.02715].

sources.summary: The paper highlights ID=7 as a high-quality lens candidate with a faint red deflector, large Einstein radius, high photo-z, and possible dark-matter-rich or small-group nature.

quotes.quote: Finally, the CNN has captured some larger Einstein radii from group/clusters like ID=7 which shows a very faint and very red central deflector but a relatively large Einstein radius ( 5'' ), with 3 arc-like images on the left and one point-like image on the right. The deflector has a high photo-z ( z_ phot =0.86 , the highest in the candidate list), which is coherent with the red color and the compact size. This is likely to be a dark matter rich system with one of the largest arc separation from an individual galaxy, especially considering the high redshift of the deflector.

However, we can not exclude the possibility that this system is a galaxy group, since there at least three reddish objects in the vicinity of the lens galaxy candidate. If their redshifts are comparable with that of the central object, then this could be a lensing event from a small group, justify in this way the larger Einstein Radii. We have checked the photometric redshifts and this does not look to be the case.

gmw_00010714.name: J0843
gmw_00010714.overview: J0843 is a merging active galactic nucleus host galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.0541 [arXiv:1902.07560]. The system exhibits tidal features, a stellar velocity gradient, and complex ionized gas kinematics that are likely disturbed by the ongoing merger process [arXiv:1902.07560].

sources.summary: The paper describes J0843 as a merging AGN host with tidal features, extended H emission, a stellar velocity gradient, and ionized gas kinematics disturbed by the merger.

quotes.quote: At z = 0.0541, the GMOS FOV of J0843 covers a 3.6 5.4 kpc region with a spatial resolution of 0.7 kpc. J0843 appears as a merging galaxy with tidal features. [ O iii ] emission is detected (with S/N > 3) in the central 3.6 2.6 kpc region, while H emission is more extended. The stellar velocity map shows a velocity gradient in the NW-SE direction. The ionized gas velocity map is complex, probably disturbed by the merging process.

gmw_00010715.name: J1019
gmw_00010715.overview: J1019 is a slightly inclined disk galaxy hosting an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1902.07560]. Its gas velocity map shows an opposite trend compared to its stellar rotation, which may indicate either gas counter-rotation or AGN-driven biconical outflows [arXiv:1902.07560].

sources.summary: The paper describes J1019 as a slightly inclined disk AGN host whose gas and stellar velocity maps differ, consistent with counter-rotation or AGN-driven biconical outflows.

quotes.quote: At z = 0.0647, the GMOS FOV of J1019 covers a 4.3 6.4 kpc region with a spatial resolution of 0.83 kpc. With the axis ratio b/a=0.60, J1019 appears as a slightly inclined disk galaxy elongated in the N-S direction. [ O iii ] and H emission present a similar spatial distribution with a symmetric shape along the NE-SW direction.

The stellar velocity map reveals a clear rotation pattern with the kinematic major axis along the NE-SW direction. The gas velocity map shows an opposite trend compared to that of stars, possibly suggesting a counter-rotation. The bi-conical outflows driven by AGNs is consistent with the observed gas kinematics, if the outflow is along the kinematic major axis.

gmw_00010716.name: J1058
gmw_00010716.overview: J1058 is a face-on barred disk galaxy that hosts an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1902.07560]. While its stars exhibit a clear rotation pattern, the kinematic major axis of the ionized gas is misaligned by 40 degrees, likely due to active galactic nucleus-driven outflows [arXiv:1902.07560].

sources.summary: The paper describes J1058 as a face-on barred disk AGN host with extended H emission, stellar rotation, and gas-stellar kinematic misalignment attributed to AGN-driven outflows.

quotes.quote: At z = 0.0397, the GMOS FOV of J1058 covers a 2.7 4.0 kpc region with a spatial resolution of 0.53 kpc. J1058 is a face-on disk galaxy with a b/a=0.89 along with a clear bar structure. H emission (with S/N > 3) is distributed in the central 2.7 2.9 kpc region, which is more extended than the distribution of [ O iii ] emission. The stellar velocity map reveals a rotation pattern. The kinematic major axis of the ionized gas is misaligned with respect to that of the stars by 40 degree, which could be due to the bi-conical outflows driven by AGN.

gmw_00010717.name: J1156
gmw_00010717.overview: J1156 is a face-on disk galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.0800 [arXiv:1902.07560]. The galaxy hosts an active galactic nucleus that dominates photoionization at its center, while star formation and LINER emission are present at the edges of its emission line region [arXiv:1902.07560].

sources.summary: The paper describes J1156 as a face-on disk AGN host with compact line emission, unclear stellar rotation, redshifted gas velocities, and central AGN photoionization.

quotes.quote: At z = 0.0800, the GMOS FOV of J1156 covers a 5.3 8.0 kpc region with a spatial resolution of 1.6 kpc. J1156 is a face-on disk galaxy with b/a= 0.84. [ O iii ] and H emission is concentrated within the 4.0 kpc region. The stellar velocity map shows no clear rotation pattern while the gas velocity map shows positive (redshifted) velocities, which may be signatures of outflows, but the velocity and velocity dispersion of the ionized gas are relatively small. AGN photoionization is dominant at the center, while there are signatures of LINER emission and star-formation at the edge of the emission line region.

gmw_00010718.name: J1311
gmw_00010718.overview: J1311 is a face-on disk galaxy at a redshift of z = 0.0873 that features an outer ring structure [arXiv:1902.07560]. The galaxy hosts an active galactic nucleus, with stellar rotation along the east-west direction and possible signatures of outflows [arXiv:1902.07560].

sources.summary: The paper describes J1311 as a face-on ringed disk AGN host with centrally concentrated [O iii], extended H emission, stellar rotation, and possible weak outflow signatures.

quotes.quote: At z = 0.0873, the GMOS FOV of J1311 covers a 5.8 8.7 kpc region with a spatial resolution of 1.1 kpc. With b/a= 0.85, J1311 appears as a face-on disk galaxy with an outer ring structure. [ O iii ] emission is centrally concentrated in the 4 kpc region.

In contrast, H emission is more extended, including a bright central component and a weak flux region, that is reaching out to the edge of the FOV. The stellar velocity map reveals a rotation pattern with the kinematic major axis along the E-W direction. The H velocity map is similar to the stellar velocity map, while the [ O iii ] velocity map is dominated by positive (redshifted) velocities, which could be due to the AGN-driven outflows.

gmw_00010719.name: J1617
gmw_00010719.overview: J1617 is a face-on disk galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.1020 [arXiv:1902.07560]. It hosts an active galactic nucleus, with stellar and gas rotation patterns that are closely aligned [arXiv:1902.07560].

sources.summary: The paper describes J1617 as a face-on disk AGN host with central [O iii], more extended H emission, aligned stellar and gas rotation, and AGN-dominated photoionization.

quotes.quote: At z = 0.1020, the GMOS FOV of J1617 covers a 6.8 10.3 kpc region with a spatial resolution of 2 kpc. With b/a= 0.85, J1617 appears as a face-on disk galaxy. [ O iii ] emission is concentrated within the central 2 kpc region, while H emission is more extended.

The stellar velocity map reveals a rotation pattern with the kinematic major axis along the NW-SE direction. The gas velocity map is similar to the stellar velocity map, showing a similar rotation pattern. The BPT classification map shows AGN dominated photoionization.

gmw_00010720.name: M135131.98-101932.90
gmw_00010720.overview: M135131.98-101932.90 is a quasar characterized by a double-lobe radio structure [arXiv:2109.00576]. The active galaxy is surrounded by a symmetric Lyman-alpha halo that may be associated with infalling gas [arXiv:2109.00576].

sources.summary: The paper identifies M135131.98-101932.90 as a radio-powerful quasar with extended Lyα halo emission, symmetric Lyα morphology, and possible infalling gas.

quotes.quote: M135131.98-101932.90 ( 16) This quasar is among top three in our sample in terms of radio size, radio power and L_ bol (see Table  tab_luminosities ) and with \ line clearly detected in its spectra (see Fig.  fig_heiifit ). We have obtained spectra along PA = 0 \ and 157 , where PA = 157 \ is aligned with the axis of radio emission (see Fig.  fig_ps1 ). We detect the \ halo in both these spectra (see Fig.  fig_lya_samp1 ).

Therefore, total extent of the \ halo is 84 kpc (distance between the outer most contours on either sides of the quasar trace) along PA=157 \ and has a total luminosity of 2.84 10^ 43 . As mentioned above the radio emission is extended with a double lobe structure (see Fig. fig_ps1 ). The separation between the WISE location and peak of the South-East and North-West lobes are \ 28 kpc and 58 kpc with peak flux densities of \ 0.41 and 0.09 mJy b^ 1 , respectively. Clearly the observed \ emission is more symmetric than the radio emission with respect to optical source. The radio emission is extended beyond the \ halo in the North-west direction whereas it is well within the \ halo (in projection) in the South-East direction.
Thus it is possible that the \ halo is associated with an infalling gas and with minimal interaction with the radio emission.

gmw_00010721.name: M210143.29-174759.20
gmw_00010721.overview: M210143.29-174759.20 is a highly radio-luminous quasar that exhibits strong nuclear helium II emission [arXiv:2109.00576]. It is surrounded by an asymmetric extended Lyman-alpha halo that is significantly brighter and more extended toward the northwest [arXiv:2109.00576].

sources.summary: The paper describes M210143.29-174759.20 as the sample’s most radio-luminous quasar, with asymmetric extended Lyα emission and possible jet-cloud interaction.

quotes.quote: M210143.29-174759.20 ( 22) As can be seen from Table  tab_radio_props , this sources has the largest radio luminosity in our sample. However, it has the fourth lowest bolometric luminosity and inferred \ continuum luminosity in our sample (see Table  tab_luminosities ). Thus naively one expects the photoionization to be less efficient compared to other sources in the list. However, we do see a strong nuclear He  ii emission in this quasar (see Fig.  fig_heiifit ).

Extended \ halo is detected along both the PAs, with maximum extension of 53 kpc and luminosity 2.35 10^ 44 \ along PA = 112 . In this case the \ halo is found to be asymmetric both in terms of size and luminosity. It is much brighter (i.e by more than 40 times) and extended (i.e up to 30 kpc from the quasar trace) in the North-West direction (see Fig.  fig_lya_samp1 ) compared to that in the South-East direction. The FWHM of this extended \ emission is found to be 1259 .

gmw_00010722.name: M114226.58-263313.70
gmw_00010722.overview: M114226.58-263313.70 is a large radio-loud quasar with high bolometric and Lyman continuum luminosities [arXiv:2109.00576]. It is surrounded by an asymmetric Lyman-alpha halo, which contains perturbed gas and exhibits extended carbon IV and helium II emission [arXiv:2109.00576].

sources.summary: The paper describes M114226.58-263313.70 as a large radio quasar with asymmetric Lyα halo emission, detectable extended C IV and He II, and perturbed gas.

quotes.quote: M114226.58-263313.70 ( 10) As can be seen from Table  tab_radio_props , this sources has the second largest linear size for radio emission in our sample. This also has the fifth highest bolometric luminosity and Lyman continuum luminosity in our sample (see Table  tab_luminosities ). The nuclear He  ii emission is clearly detected in this quasar (see Fig.  fig_heiifit ).

The \ halo is clearly detected showing asymmetry both in the size and the \ luminosity. The \ halo luminosity ( ) of this source is one of the highest in our sample, with luminosity of 1.7 10^ 44 \ and halos size of 68 kpc. The emission is asymmetric being more extended and brightest along South-East direction. The emitting gas is perturbed with FHWM \ 1007 , indicating interaction with the radio source. As discussed before, this is the only quasar in our sample where we also detect the extended \ emission.

gmw_00010723.name: SGAS J105039.6+001730
gmw_00010723.overview: SGAS J105039.6+001730 is a lensed Lyman-break galaxy at a redshift of z=3.6252 [arXiv:1703.00354, arXiv:1908.06832]. It is classified as a Green Pea galaxy [arXiv:1703.00354] with a high star formation rate and no substantial active galactic nucleus contribution [arXiv:1908.06832].

sources.summary: The paper describes SGASJ105039.6+001730 as a lensed z=3.6252 Green Pea with high ionization line ratios but negligible escape fraction implied by high neutral hydrogen column density.

quotes.quote: Another interesting object is the Green Pea SGASJ105039.6+001730 at z=3.6252 studied by Bayliss et al. (2014). This galaxy has a magnification factor of 30 , with an absolute magnitude of M_ 1500 =-20.5 after corrections for strong lensing. Like the previous example, this object also shows line ratios ( [ OIII ] / [ OII ] >10 and [ OIII ] /H >10 ) and is expected to emit ionizing radiation with a large escape fraction, due to the significant ionization efficiency. Surprisingly, the spectroscopic data indicate that the neutral column density is \ N_ H I >21.5 and thus the escape fraction is negligible ( f^ rel _ esc <1 ). It is worth noting that the profile of the Lyman- line in emission is also compatible with an associated absorption, in agreement with the measured high column density of neutral hydrogen.

sources.summary: The paper describes SGAS J105039.6+001730 as a z=3.6252 lensed Lyman-break galaxy with large magnification, measured stellar mass, high SFR, no substantial AGN contribution, and abundance measurements.

quotes.quote: The SGAS J105039.6+001730 LBG at redshift z=3.6252 is a system lensed by a foreground galaxy cluster at z=0.593 . The lensing magnification is of the order of 30 . By taking into account the magnification factor, Bayliss14 derived for the galaxy a stellar mass of 5 10^9 M_ and values of the SFR between 90 and 140M_ yr^ 1 (depending if based on [ OII ] or H_ luminosity, respectively). The Magellan/FIRE interstellar emission spectrum ruled out a substantial AGN contribution. Abundances in Table t:abundance_data are taken from Magellan/FIRE spectrum Bayliss14 .

gmw_00010724.name: SMACS J0304.3-4402
gmw_00010724.overview: SMACS J0304.3-4402 is a Lyman-alpha emitting galaxy at a redshift of 1.963 [arXiv:1908.06832]. It is gravitationally magnified by a factor of 42.0 by a foreground galaxy cluster and shows no active galactic nucleus contribution in its rest-frame ultraviolet spectrum [arXiv:1908.06832].

sources.summary: The paper describes SMACS J0304.3-4402 as a z=1.963 Ly-alpha emitter with high lensing magnification, stellar mass, emission-line SFR estimates, no AGN contribution, and abundance ratios.

quotes.quote: SMACS J0304.3-4402 This Ly emitter at redshift z=1.963 is magnified by a factor =42.0 8.0 by a galaxy cluster placed between redshift 0.3 and 0.5 Christensen12a . SED fitting reveals a stellar mass of 6.3 10^ 10 M_ . The inferred SFR from emission lines lies between 20 (friom the H_ detection) and 90 M_ yr^ 1 (from the [ OII ] detection). The rest frame UV spectrum shows no AGN contribution. The abundance ratios presented in Table t:abundance_data are from the VLT/X-Shooter emission line spectrum by Christensen12a,Christensen12b .

gmw_00010725.name: SMACS J2031.8-4036
gmw_00010725.overview: SMACS J2031.8-4036 is a strongly lensed Lyman-break galaxy and Lyman-alpha emitter at a redshift of approximately 3.5 [arXiv:1703.00354, arXiv:1908.06832]. It has a stellar mass of 2.4 billion solar masses, no active galactic nucleus contribution, and a star formation rate between 15 and 30 solar masses per year [arXiv:1908.06832].

sources.summary: The paper presents SMACS J2031.8-4036 ID 1.1 as a strongly lensed z about 3.5 LBG with a constrained LyC escape fraction, low metallicity, high ionization, and promise for UV follow-up.

quotes.quote: reported the case of a lensed LBG (SMACS J2031.8-4036 ID 1.1) at z 3.5 with a LyC escape fraction estimate from their X-shooter spectrum. This galaxy has an absolute magnitude, after correction for magnification, of M_ 1500 =-20.4 and the constraint on the escape fraction is f^ rel _ esc 3-11 . Their conservative upper limit for the escape fraction, thanks to strong lensing magnification, is fully consistent with the individual limits on z 3.3 galaxies at the depth of the CANDELS/GOODS-North U-band observations of this paper. The spectral properties of this peculiar galaxy are similar to the ones observed in Ion2 by , and usually associated with possible LyC emissivity: this galaxy indeed shows low metallicity ( Z=0.1 Z_ ) and a high ionization parameter ( logU=-2.1 ), presenting high [ OIII ] / [ OII ] and [ OIII ] /H line ratios. Following recent claims ( ), a much larger escape fraction should be expected for this object.

sources.summary: The paper describes SMACS J2031.8-4036 as a z=3.51 Ly-alpha emitter with lensing magnification, measured stellar mass, no AGN contribution, SFR estimates, and oxygen abundances.

quotes.quote: SMACS J2031.8-4036 is a Ly emitter at redshift z=3.51 . It is magnified by a factor =15.8 7.0 by a galaxy cluster at z=0.331 Christensen12a . The SED fitting reveals a stellar mass of 2.4 10^9 M_ . Its VLT/X-Shooter emission line spectrum indicates no AGN contribution and a SFR between 15 and 30 M_ yr^ 1 , inferred via [ OII ] and H_ detection, respectively ( Christensen12a,Christensen12b ). The abundance data in Table t:abundance_data were drived from the VLT/X-Shooter spectrum.

gmw_00010726.name: ESO 157-49
gmw_00010726.overview: ESO 157-49 is a nearby, low-mass edge-on disk galaxy [arXiv:2201.00566]. It exhibits extraplanar diffuse ionized gas with kinematic asymmetry and a positive radial lag gradient, which may be related to interactions with its nearby dwarf companion, ESO 157-48 [arXiv:2201.00566].

sources.summary: The paper studies ESO 157-49 with MUSE and H-alpha imaging, finding eDIG kinematic asymmetry and suggesting its positive lag gradient may relate to interaction with nearby ESO 157-48.

quotes.quote: We investigate the origin of the extraplanar diffuse ionized gas (eDIG) and its predominant ionization mechanisms in five nearby (17--46 Mpc) low-mass ( 10^9 - 10^ 10 M_ ) edge-on disk galaxies: ESO 157-49, ESO 469-15, ESO 544-27, IC 217, and IC 1553. We acquired Multi Unit Spectroscopic Explorer (MUSE) integral field spectroscopy and deep narrowband H imaging of our sample galaxies. To investigate the connection between in-plane star formation and eDIG, we measure the star formation rates (SFRs) and perform a photometric analysis of our narrowband H imaging. Using our MUSE data, we investigate the origin of eDIG via kinematics, specifically the rotation velocity lags. We also construct standard diagnostic diagrams and emission-line maps (EW(H ), [ N   ii ] /H , [ S   ii ] //H , [ O   iii ] /H ) and search for regions consistent with ionization by hot low-mass evolved stars (HOLMES) and shocks.

ESO 157-49 has three smaller dwarf neighbors with similar recession velocities, the closest of them being ESO 157-48 located 14.2 kpc projected distance southwest of the galaxy, while the others have projected distances greater than 100 kpc. The direction to ESO 157-48 is parallel to the plane of ESO 157-49, and ESO 157-49 has a positive radial lag gradient on the side nearest to ESO 157-48. The asymmetry and the positive lag gradient of ESO 157-49 could be thus related to interactions with this dwarf companion.

gmw_00010727.name: IC 217
gmw_00010727.overview: IC 217 is a low-mass, edge-on disk galaxy [arXiv:2201.00566]. It is classified as marginally star-forming, with its ionization consistent with OB stars [arXiv:2201.00566].

sources.summary: The paper studies IC 217 with MUSE and H-alpha imaging, finding marginal star formation, disturbed but model-like lag profiles, and BPT ionization consistent with OB stars.

quotes.quote: We investigate the origin of the extraplanar diffuse ionized gas (eDIG) and its predominant ionization mechanisms in five nearby (17--46 Mpc) low-mass ( 10^9 - 10^ 10 M_ ) edge-on disk galaxies: ESO 157-49, ESO 469-15, ESO 544-27, IC 217, and IC 1553. We acquired Multi Unit Spectroscopic Explorer (MUSE) integral field spectroscopy and deep narrowband H imaging of our sample galaxies. To investigate the connection between in-plane star formation and eDIG, we measure the star formation rates (SFRs) and perform a photometric analysis of our narrowband H imaging. Using our MUSE data, we investigate the origin of eDIG via kinematics, specifically the rotation velocity lags. We also construct standard diagnostic diagrams and emission-line maps (EW(H ), [ N   ii ] /H , [ S   ii ] //H , [ O   iii ] /H ) and search for regions consistent with ionization by hot low-mass evolved stars (HOLMES) and shocks.

ESO 157-49, ESO 469-15, and IC 217 are also marginally star-forming when star-forming galaxies are defined as those with sSFR > 10^ 10.6 yr ^ 1 hsieh2017manga , while ESO 544-27 falls into the green valley. For IC 217 and the southwestern side of ESO 544-27 however, while the measured profiles are disturbed, they more or less follow the model profile and do not deviate from it systematically. All the ionization in IC 217 falls into the OB star regime on the BPT diagram.

gmw_00010728.name: J0919+4906
gmw_00010728.overview: J0919+4906 is a star-forming galaxy located at a redshift of 0.40512 [arXiv:2207.14328]. It is a confirmed emitter of ionizing photons that exhibits spatial variation in its dust attenuation, nebular ionization, and photon escape fraction [arXiv:2207.14328].

sources.summary: The paper presents spatially resolved spectroscopy of J0919+4906, a z=0.40512 LyC-emitting galaxy, measuring Mg II, ionization, dust attenuation, and large spatial variation in inferred escape fraction.

quotes.quote: Here, we use HET/LRS2 observations of J0919+4906, a confirmed z 0.4 emitter of ionizing photons to achieve spatially resolved (12.5 kpc in diameter) spectroscopy of Mg II 2796 , Mg II 2803 , [ O II ] 3727,3729 , [ Ne III ] 3869 , H , [ O III ] 4363 , H , [ O III ] 4959 , [ O III ] 5007 , and H . From these data we measure Mg II emission, which is a promising indirect tracer of ionizing photons, along with nebular ionization and dust attenuation in multiple spatially-resolved apertures. We find that J0919+4906 has significant spatial variation in its Mg II escape and thus ionizing photon escape fraction. Combining our observations with photoionization models, we find that the regions with the largest relative Mg II emission and Mg II escape fractions have the highest ionization and lowest dust attenuation. Some regions have an escape fraction that matches that required by models to reionize the early universe, while other regions do not.

We observed J0919+4906 (hereafter J0919, RA: 09:19:55.78, Dec: +49:06:08.75) over 3 nights (January 8th 2021, January 9th 2021, and March 3rd 2021) with four total exposures using the LRS2 spectrograph Chonis_2014,Chonis_2016 on the Hobby-Eberly Telescope. J0919 has a stellar mass of 10^ 7.51 M , a star formation rate of 8.4 M yr 1 , and a redshift of 0.40512 Izotov_2021 .

gmw_00010729.name: J134738.24 + 474301.9
gmw_00010729.overview: J134738.24 + 474301.9 is a low-mass active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2401.15895]. It features multiple spatially distributed radio components that appear to constitute an extended jet in a roughly linear configuration [arXiv:2401.15895].

sources.summary: The paper highlights J134738.24 + 474301.9 as a low-mass AGN whose distributed radio components plausibly form an extended jet deserving deeper high-resolution observations.

quotes.quote: The second is J134738.24 + 474301.9 in the deep FIRST subsample, for which we need two Gaussian components to fit. Interestingly, it has been actually recorded also in the LoTSS-DR2 catalog yet without a multi-component tag. We inspect its LoTSS image, and see multiple radio sources within a radius 28 \ (namely, a projected physical radius of 33.6 kpc) without any other optical counterparts. Thus the spatially distributed radio emission is of the same source plausibly, with the brightest component J134738.24 + 474301.9 located in the center; all these components appear to constitute a jet, roughly in a linear configuration. The LoTSS-DR2 and FIRST images, with its optical image from the SDSS (shown as contours) overlaid, are shown in Figure fig:special_sources .

The low-mass AGNs with extended jets are very few so far, and valuable for jet physics (see, e.g., SDSS J095418.15+471725.1 by 2022MNRAS.516.6123G ). Thus J134738.24 + 474301.9 deserves deep and high-resolution pointed radio observations in the future.

gmw_00010730.name: PKS 0316-444
gmw_00010730.overview: PKS 0316-444 is a central cD galaxy that hosts a strongly absorbed active galactic nucleus [arXiv:astro-ph/0404088]. It is associated with a radio source featuring two sharply bent tails that extend nearly 100 kiloparsecs [arXiv:astro-ph/0404088].

sources.summary: The paper links PKS 0316-444 to Abell 3112's central cD galaxy, describing a strongly absorbed nuclear AGN and a VLA radio source with bent tails nearly 100 kpc long.

quotes.quote: The distortions seen near the center in the Chandra image probably signify a dynamical interaction between the ICM and the powerful radio source hosted by the central cD galaxy. The nuclear X-ray point source with a radio counterpart is characterized as a strongly absorbed AGN (Takizawa et al. 2003).

The large negative declination of the bright radio source PKS 0316-444 associated with the cD galaxy, has adversely affected the resolution of the VLA map (Takizawa et al. 2003). Nonetheless, the map shows a strong nuclear component and two sharply bent radio tails with an overall extent approaching 100 kpc, similar to the radio galaxy in Abell 2029.

gmw_00010731.name: SDSS J015830.25+125528.1
gmw_00010731.overview: SDSS J015830.25+125528.1 is a faint, low-mass dwarf host galaxy located at a redshift of approximately 0.0324 [arXiv:2305.09417]. It has an estimated stellar mass of 20 million solar masses and a stellar population with roughly half-solar metallicity [arXiv:2305.09417].

sources.summary: The paper characterizes SDSS J015830.25+125528.1 as SN 2016ije's faint, low-mass host galaxy, deriving its redshift, distance, low luminosity, stellar mass, age, and roughly half-solar metallicity.

quotes.quote: Host Galaxy To determine the redshift of the faint host galaxy of SN 2016ije, we used Keck II + DEIMOS fab03 to take a spectrum of SDSS J015830.25+125528.1 on 2017 October 14.59. The host-galaxy spectrum is shown in Figure fig:host , where two prominent emission lines are visible at 5169.92 and 6774.43 . Assuming that they are [ O iii ] 5007 and H , respectively, then we get a consistent redshift of z = 0.0324 0.0003. The corresponding luminosity distance is d_L = 136.5 2.9 Mpc and the distance modulus is m - M = 35.68 0.04 mag when adopting cosmological parameters _ m = 0.27 , _ = 0.73 , and H _0 = 73 1.4 km s ^ 1 Mpc ^ 1 riess21 . With this distance, and the grz band images and catalogs from the Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS; SDSS ), we derive the absolute magnitude of SDSS J015830.25+125528.1 to be 14.5 mag in g , which is much fainter than most host galaxies of 02es-like SNe ( 19 to 22 mag; whi15 ).

By fitting the spectrum with combinations of single-burst stellar population models using Firefly wil17 , we obtained a stellar mass of M_ * 2.0 10^ 7 M_ , an age of 1.54 Gyr, and metallicity of the stellar populations of [ Z/H ] = -0.37 . The low mass of the host galaxy can explain its low luminosity. The metallicities that were determined from different approaches are roughly consistent, which indicates that the host galaxy has half the solar metallicity.

gmw_00010732.name: F0023-0904
gmw_00010732.overview: F0023-0904 is a radio galaxy with a spectroscopic redshift of 0.946 [arXiv:0909.1566]. It exhibits [OII] emission, and its radio morphology suggests a possible jet structure offset from the galaxy [arXiv:0909.1566].

sources.summary: Detected in all bands, F0023-0904 has spectroscopic redshift 0.946, [OII] emission, and radio morphology offset toward the galaxy, suggesting a possible jet structure.

quotes.quote: F0023-0904 was detected in all bands, giving a photometric redshift of 1.49 with an Sc SED. Spectroscopy revised the redshift to 0.946 with an [ OII ] emission detection. The FIRST radio peak is offset about 3 '' from the galaxy, but the outer contours are elongated in the direction of the galaxy, suggesting a jet structure. VLA X-band morphology was inconclusive, but provided a spectral slope of -1.04.

gmw_00010733.name: F0129-0140
gmw_00010733.overview: F0129-0140 is an active galactic nucleus host galaxy with a compact, moderate radio flux and faint optical emission [arXiv:0909.1566]. Its photometric redshift is estimated at 2.44, supported by a rough spectroscopic redshift of 1.7 [arXiv:0909.1566].

sources.summary: F0129-0140 has ambiguous photometric redshifts, rough spectroscopy near 1.7, and compact radio plus faint optical properties that led the authors to prefer the higher-redshift solution.

quotes.quote: F0129-0140 had 2 solutions for photometric redshifts at 0.65 with an SB2 SED and 2.44 with an SB4 SED. Without any discernible break, it was not obvious which is the better solution. Additionally, spectroscopy gives a rough redshift of 1.7 from the spectral slope in the 5500-8000 Angstrom range which does not break the degeneracy. A compact moderate radio flux with faint optical flux suggests a higher redshift as the low redshift would give such a low luminosity ( 9*10^9L_ sun ), it would be difficult to reconcile with an AGN host. We will use the high photometric redshift solution because it was more constrained than the spectroscopic fit.

gmw_00010734.name: F0152-0029
gmw_00010734.overview: F0152-0029 is a galaxy detected only in the K band [arXiv:0909.1566]. It hosts a compact, steep-spectrum, high-luminosity radio source that suggests the presence of an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:0909.1566].

sources.summary: F0152-0029 is detected only in K band; its compact, steep-spectrum, high-luminosity radio source is interpreted as consistent with a high-redshift radio galaxy.

quotes.quote: F0152-0029 was undetected in all bands except K. It does not have a constrained redshift so is either at z > 8.0 where the Lyman break is redshifted out of z-band (see figure 20) or a highly reddened object. The radio source is compact with a relatively high radio luminosity (25mJy) and steep spectral index (-1.09), suggesting an AGN source.

gmw_00010735.name: F0202-0021
gmw_00010735.overview: F0202-0021 is a low-redshift elliptical galaxy located at a redshift of approximately 0.6 [arXiv:0909.1566]. It exhibits an elongated radio morphology and a steep radio spectrum, which is consistent with a giant elliptical galaxy hosting an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:0909.1566].

sources.summary: F0202-0021 is best fit as a low-redshift elliptical, with brightness, K-z inference, and spectroscopy supporting z about 0.6 over a secondary high-redshift solution.

quotes.quote: F0202-0021 appears to be well fit by a low redshift elliptical SED ( z=0.58 ), but also has a secondary solution at z=4.2 . The brightness and redshift inferred from the K-z relation derived in section 2.4 ( z 0.9 ) supported the first solution and was roughly confirmed with spectroscopy ( z=0.6 ). An elongated radio morphology and lower radio flux (3mJy) with a steep spectrum also is consistent with a giant elliptical with an AGN at low redshift.

gmw_00010736.name: F0916+1134
gmw_00010736.overview: F0916+1134 is a galaxy located at a redshift of 0.78 [arXiv:0909.1566]. Surface brightness fitting residuals indicate that it is likely a barred spiral galaxy [arXiv:0909.1566].

sources.summary: F0916+1134 is described as a likely barred spiral whose photometric fit and spectroscopic [OIII] detection place it at redshift 0.78.

quotes.quote: F0916+1134 appears to be a barred-spiral from the residuals in surface brightness fitting (Stalder Chambers stalder2009a , in prep.). K-band photometry was excluded from photo-z fitting (due to the poor residuals in the profile fit), which found a best redshift of 1.12 with a starburst SED. A confirmed [ OIII ] 3727 \ detection with spectroscopy gives a final redshift of 0.78.

gmw_00010737.name: F0942+1520
gmw_00010737.overview: F0942+1520 is a galaxy detected only in the H and K bands [arXiv:0909.1566]. Its large H-K color, high radio flux, and double-lobed morphology suggest it is a high-redshift active galactic nucleus [arXiv:0909.1566].

sources.summary: F0942+1520 is detected only in H and K; its color, high radio flux, and double-lobed morphology support a high-redshift AGN interpretation.

quotes.quote: F0942+1520 was undetected in all bands except H and K. Because of the large H-K color, Hyperz could fit the break with a redshift of 3 to 6.5. The high radio flux (12mJy) and double-lobe morphology is consistent with a high-redshift AGN.

gmw_00010738.name: F0952+2405
gmw_00010738.overview: F0952+2405 is a galaxy detected in both the I and H bands [arXiv:0909.1566]. It is bright in the H band, hosts a faint radio source, and is likely not at a high redshift [arXiv:0909.1566].

sources.summary: F0952+2405 is detected in I and H, is bright in H, and is interpreted as probably not high-redshift based on its faint radio source and K-z estimate.

quotes.quote: F0952+2405 was detected in both I and H bands. It is bright in H band, and a faint radio source (1.3mJy). It is probably not at high redshift (the K-z relation gives likely redshift of z=0.76).

gmw_00010739.name: F1008-0605
gmw_00010739.overview: F1008-0605 is a low-redshift elliptical galaxy [arXiv:0909.1566]. It is relatively bright across all observed filters, including the B, V, R, I, and H bands [arXiv:0909.1566].

sources.summary: F1008-0605 is bright in all observed filters; the paper rejects an extreme high-redshift starburst interpretation and adopts a low-redshift elliptical solution.

quotes.quote: F1008-0605 was detected and relatively bright in all observed filters (B, V, R, I, and H). A break was found and fit by Hyperz to either a z=0.27 elliptical SED or z=3.9 SB4 SED with little extinction. If the high redshift solution were correct, it would be hard to reconcile this particular object with a hierarchical merging scenario especially since it would have an extremely luminous stellar population ( >10^ 13 L_ ) dominating its rest frame UV light, which has to be assembled in less than 2.3Gyr (age of the universe at that redshift). We therefore adopt the low redshift solution.

gmw_00010740.name: F1010+2527
gmw_00010740.overview: F1010+2527 exhibits a close double morphology in most bands [arXiv:0909.1566]. Although there is no observed evidence of interaction, the similar high photometric redshifts of the two components suggest they are associated, and both are consistent with starburst spectral energy distributions [arXiv:0909.1566].

sources.summary: F1010+2527 has close double morphology, similar high photometric redshifts for both components, starburst SEDs, and a low-flux unresolved radio source.

quotes.quote: F1010+2527 has a close double morphology in most bands. There was not any observed evidence of interaction, but the photometric redshifts of the two galaxies were similar ( z=4.41 , z=4.63 ), which suggests they are associated. Both are consistent with starburst SEDs. The south source's parameters were used in the subsequent analyses because the fit was better constrained. The unresolved, low flux (1mJy) radio source also supports that this is not an aligned object.

gmw_00010741.name: F1014+1438
gmw_00010741.overview: F1014+1438 is a faint galaxy at a high redshift of z=3.95 [arXiv:0909.1566]. It is classified as a starburst galaxy and hosts a double-lobed, moderate-flux radio source consistent with an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:0909.1566].

sources.summary: F1014+1438 is treated as a high-redshift starburst after spectroscopy shows a steep break, with double-lobed moderate radio emission consistent with a high-redshift AGN.

quotes.quote: F1014+1438 appears to be a starburst at high redshift ( z=3.95 ), but a low redshift ( z=0.5 ) solution was also found. Because of the faintness of the source and the presence of a steep break observed with spectroscopy, we chose the high redshift solution. Double-lobe moderate flux (33mJy integrated) radio source is also consistent with a high redshift AGN.

gmw_00010742.name: F1027+0520
gmw_00010742.overview: F1027+0520 is a bright galaxy that is fit by a starburst spectral energy distribution template at a redshift of 0.6 [arXiv:0909.1566]. However, it may potentially lie at a lower redshift and hosts a compact radio source with relatively high flux [arXiv:0909.1566].

sources.summary: F1027+0520 is fit by a z=0.6 starburst SED but may lie at lower redshift; it has a compact, relatively high-flux radio source.

quotes.quote: F1027+0520 was fit to a z=0.6 starburst SED template. It is a bright galaxy, so is potentially at lower redshift due to our low confidence in our photometric measurements for bright extended objects and determining a good sky level around them. It has a compact radio source with relatively high flux (23mJy).

gmw_00010743.name: F1218-0625
gmw_00010743.overview: F1218-0625 is a galaxy detected in the V, R, I, z', and H bands [arXiv:0909.1566]. It exhibits a compact moderate 20-cm radio flux of 4.3 mJy and a faint optical flux, which suggests a high-redshift nature [arXiv:0909.1566].

sources.summary: F1218-0625 is detected from V through H, has broad photometric-redshift possibilities, and is considered more likely high-redshift because of compact radio and faint optical flux.

quotes.quote: F1218-0625 was detected in V, R, I, z ' and H. Two photometric redshift solutions were found though any redshift between 0 and 4.5 gives reasonable fits. The compact moderate 20-cm flux (4.3mJy) and faint optical flux suggest the higher redshift is more likely, but with a large possible range.

gmw_00010744.name: F1315+4438
gmw_00010744.overview: F1315+4438 is a high-redshift radio galaxy candidate [arXiv:0909.1566]. It is the faintest detected object in its candidate sample, with a slightly distorted radio morphology that may indicate radio emission from star formation [arXiv:0909.1566].

sources.summary: F1315+4438 is the faintest detected HzRG candidate; faint optical flux favors its higher photometric-redshift solution, though radio morphology leaves a lower-redshift alternative.

quotes.quote: F1315+4438 is the faintest detected object in the HzRG candidate sample ( K=21.32 ). Hyperz found 2 photometric redshifts, a SB1 SED at z=1.1 and a SB2 SED at z=2.8 . The extremely faint optical flux favors the high redshift solution though a low radio flux (0.9mJy) with a slightly distorted morphology may indicate that the radio emission is from star formation. However, this interpretation would support an even lower redshift than z 1 .

gmw_00010745.name: F1451+0556
gmw_00010745.overview: F1451+0556 is an unresolved galaxy at a redshift of 2.567 with a peculiar color [arXiv:0909.1566]. This color and the galaxy's compactness are attributed to active galactic nucleus influence on its spectral energy distribution [arXiv:0909.1566].

sources.summary: F1451+0556 is unresolved with peculiar color; spectroscopy gives redshift 2.567 and the paper attributes its SED color and compactness to AGN influence.

quotes.quote: F1451+0556 is unresolved and has a peculiar color ( R-K=0.27 ), which most resembles a young starburst SB2 SED at z=2.71, though not a very good fit. Spectroscopy confirms a redshift of 2.567 with several emission lines. This suggests significant AGN influence on the SED of this galaxy explaining the peculiar color and compactness.

gmw_00010746.name: F1505+4457
gmw_00010746.overview: F1505+4457 is a spiral galaxy with a spectral energy distribution consistent with an Sa classification [arXiv:0909.1566]. It exhibits a double-lobed radio morphology, which suggests the presence of a radio jet oriented nearly perpendicular to the plane of the sky [arXiv:0909.1566].

sources.summary: F1505+4457 has conflicting photometric and spectroscopic redshifts, an optical ID between two radio sources, and double-lobed radio morphology consistent with moderate redshift.

quotes.quote: F1505+4457 has an SED consistent with an Sa galaxy at low redshift ( z=0.55 ) though spectroscopy data suggests a higher redshift (z=1.02). The optical ID lies between the 2 sources (which are at different fluxes. This seems to suggest a radio jet with the axis almost perpendicular to the plane of the sky with the north lobe pointed away. With a total 20-cm radio of about 17mJy, with double-lobe morphology, it is consistent with this moderate redshift.

gmw_00010747.name: F1524+5122
gmw_00010747.overview: F1524+5122 is a radio galaxy with a high radio luminosity of 22 mJy, which suggests the presence of an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:0909.1566]. The object is undetected in the K-band and may be a highly reddened or very high-redshift host galaxy [arXiv:0909.1566].

sources.summary: F1524+5122 is undetected to a K-band limit, has high radio luminosity suggesting an AGN, and may be a very high-redshift or highly reddened host galaxy.

quotes.quote: F1524+5122 is undetected with 5 limit at K>20.96 . The relatively high radio luminosity (22mJy) suggests an AGN. It is potentially a very high redshift object, possibly the host galaxy could be highly reddened.

gmw_00010748.name: F2217-0837
gmw_00010748.overview: F2217-0837 is a galaxy with two spectral energy distribution solutions, representing either a low-redshift elliptical galaxy or a high-redshift starburst [arXiv:0909.1566]. The low-redshift elliptical interpretation is favored, supported by the presence of a weak, elongated radio source [arXiv:0909.1566].

sources.summary: F2217-0837 has low- and high-redshift SED solutions; the paper favors the low-redshift elliptical interpretation, supported by a weak elongated radio source.

quotes.quote: F2217-0837 has 2 Hyperz solutions, a z=0.3 elliptical and z=4.1 SB2 SED. The low redshift solution seems more likely as it is would otherwise be an extremely bright ( >10^ 13 L_ sun ) galaxy at high redshift. A weak (1mJy), elongated radio source also supports the low redshift solution.

gmw_00010749.name: F1234+2001
gmw_00010749.overview: F1234+2001 is a high-redshift starburst galaxy candidate at a redshift of approximately 5.4 [arXiv:0909.1566]. It is identified as the host of the radio source FIRST J123432.9+200134 [arXiv:0909.1566].

sources.summary: F1234+2001 is accepted as the host of FIRST J123432.9+200134; photometry and spectra indicate a compact z about 5.5 starburst radio-galaxy candidate.

quotes.quote: For the remainder of the paper we accept that optical/IR source, F1234+2001, to be the host of J123432.9+200134. The confidence in this ID is strengthened from the subsequently derived redshifts. It should be noted that a diffraction spike passes through the optical ID in V, R, I, and z ' . GALFIT successfully modeled this and the good residuals raise our confidence in the photometric measurements. The best fit SED to the broadband photometry is a z=5.4 starburst mainly from fitting the Lyman break between R and I bands.

The GMOS spectroscopy does show a strong break around 7900 \ and confirms our photometric redshift fit that the Lyman break is between R and I bands. The signal to noise ratio of the spectrum was insufficient to identify any absorption features. The SpeX spectroscopy also shows the Lyman break though just at the edge of sensitivity.

gmw_00010750.name: F1237+1141
gmw_00010750.overview: F1237+1141 is a starburst galaxy located at a redshift of approximately 3.01 [arXiv:0909.1566]. The galaxy exhibits a triple radio morphology at low radio flux, and its spectral energy distribution is consistent with a high-redshift starburst system [arXiv:0909.1566].

sources.summary: F1237+1141 is interpreted as a z about 3 starburst source with triple radio morphology; IRAC, MIPS, and spectroscopy refine the photometric-redshift solution.

quotes.quote: F1237+1141 is consistent with a moderately high redshift ( z 2.5 ) starburst SED. The radio morphology is a triple system at low radio flux (1mJy) with the ID corresponding with the center source consistent with this redshift. It was also observed with both IRAC and MIPS, which changed the best fit redshift to 2.9 to reflect the 5.8 m data point, but with large error bars.

The SED beautifully fits a z=2.9 starburst with a long wavelength starburst SED (M82) from a rest wavelength of 110 nm to 40 microns. This is a superb illustration of the potential of Spitzer for both photometric redshifts and studies of the stellar environment of these objects. Spectroscopy better constrained the redshift to 3.01.

gmw_00010751.name: SDSS J0013+1523
gmw_00010751.overview: SDSS J0013+1523 is a quasar located at a redshift of z = 0.120 [arXiv:1002.4991]. It acts as a strong gravitational lens, magnifying a background source at a redshift of z = 0.640 [arXiv:1002.4991].

sources.summary: The paper presents SDSS J0013+1523 as a z=0.120 QSO lens whose host-galaxy mass and velocity dispersion can be constrained from lensed background emission.

quotes.quote: We present the first case of strong gravitational lensing by a QSO : at z=0.120 . The discovery is the result of a systematic search for emission lines redshifted behind QSOs, among 22 298 spectra of the SDSS data release 7. Apart from the z = 0.120 spectral features of the foreground QSO, the spectrum of also displays the \ and H emission lines and the \ doublet, all at the same redshift, z = 0.640 . Using sharp Keck adaptive optics K-band images obtained using laser guide stars, we unveil two objects within a radius of 2 \ from the QSO. Deep Keck optical spectroscopy clearly confirms one of these objects at z = 0.640 and shows traces of the emission line of the second object, also at z = 0.640 .

If the z = 0.120 QSO host galaxy is modeled as a singular isothermal sphere, its mass within the Einstein radius is M_E(<1 h^ 1 kpc) = 2.16 10^ 10 \ h^ 1 \ M_ and its velocity dispersion is _ SIS = 169 . This is about 1- away from the velocity dispersion estimated from the width of the QSO H emission line, _*(M_ BH )= 124 47 . Deep optical HST imaging will be necessary to constrain the total radial mass profile of the QSO host galaxy using the detailed shape of the lensed source.

gmw_00010752.name: J1234 + 3901
gmw_00010752.overview: J1234 + 3901 is a distant, luminous star-forming galaxy located at a redshift of 0.1330 [arXiv:1902.01775]. It is one of the lowest-metallicity galaxies known, hosting a single high-excitation H II region with an oxygen abundance near 7.0 [arXiv:1902.01775].

sources.summary: J1234 + 3901 is identified as one of the lowest-metallicity SDSS star-forming galaxies, with a high-excitation H II region and direct-method abundance near 7.0.

quotes.quote: Two of these galaxies, J0811 + 4730 (see also Sect. sec:method ) and J1234 + 3901 are very close to or below the luminosity-weighted value 12 + logO/H = 7.0, representing the lowest-metallicity galaxies known so far.

One of the lowest-metallicity SFGs among selected SDSS galaxies is J1234 + 3901. A single high-excitation H ii region is observed in this galaxy with an oxygen abundance of 6.98 and 7.03 derived by the strong and direct methods, respectively. The SDSS spectra of this galaxy and another lowest-metallicity galaxy J0811 + 4730 from Table tab2 are shown in Fig. fig5 . The [ O iii ] 4363 emission line is clearly detected in both spectra allowing the oxygen abundance determination by the direct method. The galaxy J1234 + 3901 at the redshift of 0.1330 is one of the most distant and luminous galaxies in Table tab2 .

gmw_00010753.name: AGC 111945
gmw_00010753.overview: AGC 111945 is a low-surface-brightness dwarf irregular galaxy that appears as a diffuse blob with a slightly brighter central condensation [arXiv:0808.1789]. Although it lacks an obvious disk and has no major HII regions, the galaxy exhibits very low, diffuse hydrogen-alpha line emission [arXiv:0808.1789].

sources.summary: The paper describes AGC 111945 as a low-surface-brightness dwarf irregular with diffuse H emission, a poor two-burst fit, and a required recent star-formation event.

quotes.quote: AGC 111945: this dIrr galaxy shows up only as a low surface brightness blob with a slightly brighter central condensation. Since it does not show an obvious disk, it was not possible to measure the PA of its major axis. The galaxy has no prominent HII regions yet shows an H line flux of 2.98 10^ 15 erg/cm ^ 2 /s produced a diffuse H component. The line emission is very low; this is why we do not show a nH image in Figure fig:AGC111945 .

gmw_00010754.name: AGC 111946
gmw_00010754.overview: AGC 111946 is a low-surface-brightness galaxy with a disk-like light distribution [arXiv:0808.1789]. It features a faint, elongated area of enhanced hydrogen-alpha emission and experienced a star-formation burst approximately 1.5 billion years ago [arXiv:0808.1789].

sources.summary: The paper describes AGC 111946 as a low-surface-brightness disk-like galaxy with faint elongated H emission and a recent star-formation burst only 2.5 Myr ago.
