sources.summary: The paper derives fluxes for J142917+332626, identifies a nearby Chandra counterpart, and describes it as one of the few unambiguous MPS turnover sources with an X-ray counterpart.

quotes.quote: J142917+332626 is present in both the NVSS and dVMR image cutouts, but not in the catalogues because there is another source 35 arcsec away which blends with it. To determine its flux density in NVSS we simultaneously fit it and the nearby source using the pybdsm source detection package http://dl.dropboxusercontent.com/u/1948170/html/index.html . From this we found that it has an integrated flux density of 6.96 1.01 mJy, which differs by 2.5 per cent from the FIRST value. J142917+332626 was also observed by Ciliegi1999 at 1.4 GHz with the VLA in C configuration.

Matching the VLA-P sources to the Chandra XBo o tes X-ray survey of the Bo o tes field Murray2005 with a search radius of 10 arcsec, J142917+332626 was the only source from our 11 sources for which we found a counterpart. The centroid of the matched source, CXOXB J142917+332626.4, is 0.32 arcsec away from the VLA-P source and was detected in all three of Chandra's bands with seven, one and six counts in the 0.5--7, 0.5--2 and 2--7 keV bands, respectively. We note that, since J142917+332626 can unambiguously be classified as an MPS source (Fig. fig:colour-colour ), it is one of the few turnover sources with a X-ray counterpart. Matching the FIRST sources to those in XBo o tes, Bouchefry2009 associated CXOXB J142917+332626.4 with the same FIRST source to which we matched the VLA-P source.

gmw_00010871.name: J143042+351240
gmw_00010871.overview: J143042+351240 is a non-variable galaxy hosting an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1604.00171]. Its European VLBI Network flux density is lower than predicted, suggesting the source has a larger true size surrounded by low surface brightness emission [arXiv:1604.00171].

sources.summary: The paper finds that J143042+351240 recovers only a small fraction of predicted EVN flux and likely has a true size larger than the tabulated value.

quotes.quote: J143042+351240's EVN flux density of 0.2 mJy is only 4 1 per cent of the predicted value. Tapering the data did not increase J143024+352438's flux density significantly. Since the emission is related to AGN activity and J143042+351240 is not variable (Sections subsec:agn star and subsec:variability ), this leads us to conclude that the true size of J143042+351240 is larger than indicated in columns (7) and (12) in Table tbl:derived_info . If this is the case, the remaining flux density could be in low surface brightness emission surrounding the source. In this case the true source size will be larger than the measured value, but likely not significantly.

gmw_00010872.name: J143050+342614
gmw_00010872.overview: J143050+342614 is an active galactic nucleus with radio emission driven by AGN activity [arXiv:1604.00171]. The source is non-variable and is classified as a megahertz-peaked spectrum source [arXiv:1604.00171].

sources.summary: The paper recovers only one-third of J143050+342614's predicted EVN flux, concludes the source is larger than measured, and classifies it as an MPS source.

quotes.quote: We only recovered 33 per cent of the predicted flux density for J143050+342614. This percentage did not increase when we applied an uv-taper to the image. The emission is related to AGN activity and the source is not variable (Sections subsec:agn star and subsec:variability ). As is the case with J143042+351240, this leads us to conclude that J143050+342614 is larger than indicated in columns (7) and (12) in Table tbl:derived_info . We note that J143050+342614 can unambiguously be classified as an MPS source when including the errors on its spectral indices.

gmw_00010873.name: J143329+355042
gmw_00010873.overview: J143329+355042 is an active galactic nucleus detected as a single-component radio source [arXiv:1604.00171]. Its radio spectrum flattens toward lower frequencies and may turn over [arXiv:1604.00171].

sources.summary: The paper describes J143329+355042 as a single-component EVN source whose recovered flux indicates a reliable size and whose spectrum may turn over.

quotes.quote: J143329+355042 is composed of a single component in the EVN image and lies outside the survey area covered by the dVMR catalogue. We recovered 92 9 per cent of J143329+355042's predicted flux density. This indicates that its measured size is a good estimate of its true size. J143329+355042's spectrum (shown in Fig. fig:spectra ) flattens toward lower frequencies and could turn over (Table tbl:targets ).

gmw_00010874.name: J142850+345420
gmw_00010874.overview: J142850+345420 is a high-redshift active galactic nucleus likely located at a redshift greater than two [arXiv:1604.00171]. High-resolution radio observations resolve the source into a double structure, which is interpreted as an active nucleus with a core-jet morphology [arXiv:1604.00171].

sources.summary: The paper resolves J142850+345420 into a double EVN source, recovers nearly all predicted flux after tapering, and interprets it as an AGN with core-jet structure.

quotes.quote: J142850+345420, shown in Fig. fig:J142850+345420 , has a double structure in the EVN image with J142850+345420a and J142850+345420b being detected at a 14 and 5 level, respectively. Fig. fig:J142850+345420 was made using natural weighting and applying an uv-taper. The full-resolution, uniformly weighted image has a typical resolution of 3 10 mas, but we could not confirm the detection of J142850+345420b. Applying the uv-taper increased the beam size to 24.1 26.8 mas and resulted in the flux density of both components increasing. Specifically, the flux density of J142850+345420a increased by a factor of 1.7 and the percentage of recovered flux density of the source as a whole increases from 44 to 99 per cent.

Consequently, we could not determine a photometric redshift for it. This likely indicates that J142850+345420 is at z>2 . If this is the case, J142850+345420 is AGN dominated since we calculated in Section subsec:agn star that the radio emission would be the result of AGN activity if the source is at z>0.06 .

gmw_00010875.name: J143055+350852
gmw_00010875.overview: J143055+350852 is a radio source composed of two distinct components [arXiv:1604.00171]. It likely exhibits a core-jet morphology with an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1604.00171], and its radio spectrum shows a low-frequency turnover [arXiv:1604.00171].

sources.summary: The paper describes J143055+350852 as a two-component source with AGN-like brightness temperature, likely core-jet morphology, and a low-frequency spectral turnover.

quotes.quote: J143055+350852, shown in Fig. fig:J143055+350852 , is composed of two components. The eazy and lrt redshift values for J143055+350852 differ significantly. Despite this, the brightness temperature of J143055+350852a is consistent with it being an AGN.

Considering that J143055+350852b is extended in the direction parallel to the line between the components and that it is fainter than J143055+350852a, we suggest that J143055+350852 has a core--jet structure. Since we recovered most of the predicted flux density ( 71 6 per cent), and the remaining flux density is likely located either between the two components or very near them, we do not expect that J143055+350852 is significantly more extended than indicated in Table tbl:derived_info . From Fig. fig:spectra , it is clear that the upper limit on the LOFAR-62 flux density indicates that the spectrum is turning over.

gmw_00010876.name: J143213+350940
gmw_00010876.overview: J143213+350940 is an active galactic nucleus that exhibits a likely symmetric double structure [arXiv:1604.00171]. High-resolution imaging reveals that both components have brightness temperatures consistent with active galactic nucleus activity and are surrounded by extended emission [arXiv:1604.00171].

sources.summary: The paper finds J143213+350940 has a likely symmetric double EVN structure, AGN-like components, extended emission, and MPS classification depending on the adopted low-frequency flux.

quotes.quote: The uniformly weighted EVN image of J143213+350940 (Fig. fig:J143213+350940 ) has a likely symmetric double structure. Both components have brightness temperatures consistent with emission from AGN activity. After imaging J143213+350940 with uniform weighting, we also imaged it using natural weighting with an uv-taper. Note that the information given for J143213+350940 in Table tbl:derived_info was derived from the naturally weighted, uv-tapered image, while the information for J143213+350940a,b was derived using the uniformly weighted image. Because the beam size of the uv-tapered image is 24 29 mas and J143213+350940a,b are only separated by 31 mas, their flux densities merge in the uv-tapered image.

Hence, while the source is resolved, it is clear that there is a significant amount of extended emission between and surrounding the components. From the uv-tapered image, we recover 94 12 per cent of the predicted flux density. Hence, the size measured from the uv-tapered image is a good estimate of J143213+350940's true size.

gmw_00010877.name: J143718+364549
gmw_00010877.overview: J143718+364549 is an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1604.00171]. High-resolution radio observations show that the source consists of a single resolved component that is significantly extended toward the north [arXiv:1604.00171].

sources.summary: The paper describes J143718+364549 as a resolved EVN source extended northward, with tapering recovering additional flux and a spectrum that turns over or flattens.

quotes.quote: We selected J143718+364549 for observation based on its WENSS flux density. Unfortunately, because it is 2 7 from the phase center of the VLA-P image, we do not have a VLA-P flux density for it. The naturally weighted EVN image of J143718+364549 (Fig. fig:J143718+364549 ) shows that the source is composed of a single resolved component that is significantly extended towards the North.

To check if any flux density is resolved out in the naturally weighted image, we made an image in which we applied an uv-taper. The values derived from this image is reported in Table tbl:derived_info . Tapering increased the recovered flux density from 2.9 mJy to 6.1 mJy, resulting in 68 9 per cent of the predicted flux density being recovered.

gmw_00010878.name: J144230+355735
gmw_00010878.overview: J144230+355735 is an active galactic nucleus with a central core-jet structure and two lobes or hotspots [arXiv:1604.00171]. High-resolution imaging reveals that the source is composed of three resolved components and exhibits a compact symmetric object structure [arXiv:1604.00171].

sources.summary: The paper describes J144230+355735 as a three-component CSO-like source with a core, lobes, jet-like extension, and an AGN core-jet interpretation.

quotes.quote: From its spectrum (Fig. fig:spectra ), the source can either be turning over or flattening towards lower frequencies. The EVN image of J144230+355735 (Fig. fig:J144230+355735 ) is composed of three resolved components and has a CSO structure. J144230+355735's overall structure is that of a core with two lobes. From J144230+355735b a jet-like structure extends towards J144230+355735c. To check if any of the flux density is resolved out in the naturally weighted image, we made an image in which we applied an uv-taper.

Furthermore, the brightness temperatures of all three components are lower limits as they were calculated using a redshift of zero since we could not determine a redshift for the source. Hence, if J144230+355735 is at a redshift above 0.45, the central component would clearly indicate AGN activity. Combining this with the structure of the source and the results in Section subsec:agn star , we conclude that J144230+355735 is an AGN with a central core-jet structure and two lobes and/or hotspots.

gmw_00010879.name: ID=16669
gmw_00010879.overview: ID=16669 is a compact galaxy located at a redshift of approximately 3.3 [arXiv:1509.01101]. It lies near a bright, extended foreground object that is detected in the U band, which can lead to a blended detection and an unphysically high calculated Lyman continuum escape fraction [arXiv:1509.01101].

sources.summary: The paper treats ID=16669 as a z=3.3 compact galaxy whose apparent LyC signal is produced by blending with a nearby U-band foreground object, yielding an unphysical escape fraction.

quotes.quote: Fig. obj16669ima shows as an example the cutouts in the LBC U and R bands, and the HST image in the I814W filter of the galaxy with ID=16669 in Table table:gal . The spectroscopic redshift from VUDS is associated with a compact galaxy (indicated by the green circle in the HST image on the right). The compact source falls near (distance=2.1 arcsec) a bright and extended object which is clearly detected in the U band, while at the position of the z 3.3 galaxy no flux is emitted in the U band. When computing the escape fraction with our method (i.e. detecting the galaxy in the LBC R-band and measuring the LyC flux in the LBC U-band on the same area, shown by the red ellipse in the central panel of Fig. obj16669ima ), the two galaxies are merged in a single detection in the R band, and thus our procedure gives a significant LyC detection from the U band. Translating the observed U and R fluxes into an escape fraction, it turns out that this object has f^ rel _ esc =229.5 , more than twice the maximum value allowed for a typical star forming galaxy.

gmw_00010880.name: ID=52336
gmw_00010880.overview: The high-redshift galaxy ID=52336 is a source at z=3.3 [arXiv:1509.01101]. It is likely contaminated by a foreground galaxy, which is located at a distance of 2.4 arcseconds and causes an implausibly large Lyman continuum escape fraction [arXiv:1509.01101].

sources.summary: The paper groups ID=52336 with ID=55594 as a likely contaminated z=3.3 source; its offset U-band emission gives implausibly large global and local LyC escape fractions.

quotes.quote: A similar situation happens for sources ID=52336 and 55594 (Fig. obj2ima ), where the presence of an extended galaxy and a compact object, respectively, with relatively bright U band emission but with significant offset (distance of 2.4 and 1.5 arcsec, respectively) from the z 3.3 objects (green circles) clearly indicates the superposition of foreground sources. The ``global'' escape fractions for these two galaxies are 140 and 187 , respectively. If we compute instead the ``local'' escape fraction only from the region with clear detection in the U band, we ended up with even larger escape fractions, of 966 and 654 , respectively. Even in this case the most plausible explanation is a contamination by a foreground galaxy prospectively close to the z 3.3 source.

gmw_00010881.name: ID=55594
gmw_00010881.overview: ID=55594 is a high-redshift galaxy located at a redshift of approximately 3.3 [arXiv:1509.01101]. It is likely superposed with a nearby foreground source that contaminates its U-band emission measurements [arXiv:1509.01101].

sources.summary: The paper discusses ID=55594 with ID=52336 as a likely foreground-superposed z=3.3 source, where offset U-band emission yields nonphysical global and local escape fractions.

quotes.quote: A similar situation happens for sources ID=52336 and 55594 (Fig. obj2ima ), where the presence of an extended galaxy and a compact object, respectively, with relatively bright U band emission but with significant offset (distance of 2.4 and 1.5 arcsec, respectively) from the z 3.3 objects (green circles) clearly indicates the superposition of foreground sources. The ``global'' escape fractions for these two galaxies are 140 and 187 , respectively. If we compute instead the ``local'' escape fraction only from the region with clear detection in the U band, we ended up with even larger escape fractions, of 966 and 654 , respectively. Even in this case the most plausible explanation is a contamination by a foreground galaxy prospectively close to the z 3.3 source.

gmw_00010882.overview: The galaxy 25155 is an isolated high-redshift galaxy located at a redshift of approximately 3.3 [arXiv:1509.01101]. It is considered a plausible Lyman continuum emitter with a marginal U-band detection and an escape fraction of roughly 48 percent [arXiv:1509.01101].

sources.summary: The paper considers galaxy 25155 an isolated plausible LyC emitter at z=3.3, with marginal U-band detection, escape fraction near 48, and high-redshift absorption features.

quotes.quote: The galaxy 25155 (Fig. obj25155ima ) is isolated and only checking carefully the HST I814W image there is a hint of a very faint blob slightly offset from the z=3.3 galaxy. It has a distance of 0.1 arcsec from the bright object, thus it is very hard to draw any conclusions on its true nature. We will assume in the following that it could be a genuine LyC emitter at z 3.3 . This galaxy has a relatively faint magnitude U=28.6 and it is marginally detected in the LBC COSMOS U band image at S/N=2.2 , with a resulting escape fraction of 48 . Its optical spectrum by the VUDS survey shows typical absorption lines of high-z galaxies (Ly- , SiIV, CIV) so it could resemble the ``Ion1'' source discussed in and in .

gmw_00010883.name: ID=63327
gmw_00010883.overview: ID=63327 is a high-redshift galaxy at z ~ 3.3 [arXiv:1509.01101]. High-resolution imaging reveals two blobs separated by 0.7 arcseconds, which may represent a single object, two distinct galaxies, or a foreground contaminant [arXiv:1509.01101].

sources.summary: The paper treats ID=63327 as a plausible but uncertain LyC source: U-band flux lacks an obvious foreground explanation, yet HST and deblended photometry leave contamination possible.

quotes.quote: In addition, also the detected U-band flux for galaxy ID=63327 cannot be clearly explained by the presence of a lower redshift object contaminating the z 3.3 source's line of sight. We have thus another example of a plausible ionizing source by stellar radiation, with f^ rel _ esc =44.6 and a marginal detection at U=28.5 at S/N=2.2 . Fig. obj63327ima shows this source in the LBC U band (left), in the LBC R band (center) and in the I814W band by HST (right). The HST image shows two blobs separated by 0.7 arcsec, but even at this high resolution we cannot judge whether this is a unique object or two distinct galaxies. There is still the possibility that there is a foreground contaminant roughly aligned with the z 3.3 galaxy making an apparent blend that is indistinguishable even from HST data.

For further exploring this possibility we used the high resolution of the HST I814W image to carry out a de-blended photometry both in the U and in the R band with ConvPhot, as described in the previous sections, but now adopting the HST image as a prior. We obtain an upper limit of U 29.4 at 1 for the northern blob, while for the southern region we get a marginal detection U=28.9 at 2 . Translating the latter value into an escape fraction, we obtain f^ rel _ esc =119 , thus indicating that this can be a lower redshift interloper, similar to the ones discussed in the previous section.

gmw_00010884.name: SDSS J141955.26+522741.1
gmw_00010884.overview: SDSS J141955.26+522741.1 is a broad-absorption line quasar located at a redshift of z=2.145 [arXiv:2012.07254]. The galaxy exhibits broad absorption line variability that is dominated by photoionization and strongly correlates with its continuum luminosity [arXiv:2012.07254].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes SDSS J141955.26+522741.1 as a z=2.145 BAL quasar whose line variability is photoionization-driven and shows a sharp-rise signature tracing recombination timescales.

quotes.quote: We present an analysis of the variability of broad absorption lines (BALs) in a quasar SDSS J141955.26+522741.1 at z=2.145 with 72 observations from the Sloan Digital Sky Survey Data Release 16 (SDSS DR16). The strong correlation between the equivalent widths of BAL and the continuum luminosity, reveals that the variation of BAL trough is dominated by the photoionization. The photoionization model predicts that when the time interval T between two observations is longer than the recombination timescale t_ rec , the BAL variations can be detected. This can be characterized as a "sharp rise" in the detection rate of BAL variation at T=t_ rec .

In this letter, we report the first detection of such a "sharp rise" phenomenon of the fraction curve in a BAL quasar SDSS 141955.26+522741.1 at z=2.145 from the SDSS DR16. This object has 72 observations with the signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) level at g band greater than 5 in all the epochs. This object was found to have a strong correlation between the BAL trough and the continuum flux by previous studies (e.g., hemler2019,lu2019 ).

gmw_00010885.name: [ YGK81 ] 078
gmw_00010885.overview: [ YGK81 ] 078 is a member of the NGC 3079 galaxy group [arXiv:1509.00350]. The galaxy is actively interacting with NGC 3079, which has produced an associated neutral hydrogen stream to the north of NGC 3079 [arXiv:1509.00350].

sources.summary: The paper identifies [ YGK81 ] 078 as an NGC 3079-group member with an associated H I stream north of NGC 3079, interpreting the disturbance as interaction-related.

quotes.quote: Four of the group members (MCG 9-17-9, NGC 3073, SDSS J100311.18+553557.6 (labeled A) and [ YGK81 ] 078 (labeled C)) are seen interacting with NGC 3079.

There also seems to be an \ stream associated with the galaxy seen north of NGC 3079 (( [ YGK81 ] 078; labeled C in Fig. \ f:totHI2 ); visible for velocities v=1149 and v= 1182 ). Although the outer contours in the channel maps look disturbed, the extended gas still shares the disk kinematics (e.g., v= 1182 ). The more likely source of the disturbance is an interaction between the two galaxies (see Fig. \ f:totHI2 ).
Among these, two of them ( [ YGK81 ] 078 and SDSS J100311.18+553557.6) seem to be taking part in the interactions in the group.

gmw_00010886.name: galaxy 1
gmw_00010886.overview: Galaxy 1 is a foreground galaxy located near the jets of the radio source 3C9 [arXiv:astro-ph/0406568]. It has an R-band magnitude of 19.5 and a measured redshift of z_L = 0.254 [arXiv:astro-ph/0406568].

sources.summary: The paper identifies galaxy 1 as a foreground galaxy near 3C9, adopts the foreground-galaxy redshift, and simulates its central SMBH lensing image as extremely faint.

quotes.quote: The especially interesting feature of 3C9 is that there are two foreground galaxies near the jets. One (galaxy 1) lies approximately 10 arcsec from the jets, has an R-band magnitude of 19.5, and has a measured redshift of z_L=0.254 . The other (galaxy 2) lies only about 3 arcsec from the jets, has a R-band magnitude of 23.0, and has a less well determined redshift. We will assume that the two galaxies lie at the same redshift ( z_L=0.254 ). Using a simple ray tracing algorithm, we simulated the lensing that would occur from a SMBH in the center of each galaxy.
Figure fig_structure_l1 shows the image that would result from a 10^9 M_ SMBH in the center of galaxy 1. The total flux from this image is approximately 0.03 nJy.

gmw_00010887.name: Draco
gmw_00010887.overview: Draco is a Local Group dwarf galaxy with a distance modulus of 19.49 [arXiv:astro-ph/0507329]. Its stellar population includes a metal-poor, intermediate-age population that emerges in particular between 6 and 12 arcminutes from the galactic center [arXiv:astro-ph/0507329].

sources.summary: The paper presents IJKs photometry of Draco, measuring its RGB tip, distance, metallicity spread, and regional colour-magnitude differences including an intermediate-age population.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: The faint low surface brightness elliptical Local Group galaxy Draco ( _ 2000 = 17^h20^m18.1^s , _ 2000 = 57^ 55^ 13^ ; Piatek et al. )

quotes.quote: With the William Herschel Telescope in La Palma we made IJK_ s observations of an area of about 40^ 30^ of the Local Group galaxy Draco. This allows us to describe Draco's late-type stellar population across the whole galaxy at a photometric level 2 mag deeper than the 2MASS survey. We detected the red giant branch (RGB) and measured the magnitude of the tip of the RGB in the three bands. From that in the I band we obtain a distance modulus of (m-M)_0= 19.49 0.06 ( stat) 0.15 ( sys) , in excellent agreement with a measurement from RR Lyrae stars.

The peak of the (J-K_ s )_0 histogram at different M_ K_ s suggests that Draco has a mean [ Fe/H ] =-1.95 1.26 while fiducial RGB tracks of Galactic globular clusters indicate a mean [ Fe/H ] =-1.33 0.72 where the error corresponds to the spread around the mean value. There are significant differences between the colour-magnitude diagrams of stars in the inner, medium and outer areas of the galaxy. A metal poor (Z =0.0004 ) intermediate-age population (about 1.6 Gyr old) is clearly present and emerges in particular between 6^ and 12^ from the centre of the galaxy. A few additional carbon star candidates have been identified from both their location in the colour-magnitude diagram and from an indication of variability.

gmw_00010888.name: IC 4765
gmw_00010888.overview: IC 4765 is a D galaxy located close to the Galactic plane [arXiv:astro-ph/0606165]. It dominates a poor cluster of galaxies and serves as the center of a rich, interacting sub-system [arXiv:astro-ph/0606165].

sources.summary: The paper describes IC 4765 as a poor-cluster D galaxy near the Galactic plane, dominated with IC 4749, and as the center of a richer interacting sub-system.

quotes.quote: IC 4765 Sersic 129-01, PavoII, DC1842-63, Abell S805, LGG422; ser74,dre80,abe89,gar93 is defined as a poor cluster of galaxies (Bautz-Morgan I, richness 0). However, it could also be treated as a rich group. It is located close to the galactic plane ( b 23 deg) and it is dominated by the D galaxy IC 4765 and by a barred spiral galaxy IC 4749 (SB(rs)bc and 11 arcmin south of IC 4765). Following qui75 , this group is formed by two structures in interaction: one poor, spiral rich, centered around IC 4749 and another rich sub-system centered around IC 4765.

gmw_00010889.name: NGC59
gmw_00010889.overview: NGC 59 is an early-type S0 galaxy with a central stellar velocity dispersion of 37 km/s [arXiv:1503.08054]. It exhibits active star formation and possesses an extremely low stellar metallicity alongside gas abundances of approximately one-fifth solar [arXiv:1503.08054].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes NGC59 stellar populations and emission lines, finding extreme low metallicity and sub-solar abundance ratios, star-forming emission, and roughly one-fifth solar gas abundances.

quotes.quote: From our NTT data we found that the S0 galaxy NGC59 (M _B=-15.57 ) had central stellar velocity dispersion of 37 5 kms ^ 1 and the following stellar population parameter estimates: Age=1.5 0.1 Gyr, [ Fe/H ] =-1.350 0.075 and [ /Fe ] =-0.300 0.075 dex. Whilst other LLEs that we observed also had similarly unusual stellar populations, NGC59 was the most extreme.

Skillman, Cote Miller (2003) mapped and analysed the H emission in NGC59. From H emission and GALEX FUV fluxes, Karachentsev Kaisina 2013 estimated star formation rates of SFR _ H =0.0123 M _ yr ^ 1 and SFR _ FUV =0.0063 M _ yr ^ 1 respectively, which agree quite well within their expected uncertainty of 50 per cent, confirming the star forming origin of the emission lines.

1/5 Solar, e.g. compared to Grevesse Sauval 1998) that we find for NGC 59 is reasonably consistent with the abundance 8.29 0.08 dex published by Setal08 . Our data also allow us, for the first time, to determine abundances of Ne and Cl in NGC 59, which are also approximately 1/5 Solar to within about 20 .

gmw_00010890.name: J0326
gmw_00010890.overview: J0326 is the brightest cluster galaxy of ACT-CL J0326.8 0043 [arXiv:1512.00910]. It exhibits strong optical emission lines and radio emission, which suggest it is likely a rare Type II active galactic nucleus host [arXiv:1512.00910].

sources.summary: The paper defines J0326 as the BCG of ACT-CL J0326.8 0043, finds strong optical lines and radio emission, and argues it is likely a rare Type II AGN host.

quotes.quote: The BCG (which we refer to simply as J0326 in the remainder of this section) shows strong emission lines across the optical spectrum ( f:J0326 , right panel). Because our Gemini/GMOS spectrum is not flux-calibrated we use the line measurements from the SDSS MPA/JHU Value-Added Galaxy Catalog brinchmann04 throughout. We cannot distinguish whether the emission in J0326 is dominated by star formation or an AGN from the line ratio diagnostic introduced by , appropriate for high-redshift ( z>0.4 ) objects for which H falls outside the optical wavelength range (specifically, ( O ii 3726+3729/ H )=0.57 0.03 and ( O iii 5007/ H )=-0.23 0.03 ).

The 1.4 GHz luminosity of the source associated to the BCG is L_ 1.4 /( W Hz^ 1 )=24.0 , which at face value implies a star formation rate (SFR) of several hundred yr^ 1 hopkins03 . In contrast, the [ O ii ] 3727 doublet suggests a SFR of a few tens of yr^ 1 . Systems with such marked differences in estimated SFRs are almost always AGN hosts (J. \ Brinchmann, private communication). fitted spectral energy distributions to optical SDSS data plus mid infrared data from the Wide-field Infrared Survey Explorer WISE, wright10 of one million objects.

For J0326 they estimated a best-fit star formation rate of SFR =15_ 5 ^ +10 yr^ 1 , consistent with the radio emission being dominated by nuclear activity. If this is the case then J0326 is a new Type II AGN BCG (Type I AGNs are characterized by broad components in the [ O iii ] lines), similar to the case of the BCG of ACT-CL J0320.4+0032 recently reported by and noted in s:previous . Therefore J0326 probably adds to the very sparse sample of Type II AGNs in BCGs see references in kirk15 . analyzed a 10 ks Chandra observation of ACT-CL J0326.8 0043 and found no evidence for an X-ray point source in the BCG location to suggest the presence of an AGN; however, the observations are too shallow to draw any firm conclusions.

gmw_00010891.name: BCG of ACT-CL 2050.5 0055
gmw_00010891.overview: The BCG of ACT-CL 2050.5 0055 is a brightest cluster galaxy located at a redshift of approximately 0.62 [arXiv:1512.00910]. It exhibits the highest peculiar velocity among brightest cluster galaxies in the ACT sample and is surrounded by double-peaked radio emission [arXiv:1512.00910].

sources.summary: The paper reports that the BCG of ACT-CL 2050.5 0055 has the highest peculiar velocity among ACT BCGs and is surrounded by double-peaked radio emission.

quotes.quote: ACT-CL 2050.5 0055 The BCG of ACT-CL 2050.5 0055 ( z=0.623 ; hereafter simply ``the BCG'') has the highest peculiar velocity of all BCGs in the ACT sample.

As seen in f:substructure , there are haloes in our Multidark sample that have comparable BCG velocities but they tend to have lower values of than ACT-CL 2050.5 0055. studied a sample of 452 BCGs in low-redshift clusters, and found that BCGs have, on average, =0.32 (where is the cluster velocity dispersion), with only three BCGs having velocities >2 . In comparison, the BCG of ACT-CL 2050.5 0055 has =-(3.1 0.7) _ 200 . Including the BCG in the member sample by hand increases the cluster velocity dispersion to _ 200 =(607 107) , yielding =-(2.6 0.5) _ 200 . Similarly, all the BCGs studied by have <1500 . Therefore the BCG of ACT-CL 2050.5 0055 is unique in this respect; it will be interesting to study the conditions that led to such high .
The BCG of ACT-CL 2050.5 0055 ( z=0.62 ) has a peculiar velocity of =3 _ 200 and is surrounded by double-peaked (probably point-source) radio emission.

gmw_00010892.name: NGC6286
gmw_00010892.overview: NGC 6286 is an interacting galaxy with a close companion, NGC 6285 [arXiv:astro-ph/0407511]. It features a rotating gaseous disk offset from its photometric center [arXiv:astro-ph/0312564], a bright central radio core [arXiv:astro-ph/0407511], and a superwind outflowing from its central region [arXiv:astro-ph/0312564].

sources.summary: The paper studies NGC6286 in detail, finding an offset rotating disk, extended emission, high mass, and a superwind-driven bipolar outflow rather than a forming polar ring.

quotes.quote: The observations of NGC6286 with a long-slit spectrograph (UAGS) near the H_ line revealed the rotation of the gaseous disk around an axis offset by 5''-7'' from the photometric center and a luminous gas at a distance up to 9 kpc in a direction perpendicular to the galactic plane. Using a multipupil fiber spectrograph (MPFS), we constructed the velocity fields of the stellar and gaseous components in the central region of this galaxy, which proved to be similar. The similar line-of-sight velocities of the pair and the wide ( 5' 5' ) field of view of the scanning Fabry-Perot interferometer (IFP) allowed us to obtain images in the H_ and [ NII ] 6583 emission lines and in the continuum as well as to construct the line-of-sight velocity fields and to map the distribution of the [ NII ] 6583/H_ ratio for both galaxies simultaneously. Based on these data, we studied the gas kinematics in the galaxies, constructed their rotation curves, and estimated their masses ( 2 10^ 11 M_ for NGC6286 and 1.2 10^ 10 M_ for NGC6285).

In conclusion, we emphasize that our kinematics study of the gaseous component of the galaxy NGC6286 has revealed no evidence of gas rotation around its major axis. Therefore, NGC6286 is unlikely to be a galaxy with a forming polar ring, as assumed previously. Based on the entire set of observational data, we have concluded that the peculiarities of NGC6286 are attributable to the presence of a superwind that outflows from its central region.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 6286 as an interacting candidate polar-ring system with disk-wide diffuse radio emission, a substantial unresolved core, high 20 cm flux suggesting a starburst, and companion NGC 6285.

quotes.quote: PRC C-51 (NGC 6286, Arp 293) The morphology of the 20 cm emission in this galaxy is very similar to that of PRC C-28 -- there is a bright central core, surrounded by diffuse emission that extends throughout the disk of the galaxy (see Figure 5). However, the unresolved core component in this object contains 35 \ of the total 20 cm emission in this object. Again, none of the continuum emission we detect appears to be associated with the candidate polar ring. The high 20 cm flux suggests a starburst (see Section 4). The ``polar ring'' in this galaxy appears to be incomplete, and the optical morphology of the incomplete ring suggests that NGC 6286 is currently interacting with its close companion, NGC 6285 (PRC).

gmw_00010893.name: NGC 6285
gmw_00010893.overview: NGC 6285 is a disturbed companion galaxy to NGC 6286 that features a gaseous disk with solid-body rotation in its central region [arXiv:astro-ph/0312564]. The galaxy exhibits intense nuclear emission, a bright H II region, and a southeastern disk that is elongated and bent toward its neighbor [arXiv:astro-ph/0312564].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 6285 as the disturbed companion of NGC6286, with a gaseous disk, nuclear and HII emission, a bent southeastern disk, and distorted velocities.

quotes.quote: NGC 6285 The emission-line intensity distribution for the companion galaxy (Fig. 5b) exhibits the following features: a gaseous disk, intense emission in the nucleus, and a bright HII region 7'' to the west of the nucleus. The nucleus and the HII region lie on the opposite sides of the ring-like structure ( 7'' in diameter) that is clearly seen in the H_ and [ NII ] images. The SE side of the gaseous disk is elongated and bent toward the neighboring galaxy. In the unperturbed western part of the disk, the [ NII ] 6583/H_ ratio is almost constant, being 0.4 , which suggests that photoionization is responsible for the formation of the emission.

In general, the velocity field of NGC6285 is characteristic of an inclined gaseous disk with solid-body rotation in its central part. However, the isovelocities are distorted both at the center and on the periphery, particularly in the SE part of the galaxy.

gmw_00010894.name: UDF-s2-1
gmw_00010894.overview: UDF-s2-1 is a high-redshift galaxy with a spectroscopic redshift of z=8.228 [arXiv:2212.05072]. It exhibits strong [OIII] emission lines, though a physical association with its nearby sample companions is considered unlikely [arXiv:2212.05072].

sources.summary: The paper identifies UDF-s2-1 as the brightest sample galaxy, with strong 4.6205 and 4.8865 micron lines, likely [OIII] emission at z=8.228, and nearby sample companions.

quotes.quote: UDF-s2-1, the brightest object in our sample, shows two strong emission lines at =4.8865 m and =4.6205 m and one weaker emission line that is visible in the extracted 1D spectrum at =4.5775 m. The two strong lines are slightly offset along the Y-axis suggesting that they arise from spatially-distinct objects whose spectra overlap (Figure fig:spectro ). Assuming that the line at =4.6205 m is [ OIII ] 5007, the spectroscopic redshift would be z= 8.228, as expected from our data. The weaker emission line identified at =4.5775 m would therefore be associated with the [ OIII ] 4959 line.

Three galaxies from our z 8.2 sample (UDF-s2-1, UDF-s2-2 and UDF-s2-3) are co-located on the same NIRCam detector with a projected separation of 31 between UDF-s2-1 and UDF-s2-2 (corresponding to 0.15 Mpc), 1.18 (0.34 Mpc) between UDF-s2-1 and UDF-s2-3 and 1.01 (0.29 Mpc) between UDF-s2-2 and UDF-s2-3. However, these separations are larger than those observed in SMACS0723 protocluster (40 0.2Mpc) and behind A2744 ( < 50 kpc). Moreover the luminosities are fainter than in previous findings ( m_ F182M 27.5 AB) suggesting a physical association is unlikely.

gmw_00010895.name: UDF-s2-2
gmw_00010895.overview: UDF-s2-2 is a high-redshift galaxy located at z ~ 8 [arXiv:2212.05072]. It has no detected emission lines, indicating a small equivalent width for oxygen and hydrogen lines, and hosts a mature stellar population estimated to be 350 million years old [arXiv:2212.05072].

sources.summary: The paper reports no detected emission line for UDF-s2-2, finds the smallest [OIII]+H equivalent width in the sample, and infers a mature 350 Myr stellar population.

quotes.quote: For UDF-s2-2, no emission line is detected suggesting an upper limit on the EW( [ OIII ] 5007 \ ) of 1870 \ at 3 , consistent with our conclusion that this object has the smallest EW( [ OIII ] 4959,5007+H ) of our sample.

For our z 8 medium-band sample, UDF-s2-2 and UDF-s2-5 indicate a mature ( 150 Myr) stellar population with an age of 350 ^ +140 _ 150 and 180 ^ +200 _ 100 Myr respectively corresponding to a formation redshifts of z_ form =15.15 ^ +10.25 _ 4.12 and 10.43 ^ +5.42 _ 1.43 .

Three galaxies from our z 8.2 sample (UDF-s2-1, UDF-s2-2 and UDF-s2-3) are co-located on the same NIRCam detector with a projected separation of 31 between UDF-s2-1 and UDF-s2-2 (corresponding to 0.15 Mpc), 1.18 (0.34 Mpc) between UDF-s2-1 and UDF-s2-3 and 1.01 (0.29 Mpc) between UDF-s2-2 and UDF-s2-3. However, these separations are larger than those observed in SMACS0723 protocluster (40 0.2Mpc) and behind A2744 ( < 50 kpc). Moreover the luminosities are fainter than in previous findings ( m_ F182M 27.5 AB) suggesting a physical association is unlikely.

gmw_00010896.name: UDF-s2-3
gmw_00010896.overview: UDF-s2-3 is a high-redshift emission-line galaxy [arXiv:2212.05072]. It exhibits a clear emission line at 4.6185 microns, which indicates a spectroscopic redshift of z=8.224 if identified as [OIII] 5007 [arXiv:2212.05072].

sources.summary: The paper reports that UDF-s2-3 has a contaminated spectrum but a clear 4.6185 micron line, yielding z=8.224 if identified as [OIII] 5007.

quotes.quote: Following the reduction we find that three objects from our sample were observed over the appropriate wavelength range (UDF-s2-1, UDF-s2-2 and UDF-s2-3) and three were observed across a restricted wavelength range that does not include the [ OIII ] 4959,5007 emission lines (UDF-s2-4, UDF-s2-5 and UDF-s3-1).

Although the spectrum of UDF-s2-3 is similarly contaminated, there is a clear emission line at =4.6185 m which, if [ OIII ] 5007, would yield a spectroscopic redshift of z= 8.224, also in agreement with our photometric value (see Table tab:properties ).

Three galaxies from our z 8.2 sample (UDF-s2-1, UDF-s2-2 and UDF-s2-3) are co-located on the same NIRCam detector with a projected separation of 31 between UDF-s2-1 and UDF-s2-2 (corresponding to 0.15 Mpc), 1.18 (0.34 Mpc) between UDF-s2-1 and UDF-s2-3 and 1.01 (0.29 Mpc) between UDF-s2-2 and UDF-s2-3. However, these separations are larger than those observed in SMACS0723 protocluster (40 0.2Mpc) and behind A2744 ( < 50 kpc). Moreover the luminosities are fainter than in previous findings ( m_ F182M 27.5 AB) suggesting a physical association is unlikely.

gmw_00010897.name: AS1063-46
gmw_00010897.overview: AS1063-46 is a high-magnification lensed X-ray source [arXiv:2102.05788]. Its soft-band detection and counterpart non-detections are consistent with a source dominated by star formation [arXiv:2102.05788].

sources.summary: The paper classifies AS1063-46 as a high-magnification lensed X-ray source whose soft-band detection and counterpart non-detections remain consistent with expectations for a star-formation-dominated source.

quotes.quote: Applying a crude classification in terms of X-ray luminosity, we find three sources (one of which includes two optical counterparts) consistent with being dominated by X-ray emission from star formation, with luminosities L_X<10^ 42 erg/s in the soft or L_X<2 10^ 42 erg/s in the total 0.5--10 keV band (AS1063-46, M0416-12, R2129-12 and 13, and barely M1115-23).

Source AS1063-46 is detected only in the soft band with 17.5 8.6 net counts and a magnification of 30 , and it has two other counterparts with a magnification of 3.4 and 17.8. The first counterpart would be clearly too faint to be visible, but the second is expected to have 10 net counts, which does not significantly contradict with the aperture photometry at its position, which is 2.1 6.0 net counts.
For sources AS1063-46, AS1063-95, and M0416-221, our X-ray detection is therefore consistent with no detection at the position of the other counterparts.

gmw_00010898.name: M0416-208
gmw_00010898.overview: M0416-208 is a lensed X-ray candidate galaxy detected in the soft band [arXiv:2102.05788]. It overlaps within 2 arcseconds of a foreground galaxy, which may contaminate its photometry [arXiv:2102.05788].

sources.summary: The paper flags M0416-208 as a lensed X-ray candidate later removed because its expected higher-magnification counterparts were not detected and nearby cluster-member contamination was possible.

quotes.quote: We mark with an asterisk two sources (M0416-208 and M0416-241) that are discarded on the basis of the lack of detection of a counterpart with a higher magnification. In addition, M0416-208 overlaps within 2 arcsec with a foreground galaxy, thus the photometry may be contaminated. We kept these sources in the list consistent with the criteria we applied, but we removed them from the following analysis.

Source M0416-208 is detected in the soft band with 15.6 5.5 net counts. It has two counterparts that are expected to measure 30 and 14 counts in the same band. However, the photometry on the two counterparts gives 2.2 7.4 and 0.6 8.4 .

gmw_00010899.name: M0416-241
gmw_00010899.overview: M0416-241 is a high-redshift candidate source that was identified as a lensed X-ray candidate [arXiv:2102.05788]. It was later discarded from analysis because its expected higher-magnification counterpart yielded null photometry [arXiv:2102.05788].

sources.summary: The paper flags M0416-241 as a lensed X-ray candidate removed from analysis after a higher-magnification counterpart expected to be detected had null photometry inconsistent at more than 3 sigma.

quotes.quote: We mark with an asterisk two sources (M0416-208 and M0416-241) that are discarded on the basis of the lack of detection of a counterpart with a higher magnification. In addition, M0416-208 overlaps within 2 arcsec with a foreground galaxy, thus the photometry may be contaminated. We kept these sources in the list consistent with the criteria we applied, but we removed them from the following analysis.

We find that in three cases the lack of detection in the other counterparts is still consistent within 1 , while in the case of M0416-241 and M0416-208, we should have detected their counterparts with a signal of more than 3 , but we obtained a null photometry at the corresponding positions. In addition, M0416-208 is very close to a cluster member ( <2 ) that might easily contaminate its photometry. Therefore these two sources were not considered in our further analysis.

The situation is the same for M0416-241, for which we have a detection with 15.3 7.8 net counts in the hard band. There is another counterpart of this source, with higher magnification, for which about 30 net counts are expected, while its photometry is 2.0 4.7 , which is therefore inconsistent at more than 3 . We conclude that M0416-208 and M0416-241 should be removed from our candidate list.

gmw_00010900.name: UVISTA 169610
gmw_00010900.overview: UVISTA 169610 is a quenched galaxy with no ongoing star formation, exhibiting a negligible level of H-alpha emission [arXiv:2210.03747]. Its ionized gas kinematics reveal broad, gravitationally unbound outflows that are most likely driven by an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2210.03747].

sources.summary: The paper fits spectra and photometry of UVISTA 169610, finding negligible H emission, very low star formation, high [NII]/H, and broad unbound gas kinematics indicating AGN-driven outflows.

quotes.quote: For UVISTA 169610, we fit 6 Gaussians to the ionized gas spectrum accounting for both broad and narrow line components of H , [ NII ] 6550, and [ NII ] 6585. The three narrow lines share the same width, and so do all the three broad lines. The flux ratio between NII 6550/ 6585 is fixed to be 0.326, following . We fitted for 7 parameters: the width of broad and narrow line components ( _ br , _ nr ), the line shift between the broad and narrow component centroids ( _ shift ), and the flux of H , [ NII ] 6585 for both broad and narrow line components (H _ br , [ NII ] 6550_ br , H _ nr , [ NII ] 6550_ nr ).

It is clear that there is no ongoing star formation in UVISTA 169610, as indicated by the very low level of H emission; the measured H flux is negligible compared to the [ NII ] flux. From the Gaussian fit of H emission, we can set an upper limit to the SFR of UVISTA 169610 as SFR <0.07 M_ / yr , following the canonical correlation of , and a lower limit on the ratio of [ NII ] /H > 157.1 . The standard deviation of the broad and narrow line components are _ br =707 km s^ 1 ( FWHM_ br = 2.35 _ br = 1661 km s^ 1 ) and _ nr =176 km s^ 1 , respectively. The kinematics of the broad emission component suggests that the ionized gas outflow is gravitationally unbound, and thus most likely originating from AGN-driven outflows.

gmw_00010901.name: UVISTA 174150
gmw_00010901.overview: UVISTA 174150 is a red, rapidly quenched galaxy with low dust content [arXiv:2210.03747]. It lacks hydrogen-alpha and nitrogen II emission, suggesting that its star formation has ceased and its ionized gas has been blown away [arXiv:2210.03747].

sources.summary: The paper describes UVISTA 174150 as a red, low-dust rapidly quenched galaxy with no H or [NII] emission, suggesting star formation has stopped and ionized gas was blown away.

quotes.quote: Interestingly, UVISTA 174150 does not seem to show any emission features, neither in H nor in [ NII ] . The lack of H emission can be explained by no ongoing star formation, and the absence of [ NII ] emission suggests that ionized gas may have already been blown away by the feedback processes.

UVISTA 174150 is the reddest galaxy among our 12 rapidly quenched targets, and since it has low amounts of dust, its color is not likely reddened by dust (see the fitting parameter in Table. tab:prospector_fit_phot_only ). The SFH of this galaxy also suggests that it was quenched a few hundred Myrs earlier than the other two galaxies, which might be related to the absence of ionized gas. While the other two galaxies still exhibit AGN-driven emission characteristics 100 Myr after quenching, the ionized gas of UVISTA 174150 may have been all blown away 300 Myr after quenching.

gmw_00010902.name: UVISTA 77854
gmw_00010902.overview: UVISTA 77854 is a quenched galaxy located at a spectroscopic redshift of 1.333 [arXiv:2210.03747]. It exhibits a low level of star formation and hosts an ionized gas outflow that likely originates from active galactic nucleus activity [arXiv:2210.03747].

sources.summary: The paper detects emission lines in UVISTA 77854, fits its photometry and gas spectrum, and finds low H-derived star formation with an AGN-origin ionized outflow.

quotes.quote: We have also detected [ NII ] and [ OIII ] emission lines for UVISTA 77854 (no Balmer absorptions detected due to high noise) and confirmed that the spectroscopic redshift is consistent with the phometric redshift. We perform the SED fitting on its photometric data using Prospector with fixed redshift ( z_ spec =1.333 ) and assuming a fixed velocity dispersion of 200 km s^ 1 . The ionized gas spectrum is obtained by subtracting the best-fit stellar model from the spectrum, which is shown in Fig. fig:emission_lines (). We also fit the 6 Gaussians, in the same way as we did on UVISTA 169610. The resulting standard deviation of the broad and narrow line components are _ br =400 km s^ 1 ( FWHM_ br = 2.35 _ br = 940 km s^ 1 ) and _ nr =131 km s^ 1 , respectively.

The low level of H emission (setting the upper limit of SFR <1.13 M_ / yr and [ NII ] /H =3.3 ) suggests that the galaxy is indeed quenched and the ionized gas outflow is most likely originated from AGN activity.

UVISTA 77854 shows non-negligible level of H emission, setting the upper limit of SFR <1 M_ / yr , which is consistent with the estimated SFR from the Prospector fit. This galaxy is, indeed, closest to the star-forming region in the UVJ diagram.

gmw_00010903.name: UVISTA 199028
gmw_00010903.overview: UVISTA 199028 is an extremely compact galaxy [arXiv:2210.03747]. It is characterized by a rapid-quenching transition phase, though some model posteriors suggest a more complex evolutionary path [arXiv:2210.03747].

sources.summary: The paper describes UVISTA 199028 as extremely compact with a bad GALFIT size fit, and measures its rapid-quenching transition only from posterior draws crossing the rapid-quenching region.

quotes.quote: UVISTA 199028 is covered in the survey but appears to be extremely compact, resulting in a bad fit using GALFIT.
UVISTA 199028, which has a bad GALFIT fit, is plotted as a dashed arrow.

In the case of UVISTA 199028 and UVISTA 166544, with some of their random posteriors, they end up not going through the rapid quenching phase region (the blue box in the left panel of Fig. fig:color_evolution ), but rather evolve by deflecting to the right corner of the region. This explains why the lower tail of their transition time distributions seems to go below 0. For these two galaxies, therefore, we measure the median transition timescale only using the random posteriors with which they go through the rapid quenching region.

gmw_00010904.name: UVISTA 166544
gmw_00010904.overview: UVISTA 166544 is a galaxy that appears to be not fully quenched [arXiv:2210.03747]. Its evolutionary history indicates that it sometimes misses the rapid-quenching phase region, instead deflecting to the right corner of this region [arXiv:2210.03747].

sources.summary: The paper notes UVISTA 166544 is not fully quenched and, like UVISTA 199028, has posterior histories that sometimes miss the rapid-quenching region, requiring restricted transition-time estimates.

quotes.quote: UVISTA 166544 appears to be not fully quenched with (sSFR) 9.4 .

In the case of UVISTA 199028 and UVISTA 166544, with some of their random posteriors, they end up not going through the rapid quenching phase region (the blue box in the left panel of Fig. fig:color_evolution ), but rather evolve by deflecting to the right corner of the region. This explains why the lower tail of their transition time distributions seems to go below 0. For these two galaxies, therefore, we measure the median transition timescale only using the random posteriors with which they go through the rapid quenching region.

gmw_00010905.name: IC 1815
gmw_00010905.overview: IC 1815 is a galaxy characterized by a classical, pressure-supported bulge [arXiv:1409.2876]. This bulge exhibits a high Sersic index of 3.4 and a central velocity dispersion exceeding 200 km/s, with no evidence of a central dispersion depression [arXiv:1409.2876].

sources.summary: The paper describes IC 1815 as having the sample's highest bulge Sersic index, no central dispersion depression, high central dispersion, and a classical bulge.

quotes.quote: IC 1815: Only 2MASS imaging is available for this galaxy. The S ersic index of the bulge ( n=3.4 ) is the highest of our sample. The velocity profile shows a mild double-hump profile at =3.5 but the velocity dispersion does not show any evidence of flattening or depression in the central regions. The central velocity dispersion is larger than 200 km s ^ 1 suggesting the bulge is pressure supported. We suggest this galaxy hosts a classical bulge .

gmw_00010906.name: NGC 0043
gmw_00010906.overview: NGC 0043 is a weakly barred galaxy with a smooth surface-brightness distribution [arXiv:1409.2876]. Its central structure features a classical bulge, but an asymmetric radial profile suggests it may also host a boxy- or peanut-shaped bulge [arXiv:1409.2876].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 0043 as a weakly barred galaxy with classical-bulge indicators plus an asymmetric h4 minimum suggesting a possible B/P bulge.

quotes.quote: NGC 0043: The SDSS image shows a smooth surface-brightness distribution with the weakest bar of our sample. The bulge S ersic index is n=2.2 . No clear signs of a double-hump rotation curve or depression in the central velocity dispersion are found. These characteristics point towards the presence of a classical bulge.

However, the h_4 radial profile is asymmetric, showing a clear minimum on the approaching side of the galaxy at r_ B/P =6 . This corresponds to 1/3 of the bar length, in agreement with the findings of mendezabreu08b and suggesting the presence of a B/P bulge. We suggest this galaxy hosts a classical bulge and possibly a B/P bulge too.

gmw_00010907.name: NGC 0521
gmw_00010907.overview: NGC 0521 is a barred spiral galaxy that hosts a central rotating structure associated with a pseudobulge [arXiv:1409.2876]. The galaxy features a truncated disk, an inner ring, and a central velocity-dispersion plateau that corresponds to a double-hump rotation curve [arXiv:1409.2876].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 0521 as having a truncated disk, an inner ring, a central velocity-dispersion plateau matching a double-hump rotation curve, and a pseudobulge-like rotating structure.

quotes.quote: NGC 0521: A truncated disk is clearly visible in the SDSS surface-brightness profile. buta09 found the presence of an inner ring with a radius =4.3 see also comeron10 . The velocity dispersion radial profile shows a central plateau with r_ drop =3.5 , which corresponds to a steep rise of the rotation curve (showing a marked double-hump profile).
The velocity anti-correlates with h_3 . The bulge effective radius is r_ e =3.6 . We suggest this galaxy hosts a central rotating structure that can be associated with a pseudobulge .

gmw_00010908.name: NGC 0621
gmw_00010908.overview: NGC 0621 is a galaxy that likely hosts a classical bulge, suggested by its high central velocity dispersion [arXiv:1409.2876]. The galaxy's central velocity dispersion profile displays a symmetric double drop, which indicates the presence of either an inner bar or an inner ring [arXiv:1409.2876].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 0621's central symmetric double drop as hollow-like evidence for an inner bar or ring, with high central dispersion suggesting a possible classical bulge.

quotes.quote: NGC 0621: Only 2MASS imaging was available for this galaxy. The velocity dispersion radial profile in the center shows a peculiar double drop, symmetric with respect to the galaxy center. This behavior of the velocity dispersion is similar to the hollows discovered in delorenzocaceres08 , which are related to the presence of an inner bar. These hollows have turned out to be more common than expected and have been detected in other galaxies delorenzocaceres12 .

Moreover, the size of the drop ( r_ drop =2.7 ) also point towards the inner bar scenario since it is much larger that the bulge effective radius ( r_ e =1.6 ). We consider that the high central velocity dispersion obtained for this galaxy, which suggests there is a classical bulge, fits well in the scenario of a double bar, since a high velocity dispersion contrast is a necessary condition to observe the hollows delorenzocaceres08 . Nevertheless, we cannot rule out other possibilities such as the presence of an inner ring. Therefore, we suggest this galaxy hosts an inner bar (or inner ring ) and possibly a classical bulge .

gmw_00010909.name: NGC 1640
gmw_00010909.overview: NGC 1640 is a barred spiral galaxy that features strong dust lanes along its bar major axis [arXiv:1409.2876]. High-resolution imaging reveals inner spiral arms converging at the galaxy's center, which hosts a central spiral disk, a pseudobulge, and a possible boxy- or peanut-shaped bulge [arXiv:1409.2876].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 1640 as a dust-laned barred galaxy whose HST image reveals inner spiral arms matching the dispersion drop, plus a pseudobulge and possible B/P bulge.

quotes.quote: NGC 1640: The SDSS image shows the presence of strong dust lanes along the bar major axis. This is confirmed by the F606W- band image of the HST /WFPC2. In fact, this high spatial resolution image shows how the dust lanes converge to the galaxy center where inner spiral arms can be clearly seen in the unsharp masked image (Fig. fig:unsharp ).

The radius of this inner structure, as measured from the WFPC2 image, is =1.5 , which corresponds to the size of the drop ( r_ drop = 1.6 ). On the contrary, the bulge effective radius is much larger ( r_ e = 3.4 ). The h_4 radial profiles show hints of a double minimum at r_ B/P 8 , i.e., at 1/6 of the bar radius. We suggest that this galaxy hosts a central spiral disk , a slightly larger pseudobulge , and possibly an outer B/P bulge .

gmw_00010910.name: NGC 2493
gmw_00010910.overview: NGC 2493 is a barred spiral galaxy that shows no signs of dust obscuration [arXiv:1409.2876]. It hosts a classical bulge, a central cold structure, and possibly a boxy- or peanut-shaped bulge [arXiv:1409.2876].

sources.summary: The paper reports no dust in NGC 2493, a mild central dispersion plateau, a much larger bulge radius, and h4 evidence for a central cold structure with classical and possible B/P bulges.

quotes.quote: NGC 2493: No sign of dust obscuration is seen in the SDSS image. There is a mild plateau in the center of the velocity dispersion profile which can be better distinguished when comparing with the best fit exponential model extrapolation. The size of the plateau ( r_ drop =1.5 ) is much smaller than the bulge effective radius ( r_ e = 4.6 ). The h_4 radial profile shows hints of a double minimum at r_ B/P 10 (i.e., at 1/3 of the bar radius). We suggest that this galaxy hosts a central cold structure , a classical bulge and possibly a B/P bulge .

gmw_00010911.name: NGC 4838
gmw_00010911.overview: NGC 4838 is a barred spiral galaxy that hosts a pseudobulge [arXiv:1409.2876]. The galaxy exhibits a strong velocity dispersion drop in its center and a highly asymmetric rotation curve [arXiv:1409.2876].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4838 as having a strong central velocity-dispersion drop, asymmetric stepped rotation, and a drop size roughly matching the bulge radius, implying a pseudobulge.

quotes.quote: NGC 4838: The velocity dispersion radial profile shows a strong drop in the center. The rotation curve is strongly asymmetric and presents also a step rise but only on the receding side, the rotation curve being strongly asymmetric. The size of the _ drop ( r_ drop = 2.2 ) roughly corresponds with the effective radius of the bulge ( r_ e = 1.8 ). We suggest that this galaxy hosts a pseudobulge .

gmw_00010912.name: WKK 1263
gmw_00010912.overview: WKK 1263 is classified as a Seyfert 1.5 galaxy [arXiv:2203.07118]. It is an active galactic nucleus that has been studied in the X-ray band to determine its high-energy cutoff limits [arXiv:2203.07118].

sources.summary: The paper reports a high cutoff lower limit for WKK 1263, notes three similar-slope literature lower limits, and suggests Swift-XRT inclusion may explain Molina's lower measurement.

quotes.quote: WKK 1263 (IGR J12415-5750), Sy 1.5, > 530 keV. Kamraj_2018 , Panagiotou_2020 and Akylas_2021 reported lower limits of 224 keV, 1826 keV and 282 keV respectively, and all three works derive 1.8, similar to our results. Molina_2019 reported an = 123_ 47 ^ +54 keV, 1.6 and a similar R_ pexrav < 0.23 . The involvement of the quasi-simultaneous Swift-XRT data (5.7 ks XRT exposure, while 16 ks of ) in Molina_2019 may account for such discrepancy.

gmw_00010913.name: FIRST J 124942.2+303838
gmw_00010913.overview: FIRST J 124942.2+303838 is a Fanaroff-Riley class I radio galaxy with a bent-double morphology [arXiv:0806.3971]. It is associated with four nearby galaxies, forming a system with a velocity dispersion of 250 km/s [arXiv:0806.3971].

sources.summary: The paper treats FIRST J 124942.2+303838 as an FRI bent-double radio galaxy probing intragroup gas, associating it with nearby galaxies and X-ray temperature limits.

quotes.quote: We observed the FRI radio galaxies FIRST J 124942.2+303838 and SDSS J 085331.86+232400.0 (hereafter S1 and S2 respectively) in March of 2006 with the Giant Metrewave Radio Telescope (GMRT). The GMRT consists of thirty 45 meter diameter antennas with half arranged in a compact 1 km diameter, randomly distributed, central square and the remaining in three extended arms with fixed baselines ranging from 0.1 to 25 km in length. The observations were dual frequency 610/235 MHz using both the upper and lower sidebands. In this paper we will discuss only the single polarization 610 MHz data which have an effective bandwidth of 12 MHz in each sideband.

S1 is associated with four other nearby galaxies, three have redshifts measured from our WIYN data and the fourth is a photometric redshift from SDSS. These five galaxies have a velocity dispersion of 250_ 110 ^ +20 . The 68 error bars on the velocity dispersion are determined by generating bootstrap samples using the final set of v_ pec values. We use the X-ray data to examine the temperature of the IGM in these systems.

gmw_00010914.name: SDSS J 085331.86+232400.0
gmw_00010914.overview: SDSS J 085331.86+232400.0 is a Fanaroff-Riley class I radio galaxy that features bent-double radio jets [arXiv:0806.3971]. The galaxy is spatially offset from its associated galaxy group and is moving through the intergalactic medium [arXiv:0806.3971].

sources.summary: The paper treats SDSS J 085331.86+232400.0 as an FRI bent-double radio galaxy offset from a group, using its jet bending and X-rays to infer surrounding gas.

quotes.quote: We observed the FRI radio galaxies FIRST J 124942.2+303838 and SDSS J 085331.86+232400.0 (hereafter S1 and S2 respectively) in March of 2006 with the Giant Metrewave Radio Telescope (GMRT). The GMRT consists of thirty 45 meter diameter antennas with half arranged in a compact 1 km diameter, randomly distributed, central square and the remaining in three extended arms with fixed baselines ranging from 0.1 to 25 km in length. The observations were dual frequency 610/235 MHz using both the upper and lower sidebands. In this paper we will discuss only the single polarization 610 MHz data which have an effective bandwidth of 12 MHz in each sideband.

In the case of S2, the radio source appears offset spatially from the group. 10 of the galaxies circled in black have photometric redshifts and 3 (including the radio source) are SDSS spectroscopic redshifts. We estimate the speed of this source through the IGM using its velocity relative to the mean redshift of the system it is approaching ( z =0.3081 ) and find a speed of 570 . The velocity of this source is uncertain although not unreasonable and we use a rough error of 10 of its value in future calculations.

gmw_00010915.name: candidate absorbing galaxy in the Q 1209+107 PA2 field
aliases_local: candidate absorbing galaxy in the Q 1209+107 PA2 field
gmw_00010915.overview: The candidate absorbing galaxy in the Q 1209+107 PA2 field is a low-mass starburst galaxy [arXiv:1807.01755]. It is considered an unlikely absorber host, and its properties suggest it may be a smaller member of a group environment [arXiv:1807.01755].

sources.original_name: candidate absorbing galaxy in the Q 1209+107 PA2 field
sources.summary: The paper flags this low-mass star-burst galaxy as an unlikely absorber host, favoring a hidden massive host while using limits below the quasar trace.

quotes.quote: Unlikely host in the Q 1209 + 107 PA2 field The subsequent analysis of our entire sample (see Section sec:analysis ) in relation to known scaling relations and luminosity-selected samples reveals that the candidate absorbing galaxy in the Q 1209+107 PA2 field occupies a unique place in the MZ (Section sec:mass-metallicity ); on the SF main-sequence ( sec:SFR ); and in the metallicity-gradient (Section sec:metallicity_gradient ) parameter space. In particular, it is worrying that a low-mass ( _ 10 [ M _ ( M _ ) ] 8.2 ) star-burst galaxy has acquired a super-solar metallicity at a distance of 38 kpc from its luminosity-centre, when the emission-line diagnostics yield a low central oxygen abundance of [ O/H ] 8.05 . Guided by these results and discussions, we flag the absorbing galaxy as an unlikely host. Rather, its redshift, mass, metallicity and SFR are suggestive of a smaller member in a group environment around the real host. More likely, the real host is a more massive galaxy which is hiding below the quasar PSF.

We note that the SPSF-subtraction did not reveal any hidden line-flux below the quasar trace. We therefore report upper limits on the line-flux from an object under the quasar trace as the 3 residuals in a 5 spectral window centred on the emission line in the SPSF-subtracted 1D-spectrum. We find consistent upper limits at a 2 10^ 17 ergs s ^ 1 cm ^ 2 flux-level at the predicted position of [ O ii ] and H alike.

gmw_00010916.name: Q 2328+0022
gmw_00010916.overview: Q 2328+0022 is an absorbing galaxy identified near a quasar [arXiv:1807.01755]. Its real residual flux and spectral features have been successfully recovered and extracted after performing stellar point spread function subtraction [arXiv:1807.01755].

sources.summary: The paper identifies the Q 2328+0022 absorbing galaxy after SPSF subtraction, confirms real residual flux, and extracts its spectral features while avoiding quasar-PSF bias.

quotes.quote: Two additional objects are identified as the absorbing galaxies after performing SPSF subtraction (Q 1323-0021 and Q 2328+0022, see Figure fig:SPSFsub ). For these two systems, we vary the size and limits of the wavelength windows to see if the residual flux is real, or an artefact of the subtraction. In all realisations of the SPSF-subtraction, the residual flux is visible, which confirms that the signal is real (see Figure fig:SPSFsub ).

1D-spectra covering the recovered spectral features of the absorbing galaxies are then extracted with a simple extraction using standard IRAF tasks. By using a simple extraction, we avoid biasing the flux-measurement by the signal gradient present in the wing of the quasar PSF Moller2000a ; an effect which would result from the standard optimal extraction weighting scheme Horne1986 . We define the extraction apertures relative to the quasar trace, with aperture limits [ +0.38'':+1.26'' ] and [ +0.38'':+1.64'' ] for Q 1323-0021 and Q 2328+0022, respectively.

gmw_00010917.name: J1826 + 3431
gmw_00010917.overview: J1826 + 3431 is a flat-spectrum radio quasar and blazar located at a redshift of z=1.81 [arXiv:2009.07247]. Its radio jet exhibits apparent superluminal motion and relativistic beaming, while the source itself shows long-term flux density variability [arXiv:2009.07247].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J1826 + 3431 as a blazar and likely 3EG counterpart, measuring its radio position, jet morphology, superluminal motions, and long-term flux behavior.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: right ascension 18^ h 26^ min 59.9828210 0.0000091^ s and declination 34^ 31^ 14.119974 0.00019^

quotes.quote: One of them, 3EG J1824 + 3441, is associated with a flat-spectrum radio quasar at a redshift z=1.81 . This radio source was detected in the NRAO VLA Sky Survey (NVSS, ). J1826 + 3431 has a 1.4-GHz flux density of (470 14) mJy which is the highest value in the EGRET positional error box ( 1^ ) . Since the source belongs to the Very Long Baseline Array (VLBA) Calibrator Survey sample, high-resolution radio interferometric observations of the source exist, which can be used to strengthen or falsify its inferred blazar nature and in turn its association with the EGRET ray source. The accurate radio position ( http://hpiers.obspm.fr/icrs-pc/newwww/icrf/icrf3sx.txt ) of J1826+3431 in the current 3rd realization of the International Celestial Reference Frame is right ascension 18^ h 26^ min 59.9828210 0.0000091^ s and declination 34^ 31^ 14.119974 0.00019^ .

The arcsec-scale radio structure of J1826+3431 revealed by our 4.86-GHz VLA images (Figure fig:vla-maps ) indicates a continuing jet towards the southeast, in approximately the same position angle as the mas-scale VLBI jet (Figure fig:maps ). The jet can be traced out to 2.5 ^ (corresponding to 20, kpc projected distance from the core) in the higher-resolution A-configuration image. There is an indication of a sharp right-angle bend towards south, as supported by the lower-resolution B-configuration VLA image where the radio emission seems extended in that direction (Figure fig:vla-maps ). The sum of the flux densities in the fitted Gaussian model components ( 428 21 mJy at 1987.493 and 333 17 mJy at 2009.388) provide evidence for significant variability. Component Proper Motions in the Mas-Scale Jet of J1826 + 3431 To identify the jet components throughout the full time span of the VLBI observations, we considered their positions, FWHM sizes, and flux densities. The components that are identified across the different epochs are denoted by the same identifiers in Tables tabSk - tabUk . The core component is numbered with 0 at each epoch and frequency; thus, it is denoted as L0, C0, D0, and J0 at 1.7 , 2.3 , 8.3/8.7 , and 15.3 GHz, respectively. For further analysis, we assumed the core to be stationary and we derived the positions of the fitted jet components relative to it (Tables tabLk - tabUk ). The jet components are assumed to move away from the core at constant speeds along a straight trajectory. Thus, we fitted their core separations with linear functions. The derived angular proper motions were converted to apparent speeds in units of the speed of light, , using the relation _ app = d_ L (1+z) where is the angular proper motion of the component in rad s ^ 1 , d_ L is the luminosity distance in m, is the speed of light in m s ^ 1 , and z is the redshift of the source. At 2.3 GHz, at most five components were needed to describe the jet structure. The inner 10 mas region of the jet could not be resolved into two distinct components in 1996.370 , unlike at later epochs (components C1 and C2). Therefore, the single fitted inner jet component, Ca, cannot be unambiguously identified with either of the components detected later. Thus, the motion of C1 and C2 can only be tracked at the relatively closely-spaced observing epochs of the 2010s. Because of the short time baseline, these two components show no significant change in their core separation during the available observing epochs. On the other hand, component C3 could be unambiguously identified through all epochs. It shows an angular proper motion of = 0.12 0.07 mas yr ^ 1 , which corresponds to an apparent speed of _ app = 9.4 5.5 . At large core separations, component C4 could only be detected at two epochs. Therefore, it is unsuitable for further proper motion calculations. The core separations for each component and the fitted linear proper motion curve for C3 are shown in Figure fig:SXtav . At 8.3/8.7 GHz, three jet components, D2, D3, and D4, can be detected at all four epochs, while D1 can only be detected at the last three epochs. This may indicate that it was ejected some time between the epochs 1996.370 and 2015.064 . No discernible proper motions could be detected for components D1 and D2, while D3 and D4 show apparent superluminal motions (Table app.speeds ). The core separations and the fitted linear curves for D3 and D4 are shown in Figure fig:SXtav . As we performed proper motion and jet parameter calculations using D3 and D4, we present contour maps of J1826+3431 made at this frequency at all four epochs in Figure fig:maps2 , indicating the fitted model components. At 15.3 GHz, only three years elapsed between the first and last observations. This time span is too short for detecting proper motions in any of the fitted components. Component J1 could be first detected at epoch 2004.616 . This may indicate that J1 was ejected some time between the first and second epochs. Later, as it moved farther away from the core, this component became detectable even at a lower frequency, 8.7 GHz, at the epoch 2015.064 as D1. To summarize, apparent superluminal motions could be detected in three components at relatively larger core separations (C3, D3 and D4) in the jet of J1826 + 3431. Interestingly, the highest apparent speed is shown by a component (D4) located at the largest core separation ( 10 mas) implying acceleration in the jet. Acceleration is often detected in the jets of blazars (e.g., ), and although it usually takes place at smaller core separations, there are examples for faster components further down the jets (e.g., PMN J0405 1308 , PKS 2201 + 171 ). Relativistic Beaming in J1826 + 3431 At the highest observing frequency, 15.3 GHz, which provides the best angular resolution, we derived the brightness temperature of the core component using the following equation : T _ b = 1.22 10^ 12 (1+z) S ^ 2 ^ 2 K , where S is the flux density of the fitted component in Jy, is the FWHM diameter of the circular Gaussian in mas, and is the observing frequency in GHz. In 2003.655 , we were unable to resolve the core region into two components as at the later epochs. As a test, we tried to fit the innermost region with an elliptical Gaussian component and the resulting fit confirmed that the core was elongated in the direction of component J1 detected at the later epochs. Therefore, J0 and J1 were blended in 2003.655 , thus the FWHM size and the flux density are overestimates of the core parameters at this epoch. Since the brightness temperature is inversely proportional to the square of the FWHM size, its derived value can be regarded as a lower limit, 2.8 10^ 11 K. In 2004.616 , the obtained brightness temperature is T_ b =(12.3 1.4) 10^ 11 K. At epoch 2005.427 , we could only derive an upper limit for the size of the core component, thus only a lower limit for the brightness temperature can be given, 12 10 ^ 11 K. The lower limits agree with the T_ b estimate made at the middle epoch within its errors. All brightness temperature values exceed the equipartition limit, T_ int 5 10^ 10 K , indicating relativistic beaming in the source. The relativistic beaming can be quantified by the Doppler boosting factor, _ vlbi , which can be estimated from the measured T_ b value and the equipartition brightness temperature limit using the following relation : _ vlbi = T_ b T_ int . The obtained brightness temperature value corresponds to a Doppler factor of _ vlbi = 24.6 2.9 . Jet Parameters The jet parameters, the bulk Lorentz factor ( ), and the inclination angle with respect to the line of sight ( ) can be calculated from the Doppler factor and the apparent jet speed as = _ app ^ 2 + ^ 2 +1 2 and = 2 _ app _ app ^ 2 + ^ 2 1 . The component close to the innermost core region with the best-defined positions and thus proper motion is D3; therefore, we used the apparent superluminal speed obtained for this jet feature, _ app ^ D3 =4.8 1.6 , to derive the jet parameters. Using the Doppler factor obtained from the 15.3 GHz measurement at epoch 2004.616 , we get a Lorentz factor of = 12.8 1.4 , implying relativistic bulk jet velocity in units of the speed of light, = 0.997 0.002 . The corresponding jet inclination angle is = 0.9^ 0.3^ . Radio Spectrum of J1826 + 3431 We collected the radio flux density measurements of J1826 + 3431 from the literature (Table tab:spx ). We fitted a power--law curve to these data points to obtain the spectral index ( ; defined as S ^ , where S is the flux density and is the observing frequency). Since the flux density measurements are not simultaneous, source variability may affect the spectral index calculation, so the value can be considered as tentative. The resulting fit is shown with a black line in Figure fig:spx2 , the obtained spectral index is =-0.19 0.05 , indicating a flat spectrum. For comparison, we also show the sum of the VLBI Gaussian model component flux densities with blue squares and upward arrows in Figure fig:spx2 . For each observing frequency, we have chosen the observation that is the closest to the 1.7 GHz EVN measurement. VLBI observations are insensitive to the large (arcsec) scale radio structure; therefore, in the presence of such emission, they underestimate the total flux density of a source. This can be seen in Figure fig:spx2 where all VLBI points are indeed below the fitted spectrum. The simultaneous 2.3 and 8.3/8.7 GHz observations allow us to map the spectral index distribution of the mas-scale radio emission in J1826 + 3431 using VIMAP . We present a spectral index image in Figure fig:spx1 . We used our data from the epoch 2015.064. After using elliptical masks to exclude the cores from the images, we matched the 2.3 and 8.7 GHz maps of J1826 + 3431 by using two-dimensional correlation. Considering that the frequencies are relatively close to each other and that the measurements were performed at the same time, it was not necessary to shift the brightness peak. The spectral index image (Figure fig:spx1 ) suggests a flat-spectrum radio emission ( 0.1 0.1 ) in the core region and along the jet closer to the core. It is in good agreement with the spectral index calculated using the archival total flux density data (Figure fig:spx2 ) since the dominant emission feature of the source is the core. The spectrum gradually steepens further away from the core along the jet, as expected from optically thin synchrotron radio emission.
