Flux Density Variability To characterize variability in the VLBI-detected features, we plot the sum of flux densities in the fitted Gaussian model components at different observing frequencies as a function of time in Figure fluxdensity . As can be clearly seen, especially for the 2.3- and 8.3/8.7-GHz data where the observations span more than two decades, the flux density of the source is strongly variable over time. Moreover, the variations in the difference of the 2.3- and 8.3/8.7-GHz flux density values suggest variability of the spectral index. Ray Properties of 3EG J1824 + 3441 3EG J1824 + 3441 was detected with a weak ray flux of (28.7 9.3) 10^ 8 photon s ^ 2 cm ^ 1 by EGRET . However, an alternate version of the EGRET catalogue (see also ) which used reprocessed data assuming a different model for the Galactic interstellar emission does not contain as many as 107 sources previously detected in , including also 3EG J1824 + 3441. In that sense, it is not surprising that no ray source is listed in the most recent fourth Fermi /LAT catalogue within a 1^ radius of the position of the putative EGRET ray source. Alternatively, even if the source exists, long-term variability of its ray flux can also explain the Fermi non-detection. Indeed, a comparison of high-confidence ( > 4 ) EGRET detections with the third Fermi /LAT catalogue revealed 10 extragalactic ray sources missing from the latter . Subsequent analysis of seven years of Fermi data showed that five sources were in a low-luminosity state during the Fermi observations compared to their luminosity during the EGRET observations . The generally decreasing long-term trend we see in the radio flux density of the possible counterpart, the blazar J1826+3431, with respect to the 1990s when CGRO was operational (see e.g., our VLA results and Figure fluxdensity ) is also consistent with a ray flux decrease. We analyzed eight epochs of high-resolution multi-frequency VLBI data of the radio source J1826 + 3431, which is the proposed radio counterpart of the EGRET ray source, 3EG J1824 + 3441. The archival observations span a period of more than 22 years. Each data set was imaged, and the brightness distribution of the source was modeled using circular Gaussian components, in order to characterize the physical and geometric properties of the jet and to analyze the kinematics of J1826 + 3431. One and two moving jet components could be securely identified at multiple epochs in the 2.3 and 8.3/8.7 GHz data, respectively. All of them showed outward motion with apparent superluminal speed. The estimated brightness temperature of the most compact component, the core at 15.3 GHz suggests that the radio emission is relativistically beamed in the source. From the relativistic effects seen in the radio jet and the strong variability of the core component at the measured frequencies, the radio source J1826 + 3431 can be identified as a blazar.

gmw_00010918.name: kiso3193
gmw_00010918.overview: The galaxy kiso3193 is a tadpole galaxy that exhibits no rotation [arXiv:1302.4352]. It features a double-humped linewidth curve, mild lopsidedness, and may be a face-on disk [arXiv:1302.4352].

sources.summary: The paper says kiso3193 lacks rotation, has a double-humped linewidth curve and mild lopsidedness, and may differ from the other tadpoles as a face-on disk.

quotes.quote: kiso3193 . This is the only tadpole that shows no rotation, and its line-width versus position curve has a curious double-hump shape (Fig. linewidths ). Its light distribution is less lopsided than for the other tadpoles (Figs. images and brightness ). The absence of rotation combined with the increase of linewidth toward the outskirts and the mild lopsidedness may be consistent with a face-on disk, and in this sense kiso3193 differs from the rest of the sample. The observed linewidths are fairly large, comparable to those of other larger galaxies in the sample. Consequently, the dynamical mass of the head inferred from linewidths is significantly larger than its stellar (photometric) mass.

gmw_00010919.name: kiso3867
gmw_00010919.overview: Kiso 3867 is a small nearby dwarf galaxy characterized by a tadpole structure [arXiv:1302.4352, arXiv:2211.09292]. Most of its young star clusters are located near the head of the galaxy, which contains a large number of compact stellar groups [arXiv:2211.09292].

sources.summary: The paper identifies kiso3867 as the smallest sample tadpole, with head linewidths like other tadpoles but unusually high dynamical-to-stellar mass and uncertain oxygen abundance.

quotes.quote: kiso3867 . In both mass and size, this is the smallest tadpole in the sample (see the photometric masses of the full galaxies in Table obs_summary , and also the 1kpc scales given in Fig. brightness ). Its linewidths do not differ so much from the linewidths of other tadpoles, which contrasts with the low photometric mass inferred in Paper I for the head of the tadpole. Consequently, the ratio of dynamical mass to photometric mass is particularly high in this case -- the dynamical mass of the head turns out to be a hundred times larger than the stellar mass derived from photometry (Table obs_summary ). The comparatively large oxygen abundance we derive for the head ( 12+ ( O/H ) 8 ) is probably an overestimate due to observational errors. A few pixels away from the head the abundance drops by 0.3 dex (Fig. metallicity ), which would bring the galaxy down to a more natural location in the mass-metallicity relationship (Fig. masmetal a).

sources.summary: The paper finds Kiso 3867 is a small nearby dwarf with young head clusters, asymmetric very young clusters, many resolved compact groups, and a 5e4 solar-mass maximum complex.

quotes.quote: Kiso 3867 Kiso 3867 (D _ HST 46 arcsec; A _ major 1.5 kpc) has most young clusters near the head of this small dwarf galaxy, but no clear age trend away from the age and into the tail. Noteworthy are the asymmetric distribution of very young clusters extending away from the midplane of the galaxy, in agreement with the morphology of the nebular emission.

A feature of all of these galaxies, and especially SBS1 and Kiso 3867, is the large number of compact stellar groups and clusters. Kiso 3867 is the nearest system in our sample, which may partially account for the large number of resolved stellar clusters and groups.
The smallest galaxy, Kiso 3867, has a maximum complex mass of 5 10^4 M_ and a similarly young age.

gmw_00010920.name: kiso5149
gmw_00010920.overview: The galaxy kiso5149 is a massive tadpole galaxy with a highly asymmetric rotation curve [arXiv:1302.4352]. It features a fairly constant oxygen abundance and contains a second bright knot separated from its rotation center [arXiv:1302.4352].

sources.summary: The paper describes kiso5149 as a massive tadpole with a highly asymmetric rotation curve, nearly constant oxygen abundance, and a second bright knot.

quotes.quote: kiso5149 . The asymmetry of its RC is remarkable: see Fig. velocity_curves . It extends much further out to one side, which would imply that half of the disk is missing from observation. We cannot explain how this happens, unless the galaxy has a cigar-like shape rather than an axi-symmetric structure. It is unlikely that large reddening obscures half the disk, since the SDSS image does not show a large color gradient across the galaxy.

It may be a merger, but then it would have to produce an unlikely large scale velocity field resembling a RC. The oxygen abundance is fairly constant along the galaxy, which seems to be associated with being a massive object (it has the largest mass of the sample -- Table obs_summary ). The galaxy presents a second bright knot separated from the rotation center.

gmw_00010921.name: kiso5639
gmw_00010921.overview: The galaxy kiso5639 is classified as an extremely metal-poor tadpole galaxy [arXiv:1302.4352]. Its head contains a young starburst, and the galaxy exhibits narrow high-excitation emission lines alongside a highly irregular rotation curve [arXiv:1302.4352].

sources.summary: The paper classifies kiso5639 as an XMP tadpole with a young starburst head, irregular rotation curve, and narrow high-excitation emission lines without broad WR features.

quotes.quote: kiso5639 . It is one of the XMP galaxies in the sample, with the head being a young starburst (a few Myr old). It also presents a very irregular RC, particularly in the outer parts.
kiso5639 does not show the broad WR features either. Instead, its spectrum contains narrow high excitation emission lines including HeII 4686 (Fig. wr_features ).

gmw_00010922.name: kiso6669
gmw_00010922.overview: The dwarf irregular galaxy kiso6669 features two low-metallicity knots located near its head [arXiv:1302.4352]. Its rotation center is off-centered between these two emission peaks, and its linewidth curve exhibits a distinct dip that does not coincide with either peak [arXiv:1302.4352].

sources.summary: The paper says kiso6669 has two low-metallicity knots near the head, an off-centered rotation center, and a linewidth dip not coincident with emission peaks.

quotes.quote: kiso6669 . This galaxy has a second bright knot, not far from the head (Fig. brightness ). The two knots are of low metallicity, which contrasts with the metallicity of the rest of the galaxy (Fig. metallicity ). The center of the RC stays in between the two emission peaks of the galaxy (and so, off-centered; see Fig. velocity_curves ). The linewidth curve shows a significant dip which does not coincide with any of the two emission peaks (Fig. linewidths ).

gmw_00010923.name: kiso6877
gmw_00010923.overview: The galaxy kiso6877 is a young, low-metallicity tadpole galaxy [arXiv:1302.4352]. Its bright head lacks dark matter and can be modeled as a counter-rotating disk with a maximum velocity of 6 kilometers per second [arXiv:1302.4352].

sources.summary: The paper highlights kiso6877 as the youngest, lowest-metallicity sample tadpole, with a head possibly modeled as a counter-rotating disk and matching dynamical estimates.

quotes.quote: kiso6877 . This tadpole has the lowest metallicity and is the youngest object in the sample, and its bright head does not seem to contain dark matter. It also presents unusual high-excitation emission lines in the spectral region of the WR bumps (Sect. fluxes_and_else ). As we explain in Sect. dynmasses , we sought distortions in the RCs that could be associated with massive self-gravitating heads. Perhaps kiso6877 represents the only example.

The head is modeled with a counter-rotating disk with a maximum velocity of 6 km s ^ 1 , which according to Eq. ( mass_rot2 ) corresponds to a dynamical mass of (M/M_ ) 6.7 . The mass thus derived is similar to both the photometric mass of the head and the dynamical mass inferred from linewidth (see Table obs_summary ). We also note the coincidence of this wiggle in the RC with an obvious decrease in line-width associated with the head (see Fig. linewidths ).

gmw_00010924.name: kiso8466
gmw_00010924.overview: The galaxy kiso8466 is a massive tadpole galaxy characterized by a multi-hump structure [arXiv:1302.4352]. It features a unique two-hump linewidth curve and exhibits a relatively uniform oxygen metallicity throughout its structure [arXiv:1302.4352].

sources.summary: The paper describes kiso8466 as a massive tadpole with multi-hump structure, a two-hump linewidth curve, and uniform oxygen metallicity like kiso5149.

quotes.quote: kiso8466 . The galaxy has a multi hump structure (Fig. images and brightness ). The line-width curve presents a curious two-hump shape, which is not centered in photometric maxima (Fig. linewidths ). The oxygen metallicity is rather uniform along the galaxy, as for the other massive galaxy in the sample, kiso5149 .

gmw_00010925.name: J0205-55:S3
gmw_00010925.overview: J0205-55:S3 is a spiral galaxy that was previously classified as an S0 galaxy [arXiv:2011.01438]. It is characterized by a low neutral hydrogen mass fraction and a neutral hydrogen center that is shifted from its optical center [arXiv:2011.01438].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J0205-55:S3 as one of the lowest-H i-mass-fraction Choir galaxies, with an H i center shifted from its optical center and a revised spiral classification.

quotes.quote: The two central galaxies with the lowest \ mass fraction are J0205-55:S3 and J0209-10:S4.

The two most deficient galaxies J0205-55:S3 and J0258-74:S3, with stellar masses of 10 ^ 10.8 M _ and 10 ^ 9.5 M _ , appear to have \ centres shifted from their optical centres. J0205-55:S3 was classified as S0 galaxy Sweet2013 however, inspecting our deep DECam images we can re-classify it as a spiral galaxy.

gmw_00010926.name: J0258-74:S3
gmw_00010926.overview: J0258-74:S3 is an edge-on galaxy featuring a bright bulge [arXiv:2011.01438]. It is highly deficient in neutral hydrogen gas, with its gas center shifted from its optical center [arXiv:2011.01438].

sources.summary: The paper describes J0258-74:S3 as a highly H i-deficient edge-on galaxy whose gas is offset and overlapping J0258-74:S2, making an interaction likely.

quotes.quote: The two most deficient galaxies J0205-55:S3 and J0258-74:S3, with stellar masses of 10 ^ 10.8 M _ and 10 ^ 9.5 M _ , appear to have \ centres shifted from their optical centres. J0205-55:S3 was classified as S0 galaxy Sweet2013 however, inspecting our deep DECam images we can re-classify it as a spiral galaxy. J0258-74:S3 is edge-on galaxy with a bright bulge, which is seen on the top right panel in Figure fig:HI_deficient . J0258-74:S3 is in projection close to the larger J0258-74:S2 galaxy (to the east). We show J0258-74 group in Figure fig:j0258_group , and it is seen that the \ contours of J0258-74:S2 and J0258-74:S3 galaxy overlap. Being very close, it is likely that J0258-74:S2 and J0258-74:S3 are interacting.

gmw_00010927.name: J1026-19:S1
gmw_00010927.overview: J1026-19:S1 is the largest member galaxy of the HIPASS J1026-19 group [arXiv:2011.01438]. It is connected to its smaller companion, S2, by a tidal tail and a faint stellar stream that continues from its spiral arm [arXiv:2011.01438].

sources.summary: The paper describes J1026-19:S1 as the largest group member, connected to S2 by a tidal tail and stellar stream, with compressed and skewed H i features consistent with a fly-by.

quotes.quote: HIPASS J1026-19 contains six known member galaxies, three of which we detected in \ (S1, S2 and S5). The non-detected galaxies S3 and S4 are small dwarf galaxies, below our detection limit, while S6 is deficient. The largest member of J1026, S1, is connected to its smaller companion S2 (which is either a small edge-on disk or a strongly barred-dwarf galaxy) by a tidal tail which is visible in optical, UV and \ imaging (see Figure fig:j1026_multiw ). Tracing emission (optical and UV) from the S1 galaxy, we can see the faint stellar stream as a continuation of the spiral arm. This stellar stream broadens roughly at the midpoint between the S1 and S2 (looking at the projected distance between the S1 and S2) and the faint irregular structure resembles a tidal dwarf galaxy candidate.

We examine the \ intensity map in the J1026-19 and see that low-density contours overlap S1 and S2 galaxies (see Figure fig:j1026_group ). The \ contours in the north-west part of the J1026-19 group (S1 galaxy) are compressed with respect to those in the south-east. Moreover, the velocity map of the J1026-19 is being skewed towards the south-east at the roughly recessional velocity of the group. It is possible that a fly-by can explain such \ and stellar features (e.g. Kim2017 ).

gmw_00010928.name: J1026-19:S2
gmw_00010928.overview: J1026-19:S2 is a small companion galaxy that is classified as either a small edge-on disk or a strongly barred dwarf galaxy [arXiv:2011.01438]. It is connected to the larger galaxy S1 by a tidal tail visible in optical, ultraviolet, and neutral hydrogen imaging, likely resulting from a fly-by interaction [arXiv:2011.01438].

sources.summary: The paper presents J1026-19:S2 as a smaller companion linked to S1 by optical, UV, and H i tidal structure, likely after passing near S1 in a fly-by interaction.

quotes.quote: HIPASS J1026-19 contains six known member galaxies, three of which we detected in \ (S1, S2 and S5). The non-detected galaxies S3 and S4 are small dwarf galaxies, below our detection limit, while S6 is deficient. The largest member of J1026, S1, is connected to its smaller companion S2 (which is either a small edge-on disk or a strongly barred-dwarf galaxy) by a tidal tail which is visible in optical, UV and \ imaging (see Figure fig:j1026_multiw ). Tracing emission (optical and UV) from the S1 galaxy, we can see the faint stellar stream as a continuation of the spiral arm. This stellar stream broadens roughly at the midpoint between the S1 and S2 (looking at the projected distance between the S1 and S2) and the faint irregular structure resembles a tidal dwarf galaxy candidate.

We examine the \ intensity map in the J1026-19 and see that low-density contours overlap S1 and S2 galaxies (see Figure fig:j1026_group ). The \ contours in the north-west part of the J1026-19 group (S1 galaxy) are compressed with respect to those in the south-east. Moreover, the velocity map of the J1026-19 is being skewed towards the south-east at the roughly recessional velocity of the group. It is possible that a fly-by can explain such \ and stellar features (e.g. Kim2017 ).

gmw_00010929.name: J1059-09:S1
gmw_00010929.overview: J1059-09:S1 is a galaxy that forms a strongly interacting pair with its neighbor, S3 [arXiv:2011.01438]. The two galaxies are connected by a multiwavelength tidal tail, which exhibits offset hydrogen emission and triggered star formation [arXiv:2011.01438].

sources.summary: The paper describes J1059-09:S1 as part of a strongly interacting pair with S3, joined by a multiwavelength tidal tail and offset H i emission linked to triggered star formation.

quotes.quote: J1059-19 contains a strongly interacting galaxy pair S1 and S3 (MCG-01-28-013 and MCG-01-28-013, see Figure fig:j1059_multiw ) which are connected by a tidal tail. The tidal tail between S1 and S3 galaxy is visible in multi-wavelength observations (optical, H , UV, IR and ). We note that the peak of \ emission is offset from the stellar centres of both galaxies, both in the direction of interaction. The \ peak of the \ emission that corresponds to the S3 galaxy is in the tidal tail between itself and S1 galaxy (see Figure fig:j1059_multiw ). The obvious cause of the \ offsets is galaxy interaction, which is most likely triggering the star formation in the tidal tail region (e.g. Chromey1998 ).

gmw_00010930.name: J1059-09:S3
gmw_00010930.overview: J1059-09:S3 is a galaxy that forms a strongly interacting pair with the galaxy S1 [arXiv:2011.01438]. The two galaxies are connected by a tidal tail, which contains the peak of the system's neutral hydrogen emission and hosts triggered star formation [arXiv:2011.01438].

sources.summary: The paper describes J1059-09:S3 as the second member of a strongly interacting pair, with its H i peak lying in the tidal tail between itself and S1.

quotes.quote: J1059-19 contains a strongly interacting galaxy pair S1 and S3 (MCG-01-28-013 and MCG-01-28-013, see Figure fig:j1059_multiw ) which are connected by a tidal tail. The tidal tail between S1 and S3 galaxy is visible in multi-wavelength observations (optical, H , UV, IR and ). We note that the peak of \ emission is offset from the stellar centres of both galaxies, both in the direction of interaction. The \ peak of the \ emission that corresponds to the S3 galaxy is in the tidal tail between itself and S1 galaxy (see Figure fig:j1059_multiw ). The obvious cause of the \ offsets is galaxy interaction, which is most likely triggering the star formation in the tidal tail region (e.g. Chromey1998 ).

gmw_00010931.name: J1250-20:S4
gmw_00010931.overview: J1250-20:S4 is a small, compact dwarf galaxy that is deficient in neutral hydrogen [arXiv:2011.01438]. This deficiency is likely caused by tidal interactions with its larger companion galaxy, S2 [arXiv:2011.01438].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J1250-20:S4 as H i deficient, likely through tidal interaction with S2, and treats S2 as the source of overlapping H i because S4 is smaller and less active.

quotes.quote: HIPASS J1250-20 has seven known members, five of these are spectroscopically confirmed, others are thought to be the group members, as stated in Sweet2013, Dzudzar2019 . We detect the \ emission which we associated with S1 and S2 galaxies. The non-detected galaxies are small compact dwarfs and we show that one galaxy, S4, is an \ deficient galaxy. The likely reason for deficiency in S4 is the tidal interaction with S2, see Figure fig:j1250_multiw . The two most luminous galaxies in J1250-20, S1 and S2 are interacting, see Figure fig:j1250_multiw . These galaxies have a comparable stellar masses however, they have vastly different \ mass.

The \ contours that we associate with S2 galaxies overlap also on top of its smaller companion with which its interacting, S4. In this work we associate \ emission to S2, as S4 is physically smaller, has stellar mass 0.9 dex smaller, and has a lower specific star-formation. The \ mass extracted from the region between the S1 and S2 galaxies is 10 ^ 8.9 M _ . This is a lower limit of the \ mass between galaxies as there is uncertainty where to place galaxy `borders'. The \ velocity of the extracted region corresponds to the recessional group velocity (in velocity space between S1 and S2), and in this region we do not detect faint stellar streams.

gmw_00010932.name: host galaxy of AT 2023lli
gmw_00010932.overview: The host galaxy of AT 2023lli is a green-valley galaxy with a stellar mass of approximately 10^10 solar masses [arXiv:2403.01686]. Its spectral energy distribution is well fitted solely by stellar components, indicating a low star formation rate and zero contribution from an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2403.01686].

sources.summary: The paper models the host galaxy of AT 2023lli with multiband SED fitting, finding stellar-only emission, stellar mass near 10^10 solar masses, low SFR, and green-valley color.

quotes.quote: We collected multiband photometry for the host galaxy of AT 2023lli from several archives, including Swift/UVOT, PanSTARRS, the Two Micron All Sky Survey, and Wide field Infrared Survey Explorer. We used the Python package Code Investigating GALaxy Emission (CIGALE; CIGALE ) to model the spectral energy distribution (SED) of the host galaxy. CIGALE can fit the SED of a galaxy from far-UV to radio and estimate its physical properties through the analysis of likelihood distribution.

The fitting result is shown in Figure host , which is well fitted solely by stellar components with zero contribution from the AGN component. Meanwhile, the host galaxy properties were estimated by the CIGALE SED fitting. The stellar mass of the galaxy is 10^ 10.00 0.21 M_ and the star formation rate (SFR) is logSFR = -1.67 0.80 . Using the empirical relation between \ and the total stellar mass of the galaxy in the local universe (see Equation (4) in Reines2015 ), the central \ of AT 2023lli is estimated to be 10^ 6.40 0.47 M_ . We derived the value of u- of 2.08, which also falls within the ``green valley'', which dominates the host galaxies of TDEs Hammerstein2021 .

gmw_00010933.name: NGC 3686
gmw_00010933.overview: NGC 3686 is classified as an Sbc-type spiral galaxy [arXiv:1811.08923]. Its dust component exhibits a Gaussian-like profile, whereas its old stellar population follows an exponential distribution [arXiv:1811.08923].

sources.summary: NGC 3686 is used as a typical DustPedia Sbc example; its Herschel profiles look similar, and its dust component has a Gaussian-like index unlike the old stellar profile.

quotes.quote: In Fig. NGC3686_decomp we show an example of a galfit fitting in the WISE W1 band for one of the typical DustPedia galaxies, the Sbc-type galaxy NGC 3686. An example of the azimuthally averaged profile with the overimposed model in the SPIRE 250 m band (both created using the IRAF's ellipse task http://iraf.noao.edu/ ) is shown in Fig. NGC3686_decomp_GM_azim . The profiles in all Herschel bands look similar and, therefore, we only show here (and to the end of the paper) the profiles for the SPIRE 250 m band. We note that for this galaxy the \ index of the dust component n^ 250 =0.52 (i.e. it has a Gaussian profile), in comparison with n^ 3.4 =1.15 (an exponential profile) for the SB distribution of the old stellar population.

gmw_00010934.name: UGC 5936
gmw_00010934.overview: UGC 5936 is a luminous lenticular galaxy featuring a large outer ring [arXiv:2005.09383]. This ring is ionized by young stars and undergoes star formation fueled by the smooth accretion of cold gas from a nearby dwarf satellite [arXiv:2005.09383].

sources.summary: UGC 5936 is a luminous ringed S0 whose young-star-ionized, solar-metallicity outer ring forms stars from smoothly accreted satellite gas and follows the Kennicutt-Schmidt relation.

quotes.quote: The luminous S0 galaxy with a large ring, UGC 5936, is studied here. By applying long-slit spectroscopy along the major axis of UGC 5936, we have measured gas and star kinematics, Lick indices for the main body of the galaxy, and strong emission-line flux ratios in the ring. After inspecting the gas excitation in the ring using line ratios diagnostic diagrams and having ensured that it is ionised mostly by young stars, we have determined the gas oxygen abundance by using popular strong-line methods. Also we have proved the spatial proximity of the south-eastern dwarf satellite to UGC 5936 and have measured its gas metallicity.

The ionised gas of the ring is excited by young stars and has solar metallicity. Star formation in the ring is rather prolonged, and its intensity corresponds to the current HI content of UGC 5936 (to the Kennicutt-Schmidt relation). The whole morphology of the HI distribution implies current accretion of the cold gas from the satellite onto the outer disc of UGC 5936; due to the satellite location and rotation in the plane of the stellar disc of the host galaxy, the accretion is smooth and laminar providing the favourable condition for star formation ignition.

gmw_00010935.name: SDSS J105009.10 + 362009.9
gmw_00010935.overview: SDSS J105009.10 + 362009.9 is a blue dwarf satellite galaxy located near UGC 5936 [arXiv:2005.09383]. Its spectrum is dominated by H-alpha emission, indicating that the ionized gas is excited by young stars [arXiv:2005.09383].

sources.summary: SDSS J105009.10 + 362009.9 is a blue dwarf satellite near UGC 5936; spectroscopy shows young-star ionized gas and oxygen abundance above the expected mass-metallicity value.

quotes.quote: To the south-east from UGC 5936 a small satellite, SDSS J105009.10 + 362009.9, is observed in 15 kpc projected onto the sky plane (and in 19 kpc if we suggest that it lies in the plane of the UGC 5936 disc); the satellite is very blue. According to SDSS/DR9, the magnitude difference between UGC 5936 and its satellite is 5.64^m in the g band and 6.2^m in the band, so it is a bona-fide dwarf, with M_B 15^m and the stellar mass of about 10^8 \ if we apply the mass-to-light ratio corresponding to its colour g-=0.27 bell03 .

We have also plotted in Fig. bpt the measurements of the ionised-gas emission-line intensity ratios for the dwarf south-eastern satellite which has been probed by our long-slit cross-section in PA=135^ . Here the H emission line dominates in the spectrum, with the equivalent width of 38 , and the gas is unanimously excited by young stars. By taking into account this fact, we have determined the gas oxygen abundance by using the strong-line method. By exploring both O3N2 and N2 calibrations from pp04 and marino13 , we have obtained for the satellite 12+ (O/H) =8.32 0.05 dex.

gmw_00010936.name: B1
gmw_00010936.overview: B1 is a near-infrared and radio-detected galaxy that is undergoing a merger at its cluster center [arXiv:2102.11752]. Along with neighboring galaxies, it is expected to contribute to the formation of a future brightest cluster galaxy [arXiv:2102.11752].

sources.summary: B1 is a NIR/radio member of the assembling future BCG, with faint CO and 870 m detections, merger dynamics, and suppressed star formation.

quotes.quote: The assembling BCG: The two galaxies B1 and H5, detected in NIR and radio continuum, with a third (A1) detected in sub-mm and radio continuum, are expected to contribute to the formation of the future BCG of . coogan18 present a faint detection of the CO [ 4-3 ] line for B1 (3.7 ) and a 870 m continuum flux of 346 69 Jy whereas H5 has only been marginally detected ( 2.5 ) at 870 m. Conversely for A1, the CO [ 4-3 ] line has been detected at 11.9 and its 870 m continuum flux has been found to be 1370 140 Jy. Regarding the dynamics, both coogan18 and strazzullo18 conclude that B1, H5 and A1 along with H4; strazzullo18 are undergoing a merger at the cluster center. Moreover, B1 and H5 (along with H4) appear to be likely suppressed/quiescent galaxies lower main-sequence galaxies; sargent14 while A1 has a high SFR of 177.5 19 M _ yr ^ 1 , above what is expected from a main sequence galaxy Fig. 8 in coogan18 .

sources.summary: H5 is a NIR/radio member of the assembling future BCG, marginally detected at 870 m, dynamically merging, and likely suppressed or quiescent.

quotes.quote: The assembling BCG: The two galaxies B1 and H5, detected in NIR and radio continuum, with a third (A1) detected in sub-mm and radio continuum, are expected to contribute to the formation of the future BCG of . coogan18 present a faint detection of the CO [ 4-3 ] line for B1 (3.7 ) and a 870 m continuum flux of 346 69 Jy whereas H5 has only been marginally detected ( 2.5 ) at 870 m. Conversely for A1, the CO [ 4-3 ] line has been detected at 11.9 and its 870 m continuum flux has been found to be 1370 140 Jy. Regarding the dynamics, both coogan18 and strazzullo18 conclude that B1, H5 and A1 along with H4; strazzullo18 are undergoing a merger at the cluster center. Moreover, B1 and H5 (along with H4) appear to be likely suppressed/quiescent galaxies lower main-sequence galaxies; sargent14 while A1 has a high SFR of 177.5 19 M _ yr ^ 1 , above what is expected from a main sequence galaxy Fig. 8 in coogan18 .

gmw_00010937.name: S7
gmw_00010937.overview: S7 is an X-ray active galactic nucleus that forms a close merging pair with the neighboring galaxy N7 [arXiv:2102.11752]. This system exhibits very low star formation, which may be suppressed by the active nucleus, and features bent radio-jet hotspots [arXiv:2102.11752].

sources.summary: S7 is an X-ray AGN in a close merging pair with N7, has low star formation likely suppressed by AGN activity, and may power bent radio-jet hotspots.

quotes.quote: Merging galaxies with X-ray AGN: One of the two X-ray detected AGNs, S7, originally reported by brusa05 and confirmed by campisi09 , is also part of a merging pair with galaxy N7 with a velocity separation of 1380 at a projected separation < 0.5 ^ or 4 kpc; coogan18 . Although the N7-S7 system has a combined 870 m flux measurement of 217 79 Jy, it can mainly be associated with N7. This leaves S7 with a very low SFR ( 8 M _ yr ^ 1 , based on a negligible CO [ 4-3 ] detection), which could be a result of its AGN activity with an L _ x =10^ 43.7 ergs s ^ 1 ; campisi09 suppressing star formation barger15 .

Moving slightly to the south-east, we have the R7 group surrounding N7 and S7, which is a merging pair with the latter being an X-ray detected AGN campisi09 . Hence it is highly likely that they are hotspots from the jets emerging from S7 with a level of bending that is common for AGNs interacting with the dense medium of cluster cores.

gmw_00010938.name: Galaxy 13
gmw_00010938.overview: Galaxy 13 is an isolated active galactic nucleus detected in X-rays [arXiv:2102.11752]. It is classified among star-formation suppressed galaxies and exhibits a high 3 GHz radio flux that may indicate undetected jet-driven kinetic feedback [arXiv:2102.11752].

sources.summary: Galaxy 13 is an isolated X-ray AGN with appreciable 870 m and 3 GHz flux; excess radio emission may indicate undetected jet-driven kinetic feedback.

quotes.quote: Isolated X-ray AGN The second X-ray detected cluster AGN: 13, like S7, was also reported and confirmed by brusa05 and campisi09 . However unlike S7, it is isolated and features an appreciable 870 m flux of 248 69 Jy, placing it comfortably among the lower main-sequence star formation suppressed galaxies sargent14 . Furthermore, there is a vast Lyman- halo 100 kpc, valentino16 in the core of , possibly being powered by outflows from the two cluster AGNs along with the SFR in the cluster core with an estimated net energy outflow rate, E_ kin 5 10^ 44 ergs s ^ 1 ; valentino16 .

During the analysis of the jet driven feedback in , we have ignored galaxy 13 due to a lack of evident jet-like emission. However, it does showcase a high 3 GHz radio flux ( 6.6 1.3 Jy) only two-thirds of which can be accounted for by the intrinsic SFR. The rest may still suggest a level of kinetic feedback present in this galaxy with the jets having not been detected due to projection effects.

gmw_00010939.name: SPRC047
gmw_00010939.overview: SPRC047 is an edge-on galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.031 [arXiv:2312.05358]. It is surrounded by a ring-like tidal stellar stream that forms an off-centered ellipse with a faint branching loop on its northern side [arXiv:2312.05358].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes SPRC047 and its ring-like tidal stream with visual and IR photometry, deconvolving central-galaxy contamination and finding model-dependent stellar-population estimates plus an off-centered loop morphology.

quotes.quote: We have investigated the properties (e.g., age, metallicity) of the stellar populations of a ring-like tidal stellar stream (or streams) around the edge-on galaxy SPRC047 ( z = 0.031 ) using spectral energy distribution (SED) fits to integrated broad-band aperture flux densities. We used visual images in six different bands and Spitzer/IRAC 3.6 m data. We have attempted to derive best-fit stellar population parameters (metallicity, age) in three non-contiguous segments of the stream. Due to the very low surface brightness of the stream, we have performed a deconvolution with a Richardson--Lucy type algorithm of the low spatial resolution 3.6 m IRAC image, thereby reducing the effect of the point-spread-function (PSF) aliased ``emission'' from the bright edge-on central galaxy at the locations of our three stream segments. Our SED fits that used several different star formation history priors, from an exponentially decaying star formation burst to continuous star formation, indicate that the age--metallicity--dust degeneracy is not resolved, most likely because of inadequate wavelength coverage and low signal-to-noise ratios of the low surface brightness features.

As shown in Figure figopt , the ring-like streams form an ellipse that has a projected major axis that is almost perfectly aligned in the north--south orientation. However, the brightest parts of the ellipse look off-centered with respect to the central edge-on galaxy, having a larger fraction of the ellipse on the northern side of the edge-on galaxy than on the southern side. In addition, there appears to be a faint branching of the ellipse on the northern side, where a ``loop'' closes the ellipse in such a way that approximately equally large parts of the ellipse are seen on both the northern and southern sides.

gmw_00010940.name: SDSS J133751.29+354755.7
gmw_00010940.overview: SDSS J133751.29+354755.7 is a post-starburst galaxy classified as a LINER [arXiv:2012.01426]. It has experienced a factor-of-twenty decrease in star formation over the last 300 Myr, leaving a mass-weighted age of 1.11 Gyr and a dust attenuation of A_V = 0.74 [arXiv:2012.01426].

sources.summary: The paper uses SDSS J133751.29+354755.7 as a post-starburst Prospector test case, inferring a recent starburst decline, young mass-weighted age, dust attenuation, and LINER-like emission.

quotes.quote: We fit SDSS J133751.29+354755.7 from the post-starburst catalog of goto07 , specifically targeted for its high signal-to-noise spectrum and substantive emission line infilling. We fit the ugriz photometry and optical spectrum smee13 from SDSS Data Release 16 ahumada20 . A noise floor of 5 is enforced on the photometry consistent with uncertainties in the underlying stellar models. A 19-parameter physical model is used. The total mass, stellar metallicity, and velocity dispersion are set free.

As expected, a significant post-starburst event is detected within the previous 300 Myr, suggesting a factor-of-twenty decrease in the ongoing rate of star formation. The mass-weighted age is 1.11 Gyr, indicating a relatively short formation phase for this galaxy, and the dust attenuation is significant at A _V = 0.74 . This is consistent with SDSS J133751.29+354755.7 being a LINER; in such objects, the nebular emission lines are thought to be partially or fully powered by evolved stars, AGN, or shocks (e.g., kewley06 ).

gmw_00010941.name: Ark 18
gmw_00010941.overview: Ark 18 is an isolated, gas-rich low-surface-brightness disc galaxy located in the Eridanus void [arXiv:2103.00211]. Its complex structure features an extended blue disc, a warped velocity field, and a bright central region hosting an off-centered star-forming clump [arXiv:2103.00211].

sources.summary: The paper studies Ark 18 as an isolated low-surface-brightness galaxy with a complex disc, dark-matter-dominated gas-rich structure, warped kinematics, and likely dwarf--dwarf merger origin.

quotes.quote: The low-mass low-surface brightness (LSB) disc galaxy Arakelian 18 (Ark 18) resides in the Eridanus void and because of its isolation represents an ideal case to study the formation and evolution mechanisms of such a galaxy type. Its complex structure consists of an extended blue LSB disc and a bright central elliptically-shaped part hosting a massive off-centered star-forming clump. We present the in-depth study of Ark 18 based on observations with the SCORPIO-2 long-slit spectrograph and a scanning Fabry-Perot interferometer at the Russian 6-m telescope complemented by archival multi-wavelength images and SDSS spectra. Ark 18 appears to be a dark matter dominated gas-rich galaxy without a radial metallicity gradient. The observed velocity field of the ionised gas is well described by two circularly rotating components moderately inclined with respect to each other and a possible warp in the outer disc.

We conclude that the LSB disc is likely the result of a dwarf--dwarf merger with a stellar mass ratio of the components at least 5:1 that occurred earlier than 300 Myr ago. The brightest star forming clump was likely formed later by accretion of a gas cloud.

gmw_00010942.name: J1737 15
gmw_00010942.overview: J1737 15 is an extended double-sided Fanaroff-Riley II radio galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0301487]. The galaxy features a nuclear component and two jets that end in large radio lobes [arXiv:astro-ph/0301487].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J1737 15 as an extended double-sided FR II radio galaxy with a nuclear component, two jets, radio lobes, measured radio fluxes, and a host optical galaxy.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: estimated J2000.0 ICRS position of =17^ h 37^ m 12.9^ s 0.3^ s , =-15 11 02 15

quotes.quote: Source no. 10, instead, is really extended. We shall name this source hereafter as J1737 15 , according to its radio coordinates. Its morphology at 1.4 GHz (Fig. fig2 , upper panel) is typical of double-sided FR II radio galaxies. There is a nuclear component as well as two jets ending in large radio lobes.

The angular dimensions of J1737 15 are 0.04 0.11 , with an integrated flux of 55.6 1.5 mJy at 1.4 GHz. The flux density of the northern component is S_ north =21.6 0.5 mJy, whereas the southern one is S_ south =30.7 1.3 mJy. The central component has a flux density of S_ core =3.80 0.05 mJy, and an estimated J2000.0 ICRS position of =17^ h 37^ m 12.9^ s 0.3^ s , =-15 11 02 15 .

gmw_00010943.name: S1
gmw_00010943.overview: S1 is a marginally extended galaxy identified as the host of the gamma-ray burst GRB 210726A [arXiv:2308.10936]. Stellar population modeling of its photometry indicates a median redshift of z = 2.38, with a likely redshift of z > 1.2 [arXiv:2308.10936].

sources.summary: The paper identifies S1 as the marginally extended host galaxy of GRB 210726A and models its photometry, deriving a median redshift of z=2.38 with a likely high-redshift solution.

quotes.quote: To assess if the faint source within the XRT region (S1) is afterglow or a coincident galaxy, we obtained a deep band template image of the field with GMOS on Gemini-South on 2023 February 17 (Program GS-2023A-FT-101, PI: Schroeder). S1 is still clearly detected ( = 25.06 0.14 mag) in the late-time image and is marginally extended. We align the image taken at t 3.16 days and the template using standard tasks in IRAF and perform image subtraction between the epochs using HOTPANTS becker15 . No residual is detected within the XRT region to a limiting magnitude of > 24.5 mag (derived from sources in the field using IRAF/phot ), indicating that our observation at t 3.16 days is minimally contaminated by any optical afterglow. Given the spatial coincidence of S1 with the X-ray (Sections sec:Swift_Obs and sec:Chandra_Obs ) and radio (Section sec:VLA ) afterglows, we identify S1 as the host galaxy.

Host Galaxy Modeling We use the observed photometric upper limit and detections (Table tab:hostphot ), in combination with the stellar population modeling code Prospector Leja_2017 , to attempt to constrain the redshift of the host galaxy. Prospector applies a nested sampling fitting routine speagle2020 to the observed data to create posterior distributions of the stellar population properties of interest, as well as generate model SEDs Conroy2009, Conroy2010 . We employ a Chabrier initial mass function (IMF) Chabrier2003 , a parametric delayed-tau star formation history (SFH), the Milky Way dust extinction law Cardelli1989 , and allow redshift to be a sampled parameter ranging 0.1 < z < 3.0 . See for further details on the stellar population modeling. We find that the median Prospector derived redshift is z = 2.38^ +0.39 _ 0.75 .

Given that the observed optical photometry and Keck/LRIS spectrum are featureless and show no indication of a 4000 \ break, we infer that the break must occur after the z band detection, making the redshift of this host likely at z 1.2 .

gmw_00010944.name: galaxy G6
gmw_00010944.overview: Galaxy G6 is a faint optical and infrared galaxy that may be the host of a large-scale bipolar radio jet [arXiv:1607.05080]. It has a modest velocity dispersion and a relatively small central black hole, which suggests it may have lost its outer halo stars through multiple tidal encounters [arXiv:1607.05080].

sources.summary: The paper treats galaxy G6 as a faint possible host of the bipolar jet, with a modest velocity dispersion, small black-hole mass, and possible tidal stripping.

quotes.quote: The two GMRT 610 MHz radio peaks are centered near optical galaxies G5 and G2, while the third radio peak on the jet axis is close to the faint galaxy G6. Figure gmrt610 (left panel) shows the positions of G2, G5, G6 and the brightest galaxy G1 with `+' signs. suggested that the probable host AGN emitting the bipolar jet could be galaxy G6 rather than G3, which is clearly offset southward from the principal jet direction.

The galaxy G6 is very faint both in optical and infrared light (Table tab1 and tabk ). From spectroscopy, we obtained a velocity dispersion of (143 40) Km s ^ 1 for G6, which yielded a relatively small black hole mass of (0.52 0.65) 10^8 M_ (see section 4.7). However, the error margin is high due to the low SNR of the spectrum. Even though it is improbable (but not impossible) for a faint galaxy like G6 to produce such a large scale radio jet, it is possible that this now faint galaxy has been stripped off the majority of its outer halo stars in multiple tidal encounters, while still retaining a dense stellar core and black hole at the centre.

gmw_00010945.name: IGR J12319 0749
gmw_00010945.overview: IGR J12319 0749 is a high-redshift blazar with a compact radio counterpart [arXiv:1303.3136]. High-resolution radio imaging reveals a Doppler-boosted jet inclined within 14 degrees to the line of sight [arXiv:1303.3136].

sources.summary: The paper identifies IGR J12319 0749 as a high-redshift blazar, reports EVN imaging of its compact radio counterpart, and infers a Doppler-boosted jet from its brightness temperature.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: The right ascension 12^ h 31^ m 57 68547 and the declination 7 47 18 0901 have accuracies of 0.7 mas and 1 mas, respectively

quotes.quote: Here we report on our high-resolution radio interferometric observation of the radio counterpart of IGR J12319 0749, using the technique of very long baseline interferometry (VLBI) with the European VLBI Network (EVN). This is an excellent tool for confirming with imaging that a source is indeed a blazar with compact radio emission on milli-arcsecond (mas) angular scale; VLBI is also capable of determining the accurate astrometric position of a compact radio-emitting object. The EVN observation of IGR J12319 0749 (or J1231 0747, the name derived from the more accurate coordinates of the radio counterpart, and used in this paper hereafter) took place on 2012 June 19 at 5 GHz frequency.

We found that J1231 0747 is a 85-mJy radio source with sub-mas angular size. The slightly inverted radio spectrum or the flux density variability implied by our measurement at 5 GHz are expected from a blazar. With the usual assumption of energy equipartition between the magnetic field and the relativistic particles in the jet Read94 , we estimated the lower limit to the brightness temperature of J1231 0747. It indicates a Doppler-boosted radio jet inclined within 14 to the line of sight.

gmw_00010946.name: source 106b
gmw_00010946.overview: Source 106b is a lensed galaxy at a redshift of z=4.116 [arXiv:2009.08458]. Strong gravitational lensing resolves the galaxy into at least six star-forming regions of 100-200 parsec size, which are accompanied by a spatially offset Lyman-alpha nebula [arXiv:2009.08458].

sources.summary: The paper reports source 106b as a z=4.116 lensed galaxy with a continuum-break redshift, spatially offset Ly-alpha nebula, and multiple star-forming subcomponents.

quotes.quote: One example is source 106b at z=4.116 (see Figure sys106 ), for which the continuum-break is clearly detected, its redshift is measured by cross-correlating the spectrum with high-z templates Redshifts have been measured using the Eazy package within the Pandora environment garilli10 . , and found consistent with the spatially offset \ nebula. Interestingly, the spectroscopic redshift is also in very good agreement with the photometric redshift derived from ASTRODEEP, z_ phot =4.20 castellano16 . As discussed in the Sect. clumps , source 106b is also a good example of how a galaxy can be resolved into several sub-components by strong lensing (at least six star-forming regions of 100-200 parsec size).

gmw_00010947.name: Galaxy A
aliases_external: Galaxy A (apparent nucleus projected on SDSS J174049.60+661229.1)
gmw_00010947.overview: Galaxy A is a star-forming galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.30 [arXiv:2401.17423]. Although it visually appears to be the nucleus of the spiral galaxy Galaxy C, it is actually a separate background galaxy projected through Galaxy C's disk [arXiv:2401.17423].

sources.summary: The paper first treats Galaxy A as an apparent nucleus of Galaxy C, then uses spectra to show it is a separate z=0.30 star-forming galaxy projected through Galaxy C.

quotes.quote: These were, by eye, the obvious host galaxy candidates, as Galaxy A appeared to be the nucleus of Galaxy C. We positioned the slit on Galaxy A on MJD = 60068, 60077 and 60084, and on Galaxy B on MJD = 60117 and 60133. We then stacked the host galaxy spectra for total exposure times of 2700 s for Galaxy A and 4200 s for Galaxy B. The resulting combined spectra are shown in Fig. fig:hostspec .

Instead, in both galaxies we do see clear emission lines of [ O ii ] 3727, [ O iii ] 4959,5007, H and H , typical to star-forming SN host galaxies -- but lines in both objects are at a redshift of z=0.302 0.001 . The GTC/OSIRIS 2D spectrum from MJD = 60184.0 includes z=0.30 emission lines from Galaxy A in addition to the lines from Galaxy C (while the spectrum at MJD = 60208.9 only includes z=0.025 lines from Galaxy C, owing to a different slit position angle), and of the two sets of lines, the ones at z=0.30 are considerably brighter. We thus infer that Galaxy A, which appears to be the nucleus of Galaxy C, is, in fact, a separate galaxy shining through the disk of Galaxy C, most likely part of the same cluster as Galaxy B at z=0.30 .

sources.summary: The paper identifies Galaxy C as the spiral galaxy near SN 2023aew and concludes it is the likely host, with z=0.025 lines and negligible host extinction.

quotes.quote: This figure shows the location of the SN, seemingly in the outskirts of a spiral galaxy (SDSS J174049.60+661229.1) and close to a smaller galaxy (SDSS J174050.55+661220.7). These host galaxy candidates are examined in Sect. sec:host . We refer to the apparent nucleus of the spiral galaxy as Galaxy A, the smaller galaxy as Galaxy B and the spiral galaxy itself as Galaxy C.

We also see lines at a similar redshift in our GTC/OSIRIS 2D spectra at the position of the large spiral galaxy close to the SN (Galaxy C in Fig. fig:fieldRGB ). We conclude that this is the likely host galaxy of SN 2023aew. Note that this does not include any host-galaxy extinction, only that of the Milky Way, but the location of the SN at the outskirts of the host galaxy and a lack of observed narrow Na i D absorption argue against a high extinction. Therefore we assume a negligible host galaxy extinction in the rest of this paper.

gmw_00010948.name: Galaxy B
gmw_00010948.overview: Galaxy B is a small galaxy candidate located at a redshift of z=0.302 [arXiv:2401.17423]. Its spectrum exhibits clear emission lines that are typical of a star-forming galaxy [arXiv:2401.17423].

sources.summary: The paper describes Galaxy B as the smaller nearby galaxy candidate; spectra show star-forming emission lines at z=0.302, likely placing it in the same cluster as Galaxy A.

quotes.quote: This figure shows the location of the SN, seemingly in the outskirts of a spiral galaxy (SDSS J174049.60+661229.1) and close to a smaller galaxy (SDSS J174050.55+661220.7). These host galaxy candidates are examined in Sect. sec:host . We refer to the apparent nucleus of the spiral galaxy as Galaxy A, the smaller galaxy as Galaxy B and the spiral galaxy itself as Galaxy C.

We positioned the slit on Galaxy A on MJD = 60068, 60077 and 60084, and on Galaxy B on MJD = 60117 and 60133. We then stacked the host galaxy spectra for total exposure times of 2700 s for Galaxy A and 4200 s for Galaxy B. Instead, in both galaxies we do see clear emission lines of [ O ii ] 3727, [ O iii ] 4959,5007, H and H , typical to star-forming SN host galaxies -- but lines in both objects are at a redshift of z=0.302 0.001 .
